Yount narrated his memoirs to the Reverend Orange between 1854 and 1855.

gth.name: Hon. George C. Thaxter
gth.description: George Clinton Thaxter (1842-1924): an apothecary who settled in Redlands, California. Originally from Maine, he served in the Eleventh Maine Volunteer Infantry during the Civil War before moving west. He was renowned for his marksmanship.

gda.name: George Davidson
gda.description: George Davidson (1825-1911): an English geographer and engineer. Davidson was one of the most distinguished members of the United States Coast and Geodetic Survey and is remembered for his contributions to the mapping of the US Pacific coast.

ggibbs.name: George Gibbs
ggibbs.description: George Gibbs: an ethnologist known for his study of Northwest Native peoples.

gmac.name: George Grant MacCurdy
gmac.description: George Grant MacCurdy (1863-1947): anthropologist (study at universities in Vienna, Paris, and Berlin before completing a PhD at Yale). His notable career included three decades with the Peabody Museum at Yale, work with the American Museum of Natural History, various positions with the American Anthropological Association, and work with the National Research Council. MacCurdy’s published works include Recent Discoveries Bearing on the Antiquity of Man in Europe (1910), Human Origins (1924), and The Coming of Man (1932).

hearst.name: George Hearst
hearst.description: George Hearst (1820-91): a United States Senator from California, serving from 1887 to 1881. Hearst acquired a fortune from success in the mining industry, and acquired the Piedra Blanca, Santa Rosa, and San Simeon Ranchos, as well as the San Francisco Examiner, in his lifetime. Hearst eventually gave the rights to the Examiner to his son and future media mogul, William Randolph Hearst.

ghew.name: George Hewitt
ghew.description: George Hewitt: seaman. He arrived in California in 1839, shortly before the trip described here.

george1.name: George I
george1.description: George I (1660-1727): king of Great Britain and Ireland from 1714-27 and the first in the line of the House of Hanover.

gis.name: George Islip
gis.description: George Islip: a Canadian who settled in modern-day Orchard Camp (near Los Angeles, California) in 1880.  Mount Islip (pronounced eye-slip) is named after him.  He also settled north of the Stanislaus River in the 1850s.

gni.name: George Nidever
gni.description: George Nidever (1802-83): an American hunter and rancher credited with bringing the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island to Santa Barbara in 1853. The Lone Woman remained in his household until her death seven weeks after her removal to the mainland. Nidever was born in Tennessee in 1802 and moved steadily west, arriving in California in 1833. He became a Mexican citizen and married a Californio before the Mexican-American War resulted in the United States’ annexation of California. During the twentieth century, Nidever was understood to be a California pioneer.

gnis.name: George Nidever, Sr.
gnis.description: George Nidever, Sr.: father of George Nidever, the man closely associated with the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island. According to George Nidever's 1878 memoir, his father, George Sr., was originally from Pennsylvania, moved to Tennessee when he was about 20 years old, married Christina Punkhouser (Nidever's mother), and fathered eight children. Nidever was a farmer and member of volunteer military forces in the "early Indian wars."

gha.name: George P. Hammond
gha.description: George P. Hammond (1896-1993): a scholar, administrator, and historian (PhD, University of California, Berkeley) who taught at the Universities of North Dakota, Arizona, Southern California, and New Mexico. His most enduring position was as director of the Bancroft Library at the University of California, Berkeley; under his supervision, the library was modernized and expanded. Hammond's published work includes New Spain and the Anglo-American West (1932) and The Story of New Mexico: Its History and Government (1936).

gsuck.name: George Suckley
gsuck.description: George Suckley (1830-69): a physician and naturalist. Suckley attended the College of Physicians and Surgeons (now part of Columbia University) and served as a surgeon in the Union Army during the Civil War. During his time with the Pacific Railroad Survey, he collected numerous specimens and later published accounts of those surveys.

gva.name: George Vancouver
gva.description: George Vancouver (1757-1898): explorer and officer in the British Royal Navy. George Vancouver explored and mapped the Pacific Northwest from 1791-95, including the coasts of contemporary Oregon, Washington, British Columbia, and Alaska. The city of Vancouver, among many other spots, is named after him. The expedition stemmed from Great Britain’s interest in the maritime fur trade and continued desire to find a Northwest Passage connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans. Early in his career, Vancouver accompanied James Cook on his voyages to the Hawaiian Islands.

gnu.name: George Washington Morrison "Commodore" Nutt
gnu.description: George Washington Morrison "Commodore" Nutt (1848-81): a circus performer and entertainer who was born with dwarfism. He toured with P. T. Barnum.

pop.name: Pius IX the "Blessed", Giovanni Maria Mastai-Ferretti
pop.description: Giovanni Maria Mastai-Ferretti (1792-1878): the two hundred fifty-fifth Catholic pope, Pius IX; he was later canonized as a Saint. Pius IX convoked the Vatican Council during the fourth session (July 18, 1870), in which papal infallibility was established. He wrote extensively against what he considered to be the false liberalism of the early-mid nineteenth century.

gol.name: Goliah
gol.description: Goliah: a pen name. The Goliah was a tugboat that operated as a mail steamer during the mid nineteenth century, traveling along the California coast. It is possible that the ship’s captain, Salisbury Haley (who was also the first surveyor of the city of Santa Barbara, selected for the job by a committee that included Isaac J. Sparks), is the author of the article, although anyone could have adopted the boat's name as his or her nom de plume.

sancatgov.name: Governor
sancatgov.description: Governor: the identity of this man is unknown. It is possible (though improbable) that Schumacher refers to James Lick (1796-1876), who owned Santa Catalina Island during this time. More likely, he refers to an overseer or agent of Lick's.

abr.name: Grace Lincoln Hall Brosseau
abr.description: Grace Lincoln Hall Brosseau (1872-1959): President General of the Daughters of the American Revolution (DAR) from 1926-29 and a leader of the American Coalition of Patriotic Societies beginning in 1930. In both roles, Brosseau raised alarms about "radical" and "subversive" activity in the United States, and she lectured on the need for military preparedness. The DAR received substantial, controversial publicity during this time when a society member who was later expelled made public a list of left-leaning speakers and organizations that the DAR considered "doubtful."  Brosseau defended the DAR's maintenance of the list.  Brosseau, who was born in Illinois, was the wife of a wealthy Michigan businessman (president of Mack Trucks, Inc.).  Together, the couple gave what at the time was one of the largest gifts ever made to the University of Michigan, funds that supported student scholarships.

gga.name: Greta Garbo
gga.description: Greta Garbo (1950-90): a film actress. Her appearances include Grand Hotel (1932) and Camille (1936).

gcar.name: Guillermo Anastacio Eusebio Carrillo
gcar.description: Guillermo Anastacio Eusebio Carrillo: the son of Anastacio José Carillo y Lugo and much younger brother of José Raymundo Tiburcio Carrillo (commander of Santa Barbara Presidio and later mayor of Santa Barbara). He was baptized in 1826.

ghart.name: Guillermo Antonio Hartnell, Sr.
ghart.description: Guillermo Antonio Hartnell, Sr. (1826-?): businessman, ranchero, teacher, translator, and son of William Edward Petty Hartnell (1798-1854). Born in England and educated in Germany, Hartnell Sr. first moved to Chile as an agent in John Begg & Co., a British firm (British influence increased as Latin American countries gained independence). Several years later he formed a partnership with Hugh McCulloch to enter the hide and tallow trade and relocated to Alta California, where he converted to Catholicism, married Maria Teresa de la Guerra and into a wealthy Californio family, and became a naturalized Mexican citizen. He became a ranchero, operated a school (El Seminario del Patrocinio de San Jose), and served as a translator for many government documents during the transition between Mexican and US rule. His son Hartnell Jr. had married and moved to Santa Barbara by 1852.

gei.name: Gustavus Augustus Eisen
gei.description: Gustavus Augustus Eisen (1847-1940): a Swedish scientist. Eisen attended the University of Uppsala, then settled in California. He was an active member of the California Academy of Sciences, serving as curator of varied departments during his career. Eisen was a pioneering California vintner and built the Eisen Vineyard; he is also well-known for spearheading early conservation efforts for the giant sequoias of the Sierra Nevada.

bloom.name: H. R. Bloomer
bloom.description: H. R. Bloomer (1821-74): botanist and collector. Bloomer attended Newburgh College (New York, now part of the SUNY system) and served in various positions in the California Academy of Sciences.

henshaw.name: H. W. Henshaw
henshaw.description: H. W. Henshaw: a naturalist and ornithologist. Henshaw collected specimens on Santa Cruz Island for the Smithsonian in 1875, alongside Dr. Joseph Trimble Rothrock Rothrock and Dr. Oscar Loew.

hac.name: Hace
hac.description: Hace: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part. He left the group in 1831 while in New Mexico.

heb.name: Harold Hal Eberhart
heb.description: Harold Hal Eberhart: an archaeologist (PhD, cultural anthropology, UCLA) whose work centered on California; he taught at California State University at Los Angeles.

har.name: Harril
har.description: Harril: the husband of Isabella Nidever Harril, George Nidever's sister.

hrvoth.name: Heinrich Richert Voth
hrvoth.description: Heinrich Richert Voth (1855-1931): ethnographer and Mennonite missionary who moved with his family from Russia to the United States. Voth attended the Christian Educational Institute, Theological Seminary of the Evangelical Synod of North America, and St. Louis Medical College before beginning educational mission work with the Cheyenne and Arapaho. Voth believed that Native peoples must eschew their culture to thrive; as an ethnographer, he studied the cultures he simultaneously worked to eradicate. Voth later founded a Mission for the Hopi in Arizona.

hsc.name: Heinrich Schliemann
hsc.description: Heinrich Schliemann (1822-90): a German-born businessman and archaeologist who rose from a poor cabin boy to become a wealthy merchant adept at languages and free to pursue his passion in archaeology. He is best known for his discovery of Homeric Troy, once believed to be fiction. Contemporary scholars now view the original excavation of Hisarlik, Turkey to be compromised, but the work of Schliemann and especially that of his later assistant, Wilhelm Doerpfeld, added greatly to understandings of prehistoric Western culture. Schliemann’s connection to California comes via St. Petersburg, where he was sent as an agent of his firm in the 1840s. During the Gold Rush he moved to the United States and became a US citizen.

hro.name: Helen Marguerite Roberts
hro.description: Helen Marguerite Roberts (1896-1983): an American writer and educator. From 1958 to 1975, she worked in Africa, bringing literacy, health education, and Christianiaty to adults in Kenya and Rhodesia. As part of this work, she also mentored many young people, including Barack H. Obama, the father of future US president Barack Obama.

hfo.name: Henry Chapman Ford
hfo.description: Henry Chapman Ford (1828-94): an artist noted for his renderings of California landscapes. Ford co-founded both the Chicago Academy of Design and the Santa Barbara Natural History Society.

taylor.name: Henry Clay Taylor
taylor.description: Henry Clay Taylor (1845-1904): a sailor and naval officer. A graduate of the United States Naval Academy, Taylor fought in both the Civil and Spanish-American Wars and served as president of the Naval War College.

hti.name: Henry G. Tinsley
hti.description: Henry G. Tinsley (c. 1866-1921): a newsman who published several California newspapers, most notably the Pomona Progress.

hmor.name: Henry H. More
hmor.description: Henry H. More: one of the very successful More brothers, all of whom became prominent figures in the development of the region around Santa Barbara in the mid-nineteenth century. Between 1859-70, three of the brothers (A. P., T. Wallace, and Henry H. More) acquired sole proprietorship of Santa Rosa Island and developed the island into a thriving sheep-raising venture.

hey.name: Henry James Ten Eyck
hey.description: Henry James Ten Eyck (1856-87): an editor of the Albany Evening Journal. He graduated from Yale College (where he was third in his class) in 1879 before entering the newspaper industry.

hni.name: Henry Nidever
hni.description: Henry Nidever (1809-49): farmer and brother of George Nidever (junior). He was born in Sullivan County, Tennessee and died in Lamar, Delta, Texas.

htd.name: Henry T. Dittman
htd.description: Henry T. Dittman (1913-88): grandson of Carl Dittman ("Charley Brown"), who, along with George Nidever, brought the Lone Woman from San Nicolas Island to the California mainland in 1853.

hwbe.name: Henry Ward Beecher
hwbe.description: Henry Ward Beecher (1813-87): religious leader and activist. Beecher was the son of a prominent Calvinist minister of the Second Great Awakening and part of a family of reformers that included his sister, Harriet Beecher Stowe (anti-slavery activist and author of Uncle Tom's Cabin). Beecher attended Amherst College and afterward became a Presbyterian minister. As his theology became increasingly liberal (dubbed the "Doctrine of Love"), Beecher became more politically active. He advocated for the abolition of slavery, was greatly influenced by the transcendental movement, and pastored one of the first "mega-churches" in the United States.

hbrown.name: Herbert Brown
hbrown.description: Herbert Brown: this account does not give further information identifying this individual, but possibly refers to Herbert H. Brown, a naturalist, archaeologist, and the first curator of the Arizona State Museum.

her.name: Hercules
her.description: Hercules: the mythological son of Zeus and Alcmene. As a half-god, half-mortal, the story of Hercules centers around the feats by which he earns his place on Mount Olympus.

hcor.name: Hernán Cortés
hcor.description: Hernán "Hernando" Cortés (1485-1547): a Spanish conquistador, explorer, and governor and captain general of New Spain. Cortés aided Diego Velázquez in the conquest of Cuba and then commanded an expedition to Mexico, where he held the Aztec emperor Montezuma II hostage. He eventually took the capital Tenochtitlán and much of the surrounding area. Cortés later explored much of Central America trying to find a passage between the Pacific and Atlantic Oceans; instead, he arrived in California.

hva.name: Hilario Valenzuela
hva.description: Hilario Valenzuela: a Yaqui man who traveled with George Nidever and his otter-hunting crew to San Nicolas Island in 1853; the crew returned with the Lone Woman. According to Fernando Librado (Kitsepawit), J. P. Harrington's chief Ventureño Chumash informant, Valenzuela received bone needles from the Lone Woman. The Yaqui homeland is in the present-day Mexican state of Sonora, and Valenzuela was born there around the year 1820.

bouc.name: Hipólito Bouchard
bouc.description: Hipólito "Hypolite" Bouchard (c. 1785-1837): a French Argentine privateer who circumnavigated the globe in 1817. Given Argentine authority to attack and confiscate Spanish property, he raided Monterey, California, in 1818. After plundering the California coast, he retired to Peru, where he was killed by his own slaves in 1837.

hmi.name: Hiram Wallace Mills
hmi.description: Hiram Wallace Mills (1828-1915): a sheep ranch operator on San Miguel Island. Mills purchased the San Miguel ranch from George Nidever and his sons in 1869. Along with his brother Warren Mills and other investors, Mills created the Pacific Wool Growing Company. The company’s ranching efforts continued until the natural flora on San Miguel was exhausted; the ranch was sold in 1887.

hru.name: Horatio Nelson Rust
hru.description: Horatio Nelson Rust (1828-1906): businessman, abolitionist, federal Indian agent, and amateur archaeologist and collector. Born and raised in Massachusetts, Rust spent eighteen years as a traveling salesman and relic collector in New England and eastern Canada before moving to Chicago and then, in his fifties, to Pasadena. During his lifetime he collected nearly 10,000 artifacts that helped build museum collections at institutions including Amherst, Beloit College (Wisconsin), the Field Museum of Natural History, the Smithsonian, the University of Pennsylvania, and Yale.

sbaa.name: Hottentot Venus
sbaa.description: Hottentot Venus (1789?-1815): stage name for Sarah Baartman, one of several indigenous South African women brought to Europe and exhibited as sideshow attractions. Their racialized bodies became a means by which people attempted to differentiate between Caucasian and "exotic" femininity.

hswan.name: Howard Swan
hswan.description: Howard Swan: a linguist and educator. Swan attended the University of California, Berkeley, Harvard University, and London University. He taught at the Guozijian, or Imperial College, in Beijing and was a member of the American Anthropological Association.

huck.name: Hucklebery Finn
huck.description: Huckleberry Finn: titular character of Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1884-85), the hugely popular and enduring coming-of-age novel by Mark Twain.

hre.name: Hugo Reid
hre.description: Hugo Reid (1811-52) a Scottish emigrant to Mexico who migrated to Alta California and settled in Los Angeles. He married and adopted the children of a widowed Gabrieliña (Comicrabit) woman who was raised in San Gabriel Mission. Intermarriage with an Indian was an unusual practice in California at the time, but it gave Reid access to land and enabled his wife Victoria to pass into elite society during the Mexican period. Reid was given a substantial land grant, Rancho Santa Anita, by the Mexican governor Pio Pico in 1845. In 1850, Reid was elected to the California Congressional Convention. He is best known for a series of 22 letters written during 1852 that discuss the culture and language of the Gabrieliño people prior to European contact; they were published in the Los Angeles Star then reprinted in book form.

hba.name: Hubert Howe Bancroft
hba.description: Hurbert Howe Bancroft (1832-1918): historian and ethnologist who assembled and published a vast collection of archival and oral history materials on the West, broadly conceived. The University of California’s library, the Bancroft Library, is named in his honor.

iba.name: Iakov Babin
iba.description: Iakov Babin (?-1839?): a fur hunter who worked for the Russian American Company (RAC) in North America. Iakov Babin was the Russian overseer of an Alaska Native hunting crew on San Nicolas Island in 1814. Babin’s group massacred a large number of Nicoleños after one sea otter hunter was killed. Babin was held responsible for the actions of his Alaska Native hunting crew by three RAC chief managers. Following his return to New Archangel (Sitka), he married a baptized California Indian woman.

igr.name: Isaac Graham
igr.description: Isaac Graham: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part.

isp.name: Isaac J. Sparks
isp.description: Isaac J. Sparks (1803-67): hunter, merchant, and rancher. Isaac Sparks is known for sailing to San Nicolas Island in 1835 and returning to San Pedro with a group of islanders, leaving the Lone Woman behind. A Spanish document notes that Sparks and seven men sailed from Santa Barbara on the schooner Peor es Nada in 1834; Charles Hubbard may have been serving as captain. Sparks had arrived in California in 1832, and he joined in many sea otter hunting trips on the California coast. He owned the more than 22,000 acre Rancho Huasna, in present-day San Luis Obispo County.

whi.name: Isaac Whittemore
whi.description: Isaac Whittemore (?-1818): US sea captain who worked with the Russian American Company transporting hunters and trading furs. Whittemore was wrongly connected to the massacre of the Nicoleños in an 1856 Santa Barbara Gazette article, which claimed that he had left sea otter hunters on San Nicolas Island in 1811 and picked them up after a massacre occurred. This violence took place in 1814 and Whittemore was not involved.

iwi.name: Isaac Williams
iwi.description: Isaac Williams (1799-1856): an American who arrived in Los Angeles in 1832. He married a local woman in 1836 and by 1842, he owned Rancho Santa Ana del Chino. In 1855, he was appointed Collector of Customs at the Port of San Pedro. Secondary accounts claim Williams sailed to San Nicolas Island in 1835 when Nicoleños were removed and taken to San Pedro. Either Charles Hubbard or Isaac Sparks served as captain for that voyage, although Williams might have been on board. According to George Nidever, Williams brought a Nicoleña to his Los Angeles home.

ini.name: Isabel/Ysabel Tomasa Nidever
ini.description: Isabel/Ysabel Tomasa Nidever (1851-1930): one of George Nidever and Sinforosa Sanchez de Nidever’s two daughters. She was the youngest child and still a toddler, not yet two years old, when the Lone Woman came to live with the family in 1853.

inid.name: Isabella Nidever
inid.description: Isabella Nidever: sister of George Nidever (junior).

ish.name: Ishi
ish.description: Ishi (?-1916): a man who became famous as "the last Yahi" when, in 1911, he emerged from hiding and entered a populated section of California in search of food. The Yahi are a subgroup of the Yana people. This Native man was given the name "Ishi" (or "man") by anthropologists, and was studied and cared for by University of California researchers including Alfred Kroeber, T. T. Waterman, E. W. Gifford, and Saxton T. Pope. He was displayed to the public at the Phoebe Hearst Museum of Anthropology and became the subject of several books. More recent scholarship questions Ishi’s status as a "last Indian."

kuskov.name: Ivan Kuskov
kuskov.description: Ivan Kuskov (1765-1823): founder and manager of Fort Ross, a Russian settlement in northern California from 1812 to 1821. Fort Ross served as an agricultural base for the Russian American Company’s Alaska holdings.

dup.name: J. B. Pigné-Dupuytren
dup.description: J. B. Pigné-Dupuytren (1807-86): a French physician. Pigné-Dupuytren was educated in Europe and held various positions at the Anatomical Museum in Paris before emigrating to the United States.

jdgl.name: J. Duncan Gleason
jdgl.description: J. Duncan Gleason (1881-1959): an illustrator and artist. His published works include Islands of California (1950) and Islands and Ports of California (1958).

jmu.name: J. R. Mullett
jmu.description: J. R. Mullett: a sea-lion hunter operating on the coast of Southern California during this time.

jwhast.name: J. W. Hastings
jwhast.description: J. W. Hastings: no further information has been found excepting his association with the American Anthropological Association during this time. This account possibly refers to John Woodland Hastings, a prominent biologist and member of the National Academy of Sciences.

jbu.name: J. A. Bunting
jbu.description: J.A. Bunting: a resident of Centerville, California, who was part of the three-woman committee responsible for the creation of Historic Facts and Fancies, a book of state history published in 1907 by the Federated Women's Clubs of California.

jth.name: J. E.Thane
jth.description: J.E. Thane: a resident of Niles, California, who was part of the three-woman committee responsible for the creation of Historic Facts and Fancies, a book of state history published in 1907 by the Federated Women's Clubs of California.

jafo.name: James Alexander Forbes
jafo.description: James Alexander Forbes (1805-81): businessman and government official in California, both during the Mexican and American periods. Forbes arrived in California in the 1830s, and was employed by Hudson's Bay Company before serving as British Vice-Consul to Mexico. Forbes ultimately became a partner in Barron, Forbes, & Company, with interests in a Santa Clara quicksilver mine. He built the Forbes Mill, which eventually grew into Los Gatos, California.

jan.name: James Anderson
jan.description: James Anderson: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part.

bas.name: James Basey
bas.description: James Basey: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part. Nidever describes him as a "large powerful man."

dbo.name: James DeMoss Bowers
dbo.description: James DeMoss Bowers (1863?-1917): an amateur archaeologist and collector. Along with his father Stephen DeMoss Bowers, he excavated sites in Southern California and on the Channel Islands. Father and son also published the Ventura Free Press.

jli.name: James Lick
jli.description: James Lick (1796-1876): a craftsman, businessman, and philanthropist who was born in Pennsylvania but who made his fortune first in South America (making and selling pianos) and then again in real estate ventures in booming, post-statehood California. Later in life, as a California millionaire, he became a patron of the sciences and left funds for what would eventually become the University of California's Lick Observatory.

jmooney.name: James Mooney
jmooney.description: James Mooney (1861-1921): an ethnographer. Mooney was largely self-taught and worked as a school teacher and journalist before applying to work with the Bureau of American Ethnology, with which he was employed for the rest of his life. He became known as an authority on Cherokee and Kiowa cultures and was noted for detail-driven field research and longstanding relationships with the peoples he studied. Mooney contributed numerous articles to the Handbook of American Indians North of Mexico (1907 and 1910).

jsjo.name: James Santiago Johnson
jsjo.description: James Santiago Johnson (1798?-1847): an Englishman who moved to Guaymas, Mexico as a young man to engage in the merchant shipping business. Johnson married a local woman and, in 1834, moved with her and several members of her family to Alta California. Once in California, Johnson split his time between Los Angeles and San Pedro, where he operated a ranch and had a home near the harbor. Johnson’s young daughter, Adelaide, was named a godmother to Tomás Guadalupe, a Nicoleño who likely arrived in San Pedro on the Peor es Nada in 1835. Johnson was related to three other people who were named as godparents to other baptized Island Indians who likely arrived on the Peor es Nada as well. It is possible that Johnson was on the Peor es Nada when the vessel brought the Nicoleños to the mainland; J. J. Warner reported this fact in the Los Angeles Star.

jte.name: James Terry, Jr.
jte.description: James Terry, Jr. (1844-1912): an amateur anthropologist, artifact-collector, and researcher. Terry worked for his father in the clock industry before becoming a full-time collector of artifacts, primarily in the southwestern United States. He brought his findings (now known as the Terry Collection) to the American Museum of Natural History and served as curator from 1891-94, before a dispute with the Board of Trustees led to his dismissal.

jcalkins.name: James W. Calkins
jcalkins.description: James W. Calkins: a banker and president of the First National Bank of Santa Barbara. In 1878, he built a mansion that now serves as the headquarters for the University Club, a private social venue.

gillespie.name: James Waldron Gillespie
gillespie.description: James Waldron Gillespie (1865-1954): a capitalist from Granville, New York, who inherited a real estate fortune when he was 21 years old. Gillespie was renowned for his grandeur; he owned properties in Havana and Manhattan but was best known for his home “El Fureidis” (meaning “little paradise”) in Montecito, California. The estate’s Italian architecture and formal garden made it a fitting location for Gillespie’s exclusive parties.

jod.name: Jane Dorsa O'Dell
jod.description: Jane Dorsa O’Dell (née Rattenbury) (1911-2008): an advice columnist for the Los Angeles Mirror-News (under the name "Jane Palmer") and wife of Scott O'Dell when he wrote—and she typed—Island of the Blue Dolphins. The novel is dedicated to Dorsa O’Dell’s nieces and nephews. Dorsa and Scott separated in the 1960s but did not divorce until 1989; Dorsa continued to live in the Julian, California home in which Island of the Blue Dolphins was written until her death. She was active in Civil Rights, peace, and conservation organizations.

jti.name: Janice Timbrook
jti.description: Janice Timbrook: an anthropologist and ethnobotanist specializing in the Chumash people's historical use of plants for food, medicine, and basket-making in southern California. She earned a PhD in anthropology from the University of California, Santa Barbara, and has long been employed as a curator at the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. She is the author of Chumash Ethnobotany: Plant Knowledge Among the Chumash People of Southern California (2007).

jcge.name: Jean Craighead George
jcge.description: Jean Craighead George (1919-2012): a journalist, author, illustrator, and naturalist. George wrote for numerous publications including the Washington Post, Times-Herald, National Wildlife, and Audubon. She is best known today for her children's books, including My Side of the Mountain (1959, 1960 Newbery Medal Honor book) and Julie of the Wolves (1972, 1973 Newbery Medal Winner).

fde.name: Jean Ferdinand Denis
fde.description: Jean Ferdinand Denis (1798-1890): French author and library administrator. Denis was an administrator of the Sainte-Geneviève Library and a member of the French Academy. His published works include Résumé de l'histoire littéraire du Portugal, suivi du résumé de l'histoire littéraire du Brésil (1826) and Le Brahme voyageur, ou la Sagesse populaire de toutes les nations (1832).

lamberth2.name: Lamberth
lamberth2.description: Jeanne Carol Lamberth (née Hess) (1931-): resident of San Nicolas Island as a child in the late 1930s. Her parents, Reginald H. Lamberth and Evelyn Margaret, were managers of a sheep estate on the island.

jewier.name: Jeanne Elizabeth Wier
jewier.description: Jeanne Elizabeth Wier (1870-1950): an historian (PhD, University of Nevada). Wier attended Stanford University and the University of Nevada, where she later chaired the Department of History. She helped found the Nevada Historical Society and was a leader in the state's suffrage movement. Wier's published works include historical pamphlets and contributed articles to the Encyclopedia Britannica.

jcle.name: Jeremy Clegg
jcle.description: Jeremy Clegg: fictional character in Scott O'Dell's sea novel The Dark Canoe.

jwfew.name: Jesse Walter Fewkes
jwfew.description: Jesse Walter Fewkes (1850-1930): an ethnologist, zoologist, and natural historian. He earned a PhD in zoology from Harvard University and also studied at the University of Leipzig. Fewkes worked in the Museum of Comparative Zoology at Harvard and contributed to the Kingsley Standard Natural History (1885) before transitioning into a career in anthropology. He led an expedition to study Pueblo culture (with particular interest in Hopi ritual and the preservation of Native American structures). Following the Spanish-American War, Fewkes conducted excavations in Puerto Rico and extended his work on pre-Columbian sites elsewhere in the region. His published work includes Two Summers' Work in Pueblo Ruins (1897) and The Aborigines of Porto Rico and Neighboring Islands (1907).

jno.name: Jim Norris
jno.description: Jim Norris: a local California historian and author. Norris edited the newsletter of the Santa Ynez Valley Historical Society.

jai.name: Joan Delano Aiken
jai.description: Joan Delano Aiken (1924-2004): an English author and librarian. Privately educated, Aiken worked for the BBC during World War II, as a librarian for the United Nations Information Office, and in early television advertising. Later in life, she published fiction for children and adults, including The Wolves of Willoughby Chase (1962) and Mansfield Revisited (1985).

jmill.name: Joaquin Miller
jmill.description: Joaquin Miller (1837-1913): a writer, poet, and journalist known for his outlandish stories about the American “Old West.”  Miller found literary success and popularity along the Pacific Coast, as well as in Great Britain. In 1891, Miller wrote an article about the Lone Woman that appeared in the New York Herald.

jdy.name: Job F. Dye
jdy.description: Job F. Dye (1807-83): a Kentucky trapper who traveled with Ewing Young’s hunting group. Dye arrived to California in 1832 and, according to George Nidever, joined Isaac Sparks and Lewis T. Burton on the 1834 voyage of the ship Peor es Nada to hunt sea otters along the California coast. Dye may have been on the Peor es Nada in 1835 when the Nicoleños were removed from San Nicolas Island and taken to San Pedro.

jro.name: Joe Robinson
jro.description: Joe Robinson: a journalist, motivational speaker, and author. Robinson is the founder of Work to Live and is a contributing journalist to the Huffington Post.

row.name: John A. Rowland
row.description: John A. Rowland (1791-1873): a fur-trapper, distiller, and store operator who worked in Taos, New Mexico, alongside his business partner (and naturalized Mexican citizen) William Workman. In 1840, the Republic of Texas (having declared its independence from Mexico) named Rowland and Workman agents, perhaps without their knowledge. Liable for treason, the partners fled to Los Angeles, leading a wagon train of settlers with them. In 1845, Rowland and Workman acquired Rancho La Puente, a 50,000-acre tract of land east of Los Angeles.

jbmc.name: John B. McDonald
jbmc.description: John B. McDonald (1844-1911): the contractor for the first subway system in New York City. McDonald’s company provided the equipment and labor for constructing the system, while the Interborough Rapid Transit Company facilitated financing of the project. The construction of the subway began in 1900 and concluded in 1904.

jcmerriam.name: John Campbell Merriam
jcmerriam.description: John Campbell Merriam (1869-1945): a paleontologist (PhD, University of Munich). Merriam attended Lenox College (Iowa) and the University of California before studying with the famous paleontologist Karl von Zittel in Germany. He returned to the United States to teach at the University of California. After two decades of extensive field work (including in the La Brea tar pits), he became a regent of the Smithsonian Institution and helped develop educational programs with the California Institute of Technology and National Park Service. Merriam was active in conservation efforts. He is the author of The Living Past (1930) and The Garment of God (1943).

jcbranner.name: John Casper Banner
jcbranner.description: John Casper Banner (1850-1922): a geologist. Banner attended Cornell University and while still a young student, worked with his advisor on a geological survey in Brazil. Banner later served on the Geological Survey of Pennsylvania, where he conducted topographical mapping in regions central to coal production. Banner taught at Indiana University (simultaneously receiving his PhD there), was appointed the state geologist of Arkansas, and was then recruited to become one of the first professors of the newly-formed Stanford University, heading the Geology Department. Before his retirement, Banner served as the University's president.

jcfre.name: John Charles Frémont
jcfre.description: John Charles Frémont (1813-90): a US soldier, engineer, explorer, and politician. Born in Georgia, Frémont is best-known for his role in the Bear Flag Republic, the California government briefly established after US citizens and Californio allies living in Mexican California staged a rebellion to facilitate United States annexation. After the Mexican-American War, Frémont represented California as a US Senator, ran for president of the United States multiple times, and served as a general in the Union Army during the Civil War.

jcj.name: John Coffin Jones
jcj.description: John Coffin Jones (1796-1861): a diplomat and businessman. Jones attended Harvard University before entering trade and working in Hawaii both as a consul of the United States State Department and representative of the Boston trading firm Marshall & Wildes’ interests. After his conflict with missionary concerns in Hawaii resulted in his dismissal as consul, he again worked in trade on both coasts, and he owned many trading vessels. Jones was also known for his tumultuous personal life.

for.name: John Forster
for.description: John Forster (1814-82): a shipping agent and landowner. Born in Liverpool, England, Forster traveled to Guaymas, Mexico as a young man to work in the shipping firm of his uncle, James "Santiago" Johnson. As the firm Johnson and Aguirre expanded, Forster moved to Mexican California, where he settled. He became a Mexican citizen (known as "Juan Foster") and married into the Californio elite (his wife was the sister of future California governor Pio Pico). During the 1840s, Forster acquired significant holding through land grants (Rancho Trabuco and Rancho Mission Viejo) and his own purchases. He continued to prosper through various ventures (horse breeding, cattle) after California entered the United States. Foster likely encountered the Nicoleño who came to be known as Black Hawk when he served as captain of the Port of San Pedro, between 1842-44.

jga.name: John Gantt
jga.description: John Gantt (1790-1849): soldier, trapper, Indian agent, and guide on the emigrant trail West. John Gantt was the son Edward Gantt, Thomas Jefferson’s attending physician. As a young man he began his career in the US Army but was dismissed from his officer position for misconduct. He reinvented himself by moving West and entering the fur and trapping trade in partnership with Jefferson Blackwell. By the 1840s, Gantt was in California, where he died at the ranch of mountain man George Yount.

jhwright.name: John H. Wright
jhwright.description: John H. Wright: an anthropologist and professor at Harvard University. Wright was associated with the American Anthropological Association, American Association for the Advancement of Science, and the Archaeological Institute of America.

jjo.name: John Johnson
jjo.description: John Johnson: archaeologist and ethnohistorian. Johnson earned his PhD in anthropology from the University of California, Santa Barbara, and is currently the curator of anthropology at the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. He specializes in the culture and history of the Native peoples of south central California.

jni.name: John Nidever
jni.description: John Nidever (1796?-1872): older brother of otter hunter George Nidever.  Born in Tennessee, he moved to Texas with his wife and children and lived there until at least the early 1850s. He did not live in Santa Barbara at the time the Lone Woman was brought from San Nicolas Island, but he did eventually move to the city where his brother George Nidever lived. The property he purchased, near George Nidever’s, is in the current location of the Santa Barbara Zoo.

jobr.name: John O’Brien
jobr.description: John O'Brien: sea otter hunter who teamed with George Nidever, Isaac Sparks, and Allen Light during the 1830s.

jburk.name: John P. Burkhard
jburk.description: John P. Burkhard: the publisher of the sporting magazine Field and Stream.

jpha.name: John P. Harrigan
jpha.description: John P. Harrigan: a licensed electrical engineer. Harrigan attended California State Polytechnic University, Pomona, and works for the City of Los Angeles. As of 2016 he was a United States Forest Service preservation volunteer and a member of the Scenic Mount Lowe Historical Committee.

jha.name: John Peabody Harrington
jha.description: John Peabody Harrington (1883-1961): an ethnographer who worked for the Smithsonian Institution in Washington, DC, from 1915–55. Harrington interviewed numerous American Indian peoples, including Chumash and Gabrielino (Tongva), and he recorded information about Native languages and culture. His informants provided important details about interactions with the Lone Woman in 1853, and their accounts are the only source of information about the Lone Woman from a Native rather than Euro-American perspective.

jpr.name: John Price
jpr.description: John Price: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part.

jswanton.name: John Reed Swanton
jswanton.description: John Reed Swanton (1872-1958): an ethnologist (PhD, Harvard University) who did his graduate work with Franz Boas and Livingston Farrand at Columbia University. Swanton spent his entire career (1900-44) with the Bureau of American Ethnology at the Smithsonian Institution. His early research was in the Pacific Northwest and Alaska, where he studied the Haida and Tlingit. He is best known, however, for his study of the cultures and languages of the indigenous peoples of the American South (Texas, Oklahoma, and Mississippi) and Southeast. Swanton is often considered a pioneering ethnohistorian because he consulted historical records left by the English, French, and Spanish to contextualize his field work. His most important published work is the 943-page The Indians of the Southeastern United States (1946).

ruskin.name: John Ruskin
ruskin.description: John Ruskin: one of the foremost critics of the Victorian era.  Ruskin was a poet, painter, essayist, and professor of art at the University of Oxford.

jstein.name: John Steinbeck
jstein.description: John Steinbeck (1902-68): an author and journalist known for his spare prose. Steinbeck was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1962. Among his most significant works are Of Mice and Men (1937) and The Grapes of Wrath (1939).

jmcg.name: John Steven McGroarty
jmcg.description: John Steven McGroarty (1862-1944): a journalist and politician. McGroarty spent more than forty years with the Los Angeles Times while also writing for a number of other publications. His informal history, California: Its History and Romance (1911), was published in multiple editions. McGroary also served as a Representative for Congress.

jta.name: John Strong Perry Tatlock
jta.description: John Strong Perry Tatlock (1876-1948): a medieval scholar (PhD, Harvard). He served as the President of the Modern Language Association in 1938. His published works include The Modern Reader's Chaucer (1912, with Percy MacKaye) and Mohammed and his Followers in Dante (1932).

jjvickers.name: John Van "J. V." Vickers
jjvickers.description: John Van "J. V." Vickers (1850-1912): a founder of the Chiricahua Cattle Company, one of the largest cattle companies in the western United States. He purchased Santa Rosa Island with Walter Vail in 1902.

jwhudson.name: John Wilz Napier Hudson
jwhudson.description: John Wilz Napier Hudson (1857-1936): a medical doctor. After moving to northern California in the late 1890s, Hudson, together with his wife, the painter Grace Carpenter Hudson, became a collector of and expert in local Pomoan basketry. The Hudsons’ Overland Monthly article on Pomo baskets (1893) garnered national attention during a period when Indian baskets were a national fad. The Hudsons’ sizeable basket collection, with a descriptive catalogue written by John, now resides in the Smithsonian.

jtre.name: Johnny Tremain
jtre.description: Johnny Tremain: the titular character of Esther Forbes' Johnny Tremain (1943), an historical coming-of-age novel set during the American Revolution.

jhet.name: Joice Heth
jhet.description: Joice Heth (?-1836): an African American who toured with showman P. T. Barnum beginning in 1835. Barnum claimed that Heth was 161 years old and that, as a slave, she had been owned by George Washington's father, who used her as a nursemaid to the future President. When interest in Heth flagged, Barnum created controversy (an anonymous letter-to-the-editor claiming she was a fake) that reignited public interest.

jst.name: Jon C. Stott
jst.description: Jon C. Stott: a children's literature scholar, educator, and author. Stott earned his PhD in English and American literature at the University of Toronto and then taught at that institution, Western Michigan University, and the University of Alberta, from where he retired. He was a founding member of the Children's Literature Association.

jstev.name: Jonathan Drake Stevenson
jstev.description: Jonathan Drake Stevenson (1800-94): a soldier and politician. Stevenson was the private secretary of New York governor and later US vice president Daniel D. Tompkins (running mate of James Monroe). Stevenson served in the New York Assembly and was appointed to a military command in California during the Mexican-American War. He afterwards made his living in mining and real estate ventures.

jwin.name: Jonathin Winship, Jr.
jwin.description: Jonathin Winship, Jr.: a sailor and captain of the ship O'Cain around the time the Nicoleño people were taken to mainland California. Winship is credited with discovering Humboldt Bay in 1806, in this same ship.

jcastro.name: José Antonio Castro
jcastro.description: José Antonio Castro: a Mexican soldier and politician. Castro served as acting governor of Alta California during the absence of Governor José Figueroa and for a short time after his death in 1835. He also served in the Mexican-American War.

jcarillo.name: José Antonio Ezequiel Carrillo
jcarillo.description: José Antonio Ezequiel Carrillo (1796-1862): a soldier, rancher, and politician along with then-governor of Alta California Juan Alvarado. He was the bother of Carlos Antonio de Jesus Carrillo and served as a member of the California Constitutional Convention.

jsa.name: José Bernardo Sánchez
jsa.description: José Bernardo Sánchez (1778-1833): a Spanish Franciscan friar and Church administrator. Sánchez attended the missionary college at San Fernando, Mexico, and was then sent to California, where he served at Missions San Diego, Purisima, and San Gabriel. He was involved in efforts to subdue the Native populations of California and to establish interior mission sites. Sánchez later served as president of the missions and ardently opposed their secularization.

jju.name: Jose de los Santos Juncos
jju.description: José de los Santos Juncos (1841-1921): a Juaneño informant to J.P. Harrington. Santos Juncos said that mainland people baptized at Mission San Gabriel were unable to speak with people from Santa Catalina Island. Linguists believe that mainland and island groups may have spoken related, but separate, languages.

gnijr.name: George Nidever "Jr."
gnijr.description: José George Emigdio Nidever (1847-1935): the third son of George Nidever and Sinforosa Sanchez de Nidever. He was baptized as José George Emigdio Nidever in Santa Barbara in 1847 and known as George by the family and on census records. He was six years old when the Lone Woman came to stay with the family in 1853.

jgo.name: José Joaquin Gómez
jgo.description: José Joaquin Gomez: a wealthy Mexican landowner who lived in Monterey, California. He owned the 20.5 ton schooner Peor es Nada, which Isaac Sparks chartered to hunt sea otters along the California coast in 1834–35. The Peor es Nada was used in 1835 by Isaac Sparks to remove the Native people from San Nicolas Island and bring them to San Pedro, near Los Angeles. Charles Hubbard probably served as captain on this trip.

jal.name: Jose Manuel (Hiram?) Allen
jal.description: José Manuel (Hiram?) Allen: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part. He is described as a particularly good shot.

mmi.name: José Manuel Micheltorena
mmi.description: José Manuel Micheltorena (1802-53): a soldier. Micheltorena served in the Spanish army and as Commander General (Governor) of California and later Yucatán in an effort to reassert the Spanish Crown's control of those territories.

jru.name: José María de Jesús González Rubio
jru.description: José María de Jesús González Rubio (1804-75): one of three Franciscan priests at Mission Santa Barbara when the Lone Woman was brought to the city in 1853. According to George Nidever, Father González received some of her belongings when the Lone Woman died seven weeks after her arrival. He reportedly sent her dresses, bone needles, and other items to Rome, with Nidever’s approval.

jacobni.name: Jacob Nidever
jacobni.description: José Ramón Jacobo Nidever (1849-1913): the fourth and youngest son born to George Nidever and Sinforosa Sanchez de Nidever. Known in US census records as Jacob Nidever, he was four years old when the Lone Woman stayed with the Nidever family in Santa Barbara in late summer and fall of 1853.

rcar.name: Raymundo Carrillo
rcar.description: José Raymundo Tiburcio Carrillo (1810-?): son of Anastacio José Carrillo y Lugo. Carrillo was commander of the Presidio in Santa Barbara in 1846 and held a number of offices in Santa Barbara throughout the period; he served as alcalde of Santa Barbara in 1849.

rompjr.name: Romualdo Pacheco, Jr.
rompjr.description: José Romualdo Pacheco, Jr. (1831-99): sitting governor of California in 1875. Born in Santa Barbara during the Mexican era, Pacheco’s family included prominent merchants along the Californian coast. He attended Oahu Charity School in Honolulu, Hawaii, an institution run by close friends of his family, and became fluent in English and French. Pacheco accepted American citizenship in 1848 after the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo was signed, making California part of the United States. Shortly thereafter, Pacheco began his political career, serving in a variety of roles at the local and state level before running for a US House seat in 1876; he finally won the seat in 1878. He is known not only for his vocal disapproval of slavery throughout his political career, but also for his support of the controversial Chinese Exclusion Act in 1882. He died of Bright’s disease on January 23, 1899.

jka.name: José Sudón
jka.description: José Sudón/Kamuliyatset (1871?-?): the father of Policarpio, José Sudón (Kamuliyatset) was a Chumash who built plank canoes. According to J.P. Harrington’s notes, Sudón participated in the Chumash Indian revolt of 1824. He was related by marriage to two women who visited the Lone Woman and brought clams to her: Martina (Lekte) and Pilar.

vor.name: Vicente Ortega
vor.description: José Vicente Epifanio Ortega (1824-1910): third generation Californian and son of José Francisco Maria Ortega; owner of the Refugio rancho.

jchap.name: Joseph Chapman
jchap.description: Joseph Chapman (1784? -1848): merchant sailor, shipbuilder, and blacksmith from New England who sailed to Alta California, possibly as an impressed soldier, on board the ship of privateer André-Paul Hippolyte de Bouchard. He participated in an 1818 attack on Alta California that resulted in his imprisonment. After his release, Chapman oversaw the building of Mission San Gabriel and Mission Santa Inés's grist mills, among other public and private buildings. He converted to Catholicism, married a Californio woman, and became a naturalized citizen and grantee of San Pedro Rancho.

jcon.name: Joseph Conrad
jcon.description: Joseph Conrad (1857-1924): a novelist best-known for his portrayal of imperialism. Born in Poland with the name Józef Teodor Konrad Korzeniowski, Conrad served as a merchant sailor first for France and then Britain, to which he emigrated in order to avoid conscription by the Russian Army. His published works include Lord Jim (1900) and Heart of Darkness (1902).

jfol.name: Joseph Libbey Folsom
jfol.description: Joseph Libbey Folsom (1817-55): a soldier and real-estate investor. Folsom served as an officer in the United States Army during the Mexican-American War, under Jonathan Drake Stevenson. He later served as Collector of the Port of San Francisco and made his fortune in California real estate.

jhenniger.name: Joseph Morgan Henninger
jhenniger.description: Joseph Morgan Henninger (1906-99): painter, sculptor, and illustrator. He was educated at l’Ecole des Beaux Arts (Paris) and then established a studio in Indianapolis. During the Great Depression, he painted two WPA murals at Arizona State University. Henninger chaired the Illustration Department of Lockheed Aircraft during World War II. He eventually settled in Los Angeles, where he taught at the Art Center College of Design for more than three decades and worked on set designs in Hollywood (including work for MGM, Walt Disney, and Columbia Pictures). His publications include Drawing of the Hand and Its Anatomy (1973).

jlo.name: Joseph Nathan Lord
jlo.description: Joseph Nathan Lord (1852-90): a New York-born locksmith and sailor who moved to Santa Barbara as a young man. Lord was captain of the Undine until he died in the wreck of that ship, along with all onboard.

jwa.name: Joseph Reddeford Walker
jwa.description: Joseph Reddeford Walker (1798-1876): a trapper, prospector, and trader in the American West. Walker took part in numerous expeditions, including Benjamin Bonneville's explorations of the Rocky Mountains. He also served as a guide to John Charles Frémont and founded the trail that became the most famous entrance into California. Walker was also one of the first European Americans to see the falls at what is now Yosemite National Park.

rothrock.name: Joseph Trimble Rothrock
rothrock.description: Joseph Trimble Rothrock (1839-1922): a surgeon and botanist. Rothrock collected specimens on Santa Cruz Island in 1875 for the National Museum (the Smithsonian).

jel.name: Joseph Vincent Elliott
jel.description: Joseph Vincent Elliott (1860-1943): architect, building contractor, and part-owner, with his father Ezekiel Elliot, of Anacapa Island from 1882-97. The Elliotts used the island to raise sheep and to grow fruit, alfalfa, and grains. Elliott lived in Santa Barbara.

jcliff.name: Josephine Clifford McCracken
jcliff.description: Josephine Clifford McCracken (1838-1920): a writer and conservationist. Born in Germany, McCracken moved to the United States with her family as a child. She followed her brother, who had moved to California during the Gold Rush, west. As a single woman she supported herself through her pen, writing for numerous western publications, including the Overland Monthly, on both the east and west coasts. McCracken was also known for her conservation efforts, working with the California Humane and Audubon Societies and founding The Ladies' Forest and Song Birds Protection Association.

jgr.name: Josiah Gregg
jgr.description: Josiah Gregg (1806-52): a trader and explorer, raised in Missouri. Gregg dabbled in teaching, medicine, and law before finding success as a trader of goods in the West. He established new trails and routes to expand his business and led the first group of white settlers to Humboldt Bay, California, via land entry. Gregg wrote Commerce of the Prairies (1844), one of the first published works about the Santa Fe Trail. Later in life, he resumed medical study and established a practice.

jva.name: Juan Bautista Valentín Alvarado y Vallejo
jva.description: Juan Bautista Valentín Alvarado y Vallejo (1809-82): governor of Alta California from 1836-37 and 1842-45. In his memoir, Nidever mentions joining Alvarado's forces in 1836 in a revolution that unseated current governor Nicolás Gutiérrez.

tto.name: Juan de Torquemada
tto.description: Juan de Torquemada (1563-1624): a Spanish Franciscan friar best known for his history, Los veinte y un libros rituales y monarquía indiana (1615), a book commonly known by the shorthand, "Monarquía Indiana" (Indian Monarchy). It was republished a century after its initial appearance and used by later historians of Mexico.

rocha.name: Juan José Rocha
rocha.description: Juan José Rocha: soldier. Rocha was involved in the secularization of the San Juan Capistrano Mission (1833-34) and management of San Gabriel Mission (1836-37).

jwar.name: Juan José Warner
jwar.description: Juan José Warner (1807-95): merchant, ranchero, and California pioneer. Born Jonathan Trumball Warner, he moved west from Connecticut for his health and became a naturalized Mexican citizen before helping to secure California statehood and representing Los Angeles in the California Assembly. His Warner's Ranch served as a trading post and way station for travelers and, later, stagecoach passengers, on the Southern Emigrant Trail. During his lifetime, Warner was affectionately referred to as "Colonel" or "Don Juan" by many. He wrote of his pioneer experiences in "Reminiscences of Early California from 1831 to 1846."

jca.name: Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo
jca.description: Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo (?-1543): a Portuguese explorer. Cabrillo fought for the Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés and later (1542-43) led the first European exploration of the western coastline of the present-day United States. Although considered a failure at the time, the expedition produced the first written account of the North American west coast and therefore played an important part in Spain's northern expansion during the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.

jpas.name: Julia Pastrana
jpas.description: Julia Pastrana (1834-60): Mexican woman afflicted with hypertrichosis, a genetic condition that covers the body with hair, and gingival hyperplasia, a condition which thickens the lip and gums. She toured Europe and North America with circus showman Theodore Lent as the "Bearded and Hairy Lady."

jul.name: Julie
jul.description: Julie: a fictional character in Jean Craighead George’s novel Julie of the Wolves (and its sequels). Also known as Miyax, Julie is a twentieth-century Yupik teenager who sets off across the Alaskan tundra to escape an unhappy, arranged marriage. She is befriended by a pack of wolves during her journey.

jse.name: Junipero Serra
jse.description: Junípero Serra (1713-84): a California missionary known as the "father of the missions." Junípero Serra was born in Spain, entered the Franciscan order at age 16, and became a missionary in Mexico in 1749. When the Spanish Empire expelled the Jesuits from its colonies in 1767, the Franciscan order became the leader of New World missionary efforts. Serra assumed leadership of existing missions in Baja California and during the next decade and a half oversaw expansion of the mission system into Alta California, an effort designed to both Christianize and control the Native population and establish political and economic dominion over the region, preventing Russian expansion southward. Serra was a towering figure known for his powers of intellect and oratory, his administrative capabilities, his intense religious devotion (including mortifications of the flesh), and his strict rule. His legacy in California has become highly contentious; he was canonized by Pope Francis in 2015, amidst considerable protest.

jcaire.name: Justinian Caire
jcaire.description: Justinian Caire (1827-97): co-founder and later sole stockholder in the Santa Cruz Island Company, which owned Santa Cruz Island between 1869-87. Caire arrived in San Francisco from his natal France in 1851, opening a store for miners and, later, a hospital and bank. With investors, he purchased and incorporated Santa Cruz Island. Under his leadership, economic activity on the island expanded beyond sheep and cattle ranching to include horse and hog breeding, brick making, a vineyard, and orchards. At its height, more than 100 employees lived and worked on the island, many of whom were Italian.

karmik.name: Kamik
karmik.description: Kamik: sixteen-year-old Inuit protagonist of Harpoon of the Hunters (1970).

kar.name: Karana
kar.description: Karana: narrator and protagonist of Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960) and a fictionalization of the historical Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island.

kla.name: Karen Telleen-Lawton
kla.description: Karen Telleen-Lawton: journalist and certified financial planner.

kstein.name: Karl von den Steinen
kstein.description: Karl von den Steinen (1855-1929): a German physician and ethnologist. Steinen participated in numerous expeditions studying Native cultures, particularly in Brazil. He taught at Frederick William University (now Humboldt University of Berlin) and was associated with the Berlin Geographical Society.

kau.name: Kaspar Hauser
kau.description: Kaspar Hauser (1812?-1833): adolescent who emerged from the forest in Nuremberg, Germany, in 1828 speaking few words. He later claimed to have grown up in a darkened cell in total isolation. Although his social and intellectual growth suggested early human contact, Kaspar's story became associated with the trope of the "wolf child."

kro.name: Kenny Rogers
kro.description: Kenny Rogers (1938-): a US country music artist and actor. His records include Love or Something Like It (1978) and They Don't Make Them Like They Used To (1986). His films appearances include The Gambler (1980) and its sequels.

kimki.name: Kimki
kimki.description: Kimki: the man elected chief after the massacre in Scott O’Dell’s Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960). Because of an editing error, many printings of the novel have Karana wondering whether Ulape married him (or, as it was printed in the first edition, Kimko); in reality, Ulape was interested in a young man, Nanko, not the chief Kimki, who was many years her elder.

kingsp.name: King of Spain
kingsp.description: King of Spain: Ferdinand VII died the previous year, so the ruler of Spain was actually Queen Isabella II. However, land grants were issued by the governor of California, not by the Spanish crown, following Mexican independence. José Figueroa was governor at this time.

lansz.name: L. Lanszweert
lansz.description: L. Lanszweert: Curator of Zoology at the California Academy of Sciences. He may have been a chemist, based out of San Francisco.

ldi.name: L. Norton Dimmick
ldi.description: L. Norton Dimmick: a botanist. Dimmick corresponded with the Missouri physician and botanist George Englemann, among others. A number of the specimens he collected are now held at Harvard University.

larai.name: Larai
larai.description: Larai: a bird tamed by Karan in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960).

lpa.name: Lawrence A. Palinkas
lpa.description: Lawrence A. Palinkas: a medical anthropologist and the Frances L. and Albert G. Feldman Professor of Social Policy and Health at the University of Southern California. Palinkas earned a PhD in anthropology from the Universities of California, San Diego. His research centers on the effect of extreme circumstances (e.g., natural disasters) upon mental health.

lti.name: Lennox Tierney
lti.description: Lennox Tierney: likely Patrick Lennox Tierney (1914-2015), a professor of Japanese art at Pasadena City College around this time. This Lennox Tierney was a World War II "Monuments Man," serving in Allied Occupied Japan as Representative Commissioner of Art and Monuments. As such, he was responsible for overseeing the repair and conservation of culturally significant works damaged during the war. Later, Tierney became a faculty member at the University of Utah. Tierny also had a connection to the California Channel Islands, working for the Lester family ranch on San Miguel Island during the 1930s.

lhar.name: Leo Marcus Harloe
lhar.description: Leo Marcus Harloe (1916-78): the great-grandson of Isaac J. Sparks, the man who removed the Nicoleños from San Nicolas Island to the California mainland in 1835. Harloe wrote a biographical account of Sparks for his 1948 master’s thesis at University of Southern California, “The Life of Isaac J. Sparks.”

lce.name: Léon de Cessac
lce.description: Léon de Cessac (1841-91): a French geologist and ethnographer. Cessac traveled to the Americas as part of a research team in the 1870s and spent time on the California Channel Islands, including San Nicolas. The San Nicolas Island artifacts he brought back to Paris are now housed in the Musée de l'Homme.

lmloomis.name: Leverett Mills Loomis
lmloomis.description: Leverett Mills Loomis (1857-1928): ornithologist who was employed by the California Academy of Sciences, serving as curator of their Department of Ornithology and then as their director. He was author of A Review of the Albatrosses, Petrels, and Diving Petrels (1918).

lga.name: Levi M. Gano
lga.description: Levi M. Gano (c.1823-1903): a postmaster, teacher, principal, and editor of the Watkins Express, a local newspaper in Watkins, New York, beginning in 1864.

lwo.name: Lewis Keysor Wood
lwo.description: Lewis Keysor Wood (1819-74): a pharmacist and farmer. Wood was part of the first group of white settlers to enter Humboldt Bay, California, via land. He wrote The Discovery of Humboldt Bay, A Narrative (1872).

lbu.name: Lewis T. Burton
lbu.description: Lewis T. Burton (1809-79): a hunter and Tennessee native who arrived in California in 1831 and settled in Santa Barbara. According to George Nidever, Burton joined Isaac Sparks on the 1834 voyage of the schooner Peor es Nada to hunt sea otters on the California coast. Burton may have sailed with Sparks on the Peor es Nada in 1835 when the Nicoleños were taken from San Nicolas Island and brought to San Pedro.

lw.name: The Lone Woman
lw.description: Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island (1800?-53): a Gabrielina (Tongva) who lived on San Nicolas Island, California, and became famous because she lived alone on the island for eighteen years after the rest of her people left for the mainland in 1835; her real name is unknown. The Lone Woman left San Nicolas in 1853, living with George Nidever's family in Santa Barbara until her death seven weeks later. She is buried at the Santa Barbara Mission under the baptismal name Juana Maria. The Lone Woman was the model for Karana in Scott O’Dell’s Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960).

jim.name: Lord Jim
jim.description: Lord Jim: also known as Tuan Jim, the titular character in Joseph Conrad's novel Lord Jim (1900).

lya.name: Lorenzo Gordin Yates
lya.description: Lorenzo Gordin Yates (1837-1909): an English dentist whose avocation was natural history. Yates migrated to California from Wisconsin in the 1860s. In 1876, he joined amateur archaeologist Stephen Bowers on a research trip to Santa Rosa Island and then moved to Santa Barbara. He published on archaeology, paleontology, and marine animals.

lhu.name: Lorenzo Hubbard
lhu.description: Lorenzo Hubbard: author of Travels in Southern Oregon (1856).

lucifer.name: Lucifer
lucifer.description: Lucifer: regarded in Christian tradition as the name of Satan (the devil) prior to his fall from heaven due to his excessive pride and rebellion against God. After this name, which is Latin for "light bearer," was used by John Milton in Paradise Lost, the phrase "as proud as Lucifer" became common.

brinkwife.name: Lucy A. Brinkerhoff
brinkwife.description: Lucy A. Brinkerhoff (née Noyes): a Massachusetts woman who married Dr. Samuel Bevier Brinkerhoff in 1877. Brinkerhoff was a New York-born surgeon who settled in Santa Barbara and who, according to Emma Hardacre, treated the Lone Woman when she was living with the Nidever family in 1853. Lucy A. Brinkerhoff survived her husband when he passed in 1880.

lor.name: Luis Antonio Maria Ortega
lor.description: Luis Antonio Maria Ortega (1855-1927): a contemporary of Jacob Nidever (George Nidever’s son). Ortega was born in Rancho Refugio to a family that had been one of the wealthiest in Mexican California. When he spoke to J. P. Harrington about the Lone Woman, Ortega told Harrington that she had been brought to the home of Jacob’s parents after her arrival in Santa Barbara.

lyg.name: Luisa Ygnacio
lyg.description: Luisa Ygnacio (1836-1922): one of J. P. Harrington's Barbareño Chumash informants. She was married to Policaprio, who met the Lone Woman on San Nicolas Island as part of George Nidever's otter hunting part.  Luisa Ygnacio told Harrington that she thought the Lone Woman would have lived longer had she stayed with the Chumash in Santa Barbara instead of living with the Nidever family. Luisa Ygnacio also mentioned that two Chumash women, Martina (also known by her Native name, Lekte) and Pilar, gathered clams and brought them to the Lone Woman so that she had familiar food to eat. According to notes written by her granddaughter, Luisa Ygnacio met the Lone Woman in Santa Barbara and tried to speak with her, but was unable to communicate with her.

lburbank.name: Luther Burbank
lburbank.description: Luther Burbank (1849-1926): a horticulturist. Burbank's methods of cross-breeding plants resulted in over 800 new types, including the Idaho potato and numerous strains of lilies.

lyell.name: Lyman Elliott
lyell.description: Lyman P. Elliott (1892-1949): a sheep rancher on San Nicolas Island. Elliott and his family moved to San Nicolas in 1929, overseeing the large sheep herd on the island for a number of years.

lat.name: Lynn S. Atkinson
lat.description: Lynn S. Atkinson (1893-1925?): a contractor and engineer instrumental in the building of the Coolidge Dam in Arizona.  He was head of the Atkinson Construction Company, based in Los Angeles.

mdu.name: M. E. Dudley
mdu.description: M. E. Dudley: an Anglo-Californian writer who was active in historic preservation of the Spanish California past, including efforts to build El Camino Real. Her periodical writing appeared under the name Mrs. M. E. Dudley or M. E. Dudley in the Argonaut (in San Diego), Los Angeles Times, Ventura Daily Democrat, and Overland Monthly. She is likely Mary Elizabeth Dudley, born in 1853.

mllam.name: M. L. LaMoreaux
mllam.description: M. L. LaMoreaux: no further information has been found excepting her association with the American Anthropological Association and her position as President of the Texas Anthropological Association during this time.

maf.name: Mafatu
maf.description: Mafatu: the protagonist of Armstrong W. Sperry's Newbery Honor book, Call It Courage (1940).

mrog.name: Malcolm Rogers
mrog.description: Malcolm Jennings Rogers (1890-1960): an archaeologist who worked in southern California and Arizona. Rogers studied geology at Syracuse University, but never earned a degree. He spent several weeks on San Nicolas Island in 1929-30, excavating archaeological sites, and from 1930 to 1945, Rogers served as the curator and director for the San Diego Museum of Man.

msh.name: Melquiades
msh.description: Melquiades (1815-71): a Chumash man who lived in Ventura, California and who accompanied George Nidever on the 1853 hunting trip that brought the Lone Woman from San Nicolas Island to Santa Barbara. Melquiades memorized the words of a song he heard the Lone Woman sing, the Toki Toki song, and performed it for others. In 1913, more than half a century after the Lone Woman's death, a Chumash man named Fernando Librado sang the Toki Toki song for ethnographer J. P. Harrington, who recorded it onto a wax cylinder.

mca.name: Manuel Castillo
mca.description: Manuel Castillo: a Chicano boy and the protagonist of Scott O'Dell's Child of Fire (1974).

mrni.name: Marcos Ramón Nidever
mrni.description: Marcos (Mark) Ramón Nidever (1842-1909): the first-born son of George Nidever and Sinforosa Sanchez de Nidever.

Nicolenowoman3.name: María Aleja
Nicolenowoman3.description: María Aleja: an Island Indian who was baptized when she was about 45 years old as María Aleja at the Los Angeles Plaza Church in July 1836. She is highly likely to be one of the San Nicolas Island Natives who sailed from the island to San Pedro in 1835. Her baptism occurred shortly after the arrival of the schooner Peor es Nada, which brought the Nicoleños to San Pedro. Her godmother was María Ignacia Amador.

mrfni.name: María del Refugio Francisca Nidever
mrfni.description: María del Refugio Francisca Nidever (1844-69): the older of two daughters born to George Nidever and Sinforosa Sanchez de Nidever. She was nine years old when the Lone Woman lived in the Nidever home.

mjos.name: María Josefa Dana
mjos.description: María Josefa Dana (1812-83?): a member of the Californio elite, her marriage to the Boston merchant William Goodwin Dana (after his conversion to Catholicism and naturalization as a Mexican citizen) helped Dana to establish himself in California. María Josefa was the daughter of Carlos Carillo, provincial governor of Alta California, 1837-38.

sni.name: Maria Ramona Sinforosa Sanchez de Nidever
sni.description: María Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever (1812-92): the Santa Barbara-born woman who married George Nidever on February 13, 1841. The couple had four sons and two daughters. According to her husband’s account, Sinforosa looked after the Lone Woman while she lived with the Nidever family, and she may have nursed her when the Lone Woman became ill. She would have spoken to the Lone Woman in Spanish.

mhol.name: Marion Grace Hollenbach Saunders
mhol.description: Marion Grace Hollenbach Saunders (1923-98): archaeologist who was employed by the Los Angeles County Museum of Natural History. She was an assistant to Arthur Woodward on the 1940 research trip to San Nicolas Island and other research endeavors. During World War II, Hollenbach was a member of WAVE. After her marriage to Allan Saunders, whom she met while teaching at Smith College's WAVE training school, she moved to Hawaii and was involved in numerous civic activities. Ultimately, however, her career was overshadowed by that of her husband, who was a longtime professor and administrator at the University of Hawaii.

mni.name: Mark Nidever
mni.description: Mark Nidever: brother of George Nidever. Mark was born in Tennessee and accompanied George on the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition. He was killed in a skirmish with Indians.

mpa.name: Markoosie Patsang
mpa.description: Markoosie Patsang: an Inuit author. His published works include Harpoon Hunter (1970).

mda.name: Marla Daily
mda.description: Marla Daily: president of the Santa Cruz Island Foundation and editor of Islapedia, an extensive online resource on the California Channel Islands.

marlow.name: Marlow
marlow.description: Marlow: character in and narrator of Joseph Conrad's novel, Lord Jim (1900).

mkim.name: Martin Morse Kimberly
mkim.description: Martin Morse Kimberly (1826-78): a sailor, rancher, and otter hunter. Kimberly lived on Santa Cruz for a time before establishing a sheep ranch on San Nicolas Island. After a period of drought ruined that business, he established a store in Santa Barbara, which he later sold to take up otter hunting in the Pacific Northwest.

mvio.name: Martín Violín
mvio.description: Martín Violín (1840?-1914?): a Gabrielino (Tongva) whose father came from Santa Catalina Island. Violín told Fernando Librado, J. P. Harrington's Ventureño Chumash informant, about the different people who lived on the southern Channel Islands and the languages they spoke. Violín’s father, according to Violín’s half-brother Sétimo Moraga, was said to have transported people from one of the islands by canoe.

martina.name: Martina Lekte/Lekete
martina.description: Martina Lekte/Lekete (1810?-?): a Cruzeño Chumash woman who, according to J.P. Harrington informant Luisa Ygnacio, walked with Pilar, a Ventureño Chumash, to the Nidever house in 1853, when the Lone Woman was living there. They brought clams, which they gathered along the way, as a gift for the Lone Woman. Martina thought the Lone Woman spoke like a Fernandeño, the name given to Native peoples who lived at Mission San Fernando, south of Santa Barbara.

msc.name: Mary Elizabeth Scott
msc.description: Mary Elizabeth Scott (1873-1947): Scott O'Dell's mother. She was born and raised in Columbus, Ohio, and moved to southern California shortly after her marriage to Bennett Mason Scott.

hhb.name: Matilda Coley Griffing Bancroft
hhb.description: Matilda Coley Griffing Bancroft (1848-?): the second wife of Hubert Howe Bancroft, historian for whom the University of California’s library is named.

mlo.name: Maud Hart Lovelace
mlo.description: Maud Hart Lovelace (1892-1980): an author. Lovelace attended the University of Minnesota and worked as a journalist before writing fiction full-time. Lovelace's most enduring work, the beloved Betsy-Tacy series, began as bedtime stories about her own life, told to her daughter.

muhle.name: Max Uhle
muhle.description: Max Uhle (1856-1944): a German philologist and archaeologist. Uhle was curator of the Dresden Museum and conducted field research in South America for the Universities of Pennsylvania and California. His work there, including a detailed stratigraphic excavation procedure, was important to the study of pre-Columbia South America; his chronology of the region was not displaced until late in the twentieth century.

max.name: Maximus I
max.description: Maximus I: may refer to Roman Emperors Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximainus, Maximus Thrax, or even Maximus Daia. The veneration of Saint Barbara occurred after her lifetime supposedly occurred, making it difficult to link figures within her story to specific individuals.

gma.name: Maynard J. Geiger
gma.description: Maynard J. Geiger (1901-77): a Roman Catholic priest, historian, and archivist. Geiger attended St. Anthony's Seminary and the Catholic University of America. He spent four decades as the archivist of the Santa Barbara Mission and devoted his career to preserving and defending the historic California missions and mission system. Geiger's published works include The Life and Times of Fray Junipero Serra, O.F.M. (1959) and Franciscan Missionaries in Hispanic California, 1768-1848: A Biographical Dictionary (1969).

mph.name: Michael J. Phillips
mph.description: Michael J. Phillips: a short-story writer and Air Force veteran. He was the editor of the Santa Barbara Daily News for several years.

mred.name: Michael Redmon
mred.description: Michael Redmon: a librarian and author. As of 2016 Redmon was the Director of Research at the Santa Barbara Historical Museum and a contributor to the Santa Barbara Independent.

msu.name: Milton Green Sublette
msu.description: Milton Green Sublette (1800-36): mountain man and fur trader. During the 1830s he was among those who blazed trails through the northern Rocky Mountains, clearing the way for wagons to travel west on the Oregon Trail. George Nidever met Sublette and his trappers at Pierre’s Hole, Idaho.

mwa.name: Minnie Warren
mwa.description: Minnie Warren (1849-78): circus performer and signer who was born with proportionate dwarfism. She toured with P. T. Barnum.

msin.name: Miss Sinclair
msin.description: Miss Sinclair: refers to Naomi Sinclair, the wife of Henry Nidever and sister-in-law of George Nidever. They had ten or eleven children.

mon.name: Mon-a-nee
mon.description: Mon-a-nee (later Won-a-nee): the otter Karana nurses back to health in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960).
