st_az.description: Arizona: the forty-eighth state of the United States, which it joined in 1912. Its borders include the homelands of many Native people, including the Navajo and Hopi. Native resistance to land incursions were effectively suppressed by 1886, paving the way for white settlement.

re_arkriver.name: Arkansas River
re_arkriver.description: Arkansas River: the sixth largest river in the United States and a tributary of the Mississippi River. It begins near Leadville, Colorado, and goes through the Rockies and Great Plains.

st_ar.name: Arkansas
st_ar.description: Arkansas: the twenty-fifth state of the United States, which it joined in 1836. Arkansas is the home territory of the Quapaw, Osage, Caddo, and Tunica peoples. Before the Civil War, the majority of Arkansas settlers were small-scale farmers.

arling.name: Arlington
arling.description: Arlington: a luxury hotel located on State Street in Santa Barbara, California. Though the original hotel burned down in 1909, a second hotel was built in 1911, which was badly damaged by an earthquake in 1925. The hotel was later torn down and replaced by the Arlington Theater, which opened in 1931.

re_abu.name: Arroyo Burro
re_abu.description: Arroyo Burro: a six-mile stream that begins at the head of Barger Canyon and travels to the Santa Barbara Channel. It is also known as San Roque Creek.

re_ase.name: Arroyo Seco
re_ase.description: Arroyo Seco: watershed in Southern California that flows from the San Gabriel Mountains to Los Angeles. It is part of the homeland of the Gabrielino (Tongva) people.

re_arroyo.name: Arroyo Sequit
re_arroyo.description: Arroyo Sequit: stream in the Santa Monica mountains of Los Angeles County that travels towards the Pacific Ocean. Variant spellings of this place include Arroyo Sequet, Arroyo Siquet, and Arroyo Siquis.

ci_ashland.name: Ashland
ci_ashland.description: Ashland: a city located in northern Wisconsin on Lake Superior. The area is part of the Ojibwa homeland. French traders arrived around 1659 and a Jesuit mission was founded in 1665. The town of Ashland was settled in 1854 and incorporated in 1887.

re_asia.name: Asia
re_asia.description: Asia: a central destination of the maritime fur trade during the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries. Canton, China was the principal point of entry for California furs, as well as many other Pacific goods.

asucs.name: Asucsagna
asucs.description: Asucsagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) settlement located at Azuca in Los Angeles County, California. Its more common name is Azucsagna, as it was called by the Smithsonian Bureau of Ethnology.

re_agrant.name: Austin's Grant
re_agrant.description: Austin's Grant: refers to the area of land in Texas granted to Moses Austin (and later his son, Stephen). With the permission of the government of the eastern portion of the Provincias Internas, 300 families settled this area in 1821.

awigna.name: Awigna
awigna.description: Awigna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) settlement near present-day La Puente in the San Gabriel Valley of Los Angeles County, California. Its name has many possible variations, including Ahwingna, Awigana, and Awizna.

re_baja.name: Baja California
re_baja.description: Baja California (Lower California): a province of the Viceroyalty of New Spain and after Mexican independence, a territory of Mexico.  Its land included the Baja California Peninsula.  Seventeen missions to convert the indigenous people of the peninsula were founded by the Jesuits and then, in the late eighteenth century, taken over by the Dominican order. Today, Baja California refers to two states in the Republic of Mexico, Baja California Norte and Baja California Sur.

ci_bk.name: Bangkok
ci_bk.description: Bangkok: the capital and primary cultural center of Thailand located on the Chao Phraya River. In 1782, Rama I moved the royal court to the east bank of the Chao Phraya, making Bangkok the capital of the country. In 1971, the city on the east bank (Krung Thep) and the city on the west bank (Thon Buri) were united, merging into the larger city of Krung Thep Maha Nakhon (Bangkok Metropolis).

ci_ba.name: Barrow
ci_ba.description: Barrow: the northernmost city in the United States. Barrow is located on the Chukchi Sea coast of Alaska. Its traditional name is Ukpeagvik, meaning "the place where snowy owls are hunted."

re_bear.name: Bear River
re_bear.description: Bear River: the longest river in the western hemisphere never reaching the ocean. The Bear River passes through Idaho, Utah, and Wyoming and is the largest tributary to the Great Salt Lake. The original inhabitants of the River Valley were the Fremont (700-1300 CE) and Shoshone, but French-Canadian trappers arrived in the early 1800s, and settlers arrived by 1864.

re_johnrock.name: John Begg's Rock
re_johnrock.description: Begg’s Rock: a 15-foot-tall rock located eight miles northwest of San Nicolas Island. It is named after the John Begg, a ship that hit a nearby rock in 1824. Today, Begg Rock is a popular scuba diving destination.

co_bel.name: Belgium
co_bel.description: Belgium: a country in Western Europe bordered by the North Sea, France, and the Netherlands. It did not gain its independence from the Netherlands until 1830 and was invaded by Germany during World War I and II.

ci_ben.name: Benicia
ci_ben.description: Benicia: a town founded in 1847 and located in California near the Carquinez Strait. Several schools were established in Benicia during the 1850s, which led it to be referred to as the "Athens of California." It was the state capital from 1853-54.

ci_berk.name: Berkeley
ci_berk.description: Berkeley: a city on California's East Bay.  Berkeley is home to the University of California's flagship campus, one of the nation's premier universities.  In 2016, Berkeley had a population of approximately 100,000 people, nearly 40,000 of whom were enrolled in the university.

ci_ber.name: Steglitz-Berlin
ci_ber.description: Steglitz-Berlin: a neighborhood in southwest Berlin, Germany. During the Cold War, this neighborhood was part of West Germany.

re_bigdog.name: Big Dog Cave
re_bigdog.description: Big Dog Cave: the site of important archaeological discoveries on San Clemente Island, including the remains of human and animal burials.

ci_belle.name: Billerica
ci_belle.description: Billerica: a town twenty-four miles northwest of Boston. Until the 1850s, the town had approximately 10,000 residents and an agriculturally-based economy.

biltmore.name: Biltmore
biltmore.description: Biltmore: a luxury hotel in Santa Barbara, California, that opened in 1927. Originally part of the Biltmore Hotels group, it is now owned by the Four Seasons. The Coral Casino, an elite social club, was founded at the hotel in 1937.

blcave.name: Black Cave
blcave.description: Black Cave: a fictional cave on the Island of the Blue Dolphins in Scott O'Dell's 1960 novel. Here, Karana discovers the remains of her ancestors, though the significance of this place in her people's history has been forgotten.

re_bp.name: Black Point
re_bp.description: Black Point: the headquarters for the commandant of nearby Fort Point located in the San Francisco Bay area. Today, it is incorporated with the area of Green Point and remains a relatively rural area, with a population of 1,306 in 2010.

ci_bodega.name: Bodega
ci_bodega.description: Bodega: area located in Sonoma County, California. The Bodega Bay area was first inhabited by the Miwok and Pomo peoples. Russian fur traders, who constructed Fort Ross twenty-four miles north of the bay, were the first European settlers in the region.

ci_bo.name: Boston
ci_bo.description: Boston: Massachusetts city founded in 1630. Due to its deep harbor and convenient location, it became a large center of sea-based commerce, becoming the richest and largest city in the American colonies. The New England port was so central to the Northwest Trade of the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries, in which otter pelts loomed large, that in parts of the Pacific Northwest, all American seamen, regardless of their home ports, became known as "Boston men."

re_botanybay.name: Botany Bay
re_botanybay.description: Botany Bay: located in Sydney, Australia, and originally named Stingray Harbour by James Cook in 1770. The British planned to use this site as their first penal colony to exile prisoners.

branch.name: Branch's Ranch
branch.description: Branch's Ranch: 17,000-acre ranch located eight miles from Pismo Beach in the central coast area of California. The ranch belonged to Francis Ziba Branch, a sea otter hunter who requested a land grant from the Mexican government in 1837.

bridgeport.name: Bridgeport
bridgeport.description: Bridgeport, Connecticut: a city located in Connecticut on the Long Island Sound. Traditionally occupied by the Pequot, the area was first settled by Europeans in 1639. Bridgeport was incorporated as a city in 1836. P. T. Barnum, the famous showman, was a resident of Bridgeport and served as its mayor from 1875-76.

re_britline.name: British line
re_britline.description: British line: refers to the international boundary line that divides the continental United States and Canada. Historically, this line was extremely contentious (particularly in the western part of the United States) because many Americans questioned the legitimacy of British surveying methods. This resulted in land disputes which were settled by treaties.

i_buenport.name: Bueno Porto Island
i_buenport.description: Bueno Porto Island: refers to Santa Catarina Island off the coast of Brazil. Bueno Porto was a creek on the island in which ships could anchor to find respite from heavy winds. The explorer Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo had a shelter here.

co_bunc.name: Buncombe County
co_bunc.description: Buncombe County: a county in western North Carolina; Asheville is the county seat. Although originally the home of the Cherokee, settlers named it after Revolutionary War hero Col. Edward Buncombe. Established in 1792, the county's boundaries included most of western North Carolina, where about 1,000 people lived. By 1850, its economy was mostly based on the drover trade (driving animals from the West to market). Today, the county is a major tourist center due to its location in the Appalachian Mountains.

ci_burtons.name: Burton Mound
ci_burtons.description: Burton Mound: originally a vibrant Chumash village, the area became part of Mission Santa Barbara during the Spanish period. Following secularization of the missions, the land was eventually sold to Joseph Chapman, who built an adobe that was occupied by George Nidever and his family during the 1840s. 1851, Nidever sold the home to Augustus F. Hinchman, who in turn sold it to Lewis T. Burton, whose name became associated with the site. During the early twentieth century, a hotel was built on the property, but it burned to the ground in 1921. At this point, archaeological excavation was conducted in the area under the direction of John P. Harrington. Today, a plaque notes marks Burton Mound as California Registered Historical Landmark No. 306.

bushst.name: Bush Street
bushst.description: Bush Street: a street in San Francisco, California where a musical hall and theater were located. At this musical hall, the Lone Woman's dress was displayed as part of a curiosity exhibit in 1853. A fire erupted on the street in 1877 but did not cause much damage.

co_butte.name: Butte County
co_butte.description: Butte County: a county in northern California that was part of the homeland of the Maidu. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after Marysville or Sutter Buttes, which were within the county boundaries when established. The county seat is located in Oroville, and the county had a population of 224,301 in 2016.

cabeug.name: Cabeugna
cabeug.description: Cabeugna: a former Tongva (Gabrielino) village located near the San Fernando Mission in California (its precise location is unknown). Popular variations of the name include Cahuenga.

ci_cah.name: Cahuenga
ci_cah.description: Cahuenga: a former Tongva (Gabrielino) village located near the San Fernando Mission in California (its precise location is unknown). Popular variations of the name include Cabuenga.

cairerancho.name: Caire rancho
cairerancho.description: Caire rancho: extensive ranch on Santa Cruz Island named after Justinian Caire, who acquired all the holdings of the Santa Cruz Island Company in the 1880s. Caire built up the Company's holdings and diversified operations to include ranching as well as carpentry, blacksmithing, and construction facilities. Until the late 1930s, the Caire family owned the island and continued ranching operations.

co_calaveras.name: Calaveras County
co_calaveras.description: Calaveras County: a county in Northern California and the homeland of the Miwok. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850 and is named after the Calaveras River, which an explorer reportedly named due to the large quantity of Indian skulls found on the river’s banks. The county seat is located in San Andreas, and the county had a population of 44,881 in 2016.

ci_cal.name: Calcutta
ci_cal.description: Calcutta: a city in western India now known as Kolkata. Calcutta, which is an Anglicized form of the Bengali word Kalikata, was the capital of British India from 1772 to 1911. Today, Kolkata is the capital of West Bengal and one of the most populated and overcrowded cities in India.

i_chi.name: California Channel Islands
i_chi.description: California Channel Islands: eight islands that lie west of the California coast around Santa Barbara, Ventura, and Los Angeles. The Channel Islands (Anacapa, San Clemente, San Miguel, San Nicolas, Santa Barbara, Santa Catalina, Santa Cruz, and Santa Rosa) were home to the Chumash (northern islands) and Gabrielino (Tongva) (southern islands) peoples for thousands of years before the arrival of Europeans. After the establishment of the Spanish mission system, the Channel Islands were gradually depopulated, both as a result of Spanish removal policies and due to migration following the disruption of traditional lifeways. The islands were variously used for sheep grazing, cattle ranching, and commercial and recreational fishing. William Wrigley, Jr., of chewing gum fame, also developed Santa Catalina as a destination for tourists in the 1920s. Today, San Nicolas and San Clemente Islands are controlled by the US Navy, Santa Catalina, which has a resident population of approximately 4,000, is largely administered by the Catalina Nature Conservancy, and the other five islands are protected as part of the Channel Islands National Park.

re_caldesert.name: California Desert
re_caldesert.description: California Desert: a desert area in the territory of the Mojaves, Chemehuevis, and Quechans, who occupied the land until about 1868. At this point, the desert land filled with military bases, utility roads and lines, ranches, and mining sites. In 1976, a 25-million-acre region of the California Desert was designated by Congress as the California Desert Conservation Area.

st_cal.name: California
st_cal.description: California: the thirty-first state to join the union, on September 9, 1850. The land within its borders was annexed from Mexico after the Mexican-American War. When California became a US state, slavery was prohibited as part of the Compromise of 1850. Today, California has the largest population of any state in the union.

ci_callao.name: Callao
ci_callao.description: Callao: a port city on the coast of Peru. Founded in 1537 by Francisco Pizarro, it was also the site of Spain’s 1826 surrender in the Peruvian War of Independence. Callao was a popular stop for ships engaged in the China trade in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries; most of the cargo taken aboard consisted of gold and silver seized from the Inca Empire by Spanish conquistadores.

re_chorn.name: Cape Horn
re_chorn.description: Cape Horn: the southernmost point of South America, located at the tip of the Isla Hornos off Chile. "Rounding the Horn" by ship is both difficult and dangerous, requiring great skill.

ci_car.name: Carpinteria
ci_car.description: Carpinteria: a city that, during the pre-contact period, was home to the Chumash and an active trading site. In 1769, members of the Portolà expedition, the first European exploration of what is now California, noted the native population's use of redwood canoes and called the place a "carpentry" shop.

olive.name: Casa de Olive
olive.description: Casa de Olive: the home of Carl Dittman, located on State Street in Santa Barbara, California.  Dittman bought the house in 1850 from the estate of a ship captain, James Scott.

re_caveofwhales.name: Cave of the Whales
re_caveofwhales.description: Cave of the Whales: sea cave on the south coast of San Nicolas Island named for the petroglyphs, or cave paintings, found inside. Some figures prominently feature the kind of dorsal fins found on whales or sharks.

i_cedros.name: Cedros Island
i_cedros.description: Cedros Island: an island in the Pacific Ocean approximately fourteen miles from Baja California. Also known as Cerros Island, the island was originally occupied by Cochimí-speaking people. The Spanish first visited the island in 1539 and transported the Natives to the mainland of Baja California in 1732. Today the island is home to a fishing village established in the 1920s.

re_ceam.name: Central America
re_ceam.description: Central America: the region between Mexico and South America consisting of Panama, Costa Rica, Nicaragua, Honduras, El Salvador, Guatemala, and Belize. The Mayan civilization flourished in the region from 250 to 900 CE. The Spanish first arrived in 1502, when Christopher Columbus sailed near Panama. The Spaniards colonized Central America by splitting it into viceroyalties, and many Native peoples died due to disease and labor exploitation. The colonial period ended in 1821, when Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Costa Rica, and Nicaragua all became independent nations. In the twentieth century, the region was politically dominated by dictators, and its economy was increasingly reliant on exporting coffee and bananas.

i_cerros.name: Cerros Island
i_cerros.description: Cerros Island: an island in the Pacific Ocean approximately fourteen miles from Baja California. Also known as Cedros Island, the island was originally occupied by Cochimí-speaking people. The Spanish first visited the island in 1539 and transported the Natives to the mainland of Baja California in 1732. Today the island is home to a fishing village established in the 1920s.

ci_chester.name: Chester
ci_chester.description: Chester: a city in Pennsylvania located between Philadelphia and Wilmington, Delaware. Originally settled by Europeans in 1645, it is the oldest town in Pennsylvania. Due to its location on the Delaware River, there were five shipyards nearby, including the one in which the Santa Rosa steamer was built.

chetko.name: Chetko
chetko.description: Chetko: likely refers to the Chetco River, a river in Curry County, Oregon. It begins in the Kalmiopsis Wilderness area of the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest and travels 55.5 miles before emptying into the Pacific Ocean between the towns of Harbor and Brookings.

st_chi.name: Chiapas
st_chi.description: Chiapas: a state in southern Mexico that borders Guatemala. Chiapas contains a large population of indigenous peoples, most notably those of Mayan descent. Although Chiapas was connected with Guatemala during Spanish colonization, it became a state of Mexico in 1824. Due to the socioeconomic inequality in the region, a large armed uprising began in 1994 and lasted into the twenty-first century.

co_china.name: China
co_china.description: China: one of the largest nations in the world, occupying the greater part of East Asia and 3,705,405 square miles of land. China also has one of oldest records of continuous civilization, dating back 4,000 years. Technologies linked to Chinese civilization include silk production, which began as early as 1300 BCE, and a lunisolar calendar system dating to 500 BCE. In the thirteenth century, the Venetian Marco Polo traveled across China on the Silk Road, establishing a relationship with the Mongol leader Kublai Khan. Through his writings, Marco Polo sparked Europe’s interest in the region. However, it was not until the nineteenth century that Chinese interaction with Europe increased as Western military powers forced the nation to open its doors to trade and to Christian missionaries. China became a central destination for maritime furs and abalone meat from California.

re_chineseharbor.name: Chinese Harbor
re_chineseharbor.description: Chinese Harbor: a stretch of sand that lies on the northwest coast of San Nicolas Island. Its name stems from its use by nineteenth-century Chinese abalone harvesters.  It is also called Corral (or Coral) Harbor.

chokis.name: Chokisgna
chokis.description: Chokisgna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Bell Gardens, California. Also known as Chibugna, it is mentioned in the San Fernando Mission registry.

chowigna.name: Chowigna
chowigna.description: Chowigna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Palos Verdes, California. Human occupation dates back at least 8,000 years.

re_cim.name: The Cimmeron
re_cim.description: Cimarron: a tributary of the Gunnison River in Colorado. The Cimarron flows through Montrose and Gunnison counties before reaching the Gunnison River at Black Canyon in Gunnison National Park.

ci_cla.name: Claremont
ci_cla.description: Claremont: a city on the eastern edge of Los Angeles County, California, it is part of the homeland of the Serrano people. Much of the city’s land was incorporated into the San Gabriel Mission in the late eighteenth century, and became the site of Rancho San Jose in the nineteenth century. By the early twentieth century, Claremont saw the establishment of several prestigious colleges, collectively known as the Claremont Colleges, and a growing citrus industry that included four citrus packing and distribution houses.

re_ccreek.name: Clear Creek
re_ccreek.description: Clear Creek: refers to either Clear Creek, a 5.25-mile-long tributary of the Steely Fork Cosumnes River in El Dorado County, California, or Clear Creek, an eight-mile-long tributary of the Cosumnes River’s north fork in El Dorado County, California.

re_coastrange.name: Coast Ranges
re_coastrange.description: Coast Ranges: mountains that extend along two-thirds of California’s coast, from Humboldt County in the north to Santa Barbara County in the south. The northern and southern ranges are divided by San Francisco Bay, with the tallest mountains located in the southern region.

re_coche.name: Coche Prietos
re_coche.description: Coches Prietos: an anchorage on the south-central coast of Santa Cruz Island. It was the site of the largest Chumash village on Santa Cruz, Liyam. Now encompassed by the Channel Islands National Park, Coches Prietos is a popular access point for visitors because of its wide, calm beach and proximity to hiking trails.

i_qui.name: Coiba Island
i_qui.description: Coiba Island: a Panamanian island that is part of an underwater mountain chain located fifteen miles from the mainland. Until 2005, the island was the site of a prison infamous for human rights abuses. The island is now a UNESCO World Heritage site and part of Coiba National Park

st_co.name: Colorado
st_co.description: Colorado: the thirty-eighth state of the United States, which it officially joined on August 1, 1876. Colorado is the home of the Apache, Arapaho, Cheyenne, Pueblo, Shoshone, and Ute people. Today, its primary industries are finance, manufacturing, agriculture, mining, tourism, and oil production.

ci_colton.name: Colton
ci_colton.description: Colton: a city in San Bernardino County, California. Originally the home of the Guachama, Seranno, and San Gorgonio people, Colton became part of the San Bernardino and Jurupa ranchos in the early 1800s. The town was officially founded in 1875, when the Southern Pacific Railway came through the area.

co_colusi.name: Colusi County
co_colusi.description: Colusi County: a variant spelling of northern California’s Colusa County. Originally the homeland of the Wintun, it was incorporated on February 18, 1850 and is named after Native peoples that lived west of the Sacramento River. The county seat is located in the city of Colusa, and the county had a population of 22,312 in 2016.

st_ct.name: Connecticut
st_ct.description: Connecticut: one of the thirteen original colonies, it became the fifth state to join the Union on January 9, 1788. Connecticut is the third smallest state and is the homeland of many Algonquian-speaking peoples, including the Mohegan, Niantic, Nipmuc, Pequot, Paugussett, Quinnipiac, Saukiog, Siwanog, Tunxis, Uncas, and Wangunk. Today, the state’s primary industries are based in health services, technology manufacturing, and tourism.

consthall.name: Constitution Hall
consthall.description: Constitution Hall: a performance hall in Washington, DC, built by the Daughters of the American Revolution (DAR) in 1929. The hall served as a cultural center for decades, hosting the National Symphony and the National Geographic Film Series. The hall was the center of racial strife in 1939, when the DAR refused to allow African-American opera singer Marion Anderson to perform there.

co_ccosta.name: Contra Costa County
co_ccosta.description: Contra Costa County: a county in northern California that encompasses the homeland of the Miwoks, Yokuts, and Ohlone. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850 and is named because it situated opposite San Francisco on the San Francisco Bay. The county seat is located in Martinez, and the county had a population of 1,116,385 in 2016.

re_coqforks.name: Coquille Forks
re_coqforks.description: Coquille Forks: likely refers to the South Fork of the Coquille River, which is in Coos County, Oregon. The South Fork is home to large populations of spawning salmon and steelhead and the longest tributary of the Coquille River.

re_coqriv.name: Coquille River
re_coqriv.description: Coquille River: a river mostly located in Coos County, Oregon, which is the largest river system in the state with a 1,059 square mile watershed. As of 2016, this area consists of approximately 16,800 people who reside within neighboring towns such as Bandon, Coquille, Myrtle Point, and Powers. Descendants of Native peoples who long occupied the Coquille River basin are federally recognized as the Coquille Indian Tribe.

re_coralharbor.name: Corral Harbor
re_coralharbor.description: Corral Harbor: a stretch of sand that lies on the northwest coast of San Nicolas Island, but is not a true harbor. Its name stems from its use as a corral when sheep were grazed on the island. It is also called Coral Harbor or, because it was used by abalone fishermen, Chinese Harbor.

re_cortezshoal.name: Cortez' Shoal
re_cortezshoal.description: Cortes Bank: an underwater mountain range 40 miles southwest of San Clemente Island, California. The region is known for being difficult to navigate by ship and for producing some of the world’s largest surfable waves.

cripcre.name: Cripple Creek
cripcre.description: Cripple Creek: a city in Teller County, Colorado. The town was formed, in Ute territory, after gold was discovered by Robert Womack and Scott Stratton in 1891. After 1920, gold production decreased, and Cripple Creek became almost a ghost town. The town was revitalized by the legalization of gambling in 1991 and serves as a tourist destination today.

re_ctim.name: Cross Timbers
re_ctim.description: Cross Timbers: a region in Oklahoma, Kansas, and Texas comprised of prairies, woodlands, and forests. The region, which settlers passed through as they traveled west, is the traditional boundary between the hunting grounds of the East Texas and Plains Indians.

cucomogna.name: Cucomogna
cucomogna.description: Cucomogna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located in the San Gabriel Valley of Los Angeles County, California, although its precise location is unknown. Its name has also been recorded as Cucamonga, Cucumogna, and Cucumonga.

re_cuylerharbor.name: Cuyler's Harbor
re_cuylerharbor.description: Cuyler’s Harbor: a mile-wide bay on the northeast side of San Miguel Island. It is the safest place to anchor on San Miguel, and it is believed to be the place where Spanish explorer Juan Cabrillo is buried.

guerrahouse.name: de la Guerra house
guerrahouse.description: de la Guerra house: the home of the family of Don Jose de la Guerra, who was the military commander of Santa Barbara from 1817 to 1842 and owned over 500,000 acres of land in California. The building, located on 15 East De la Guerra Street, was considered the center of social activity during de la Guerra’s lifetime.

selaguerra.name: de la Guerra street
selaguerra.description: de la Guerra Street: a street in Santa Barbara named after Don Jose de la Guerra, who was the military commander of Santa Barbara from 1817 to 1842 and who owned more than 500,000 acres of land in California.

i_dmi.name: Deadman's Island
i_dmi.description: Deadman's Island: a small, rocky island at the entrance to San Pedro Bay on the coast of southern California. By 1830 the tiny piece of land had gained its name from its use as a cemetery; by 1858 it had at least eleven shallow graves. Among those buried here was Black Hawk, the last male Nicoleño Indian, found with three women companions by missionaries on the island of San Nicolas. He and two of the women were taken to the mainland but the third, supposedly the Lone Woman, ran away and was left on the island. To contribute to San Pedro’s harbor infrastructure, a long rock jetty was built between Deadman’s Island and nearby Rattlesnake Island to carve out a deep channel for ships between the island and the mainland. The breakwater caused erosion of much of the island, exposing coffins that then had to be relocated. In 1928, the island was deconstructed and the resulting material added to Rattlesnake Island, which is now called Terminal Island.

ci_dm.name: Des Moines
ci_dm.description: Des Moines: the capital city of Iowa, located on the Des Moines River. Part of the homeland of the Sauk and Fox, Des Moines’ first European settlers arrived in 1843, when Fort Des Moines was established. In the early twentieth century, Des Moines grew rapidly due to the coal industry. In 2010, 569,633 people lived in Des Moines and the surrounding metropolitan area.

brinkhouse.name: Dr. Brinkerhoff's house
brinkhouse.description: Dr. Brinkerhoff's house: according to the 1860 US Census, the New York-born physician Dr. Samuel Bevier Brinkerhoff was living in the same Santa Barbara neighborhood as Isaac Sparks and Lewis T. Burton: midtown, on or near State Street. It is uncertain where he lived in 1853, the year of the Lone Woman’s arrival in the city. Brinkerhoff does not appear in the 1852 California State Census, but it seems likely that he was in the city as a local history reports that he arrived in Santa Barbara on the Ohio in July of that year.

re_drakesbay.name: Drakes Bay
re_drakesbay.description: Drakes Bay: a bay on the Point Reyes National Seashore in California, in Coast Miwok territory. This bay was deemed a National Historic Landmark in 2012 because it is most likely the spot of Sir Francis Drake's California landing during his circumnavigation of the globe, making it the site of the earliest documented contact between Europeans and the Native people of California.

re_dutchharbor.name: Dutch Harbor
re_dutchharbor.description: Dutch Harbor: a bay located on the southeastern end of California's San Nicolas Island between the South Range Marker and Bent Pole.

re_etenn.name: East Tennessee
re_etenn.description: East Tennessee: a region comprised of the eastern third of the state's counties. The area was designated as East Tennessee in 1806, when surveyor’s districts were created in the state. Eastern Tennessee is part of the Appalachian region and is home to the Great Smoky Mountains National Park.

re_easternmed.name: Eastern Levant
re_easternmed.description: Eastern Levant: a region of Southwest Asia in the eastern end of the Mediterranean Sea, bordered to the north by the Taurus Mountains and to the east by the Syrian desert. The Levant is said to have a climate similar to that of Southern California, with warm, dry summers and mild, rainy winters.

co_ec.name: Ecuador
co_ec.description: Ecuador: a country in South America bordered by Colombia and Peru. It was part of the Inca Empire until 1533, when Spanish conquistadors arrived.

co_egy.name: Egypt
co_egy.description: Egypt: a country in northeastern Africa. Humans settled along the Nile River beginning in approximately 7000 BCE, producing a thriving culture. From 669 BCE to 642 CE, Egypt was conquered by the Assyrians, Persians, Macedonians, Romans, and Arabs, eventually becoming part of the Ottoman Empire in 1517. The British occupied Egypt starting in 1882, and although Egypt became an independent country in 1922, Britain remained influential until the mid-1950s. After the Arab Spring protests began in 2010, Egypt entered a state of ongoing political turbulence.

ci_elcajon.name: El Cajon
ci_elcajon.description: El Cajon: a city in southern California, near San Diego. The Spanish Mission Basilica San Diego de Acalá was built here, on Kumeyaay land, in 1769. After Mexican independence, the land was given as a governmental land grant to Dona Maria Antonio Estudillo, in 1845. An American settlement was established in the mid-nineteenth century, and the city was incorporated in 1912. In 2017, it had a population of 102,000.

co_eldor.name: El Dorado County
co_eldor.description: El Dorado County: a county in northern California that encompasses the homeland of the Southern Maidu (Nisenan) and Miwok people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850 and is named after the mythical city of El Dorado. The county seat is located in the city of Placerville, and the county had a population of 182,917 in 2016.

teckolote.name: El Teckolote
teckolote.description: El Teckolote: Spanish name for Helapoonuch, a former Chumash village approximately fifteen miles from the Santa Barbara Mission.

re_elkriv.name: Elk River
re_elkriv.description: Elk River: a river in Curry County, Oregon. It begins at the Grassy Knob Wilderness and Copper-Salmon Wilderness and travels for 19 miles before emptying into the Pacific Ocean between Port Orford and Cape Blanco. The river is home to large salmon and steelhead populations.

empland.name: Empire Landing
empland.description: Empire Landing: a beach on Santa Catalina Island. In the early twentieth century, a commercial quarry known for its steatite and serpentine was established there. As of 2015, the beach at Empire Landing is privately owned and closed to the public.

ci_englewood.name: Englewood
ci_englewood.description: Englewood: a city located in Bergen County in the northeast corner of New Jersey.

ci_eureka.name: Eureka
ci_eureka.description: Eureka: a city located on the coast of Humbolt Bay, north of San Francisco. Eureka was founded in 1850, just a few years before the Lone Woman was brought to Santa Barbara, California.

re_europe.name: Europe
re_europe.description: Europe: a continent occupying the western part of Eurasia bordered by the Arctic Ocean, the Atlantic Ocean, the Mediterranean Sea, the Black Sea, and the Caspian Sea. European civilization began in Crete during the Bronze Age with the Minoan civilization, followed by the Mycenaean civilization. The classical civilizations of ancient Greece and Rome followed. By 500 CE, after the Roman Empire's decline, a period of relative economic and cultural stagnation lasted until the Renaissance, which began in the fourteenth century. In the fifteenth century, European voyagers explored and began colonizing the Americas, Africa, and parts of Asia, establishing new world empires that collapsed only after World War II. The European Union was established in 1993.

i_far.name: Farallon Islands (Farallones)
i_far.description: Farallon Islands: a group of islands off the coast of San Francisco, California. In 1810, seal hunters began visiting the islands, and the Russian hunters did not leave until 1841, when seal hunting was no longer profitable in the region. During the Gold Rush, the island was used for egg-gathering; employees of the Farallones Egg Company stole the eggs of seabirds and sold them to miners. The North and Middle Farallones were declared a wildlife refuge in 1906, and the South Farallones became part of the refuge in 1969.

re_feather.name: Feather River
re_feather.description: Feather River: a tributary of the Sacramento River in California. It begins at the Oroville Dam at Lake Oroville, and runs through Sutter, Yuba, and Butte counties to the Sacramento River. The Spanish likely named it "Rio de las Plumas" because the local people often decorated themselves with feathers.

re_fivemile.name: Five-mile Bight
re_fivemile.description: Five-mile Bight: a bay on the northern end of Santa Rosa Island (now part of Channel Islands National Park in California). It serves as an anchorage for ships and is more commonly known as Bechers Bay.

ci_flo.name: Florence
ci_flo.description: Florence, Italy: a city in Italy. Founded in 59 BCE by Julius Caesar as a retirement location for his soldiers, Florence is now one of the most famous cities in the world. It is considered the birthplace of the Italian Renaissance and is home to Brunelleschi’s Duomo, Michelangelo’s David, and the Ponte Vecchio. Scott O'Dell wrote his first novel here.

re_flcreek.name: Flores Creek
re_flcreek.description: Flores Creek: likely refers to Floras Creek, a relatively unknown stream in Curry County, Oregon, that flows through the town of Langlois and empties into Floras Lake. Floras is home to large salmon and steelhead populations.

re_forneyscove.name: Forney's Cove
re_forneyscove.description: Forney Cove: a bay on the western tip of Santa Cruz Island, California.  The cove is named after Stehman Forney, a Civil War veteran who later worked for the Coast and Geodetic Survey.

ci_cbow.name: Camp Bowie
ci_cbow.description: Fort Bowie: a former military outpost established in 1862 after the Battle of Apache Pass, in which a regiment of the US Army was ambushed by a band of Chiricahua Apache. After Geronimo surrendered in 1886 and the Chiricahua Apache were driven from their homeland to Florida and Alabama, the fort was used as a traveler’s stop until it was completely abandoned in 1894. The fort became a National Historic site in 1964.

fortpoint.name: Fort Point
fortpoint.description: Fort Point: a fort located at the southern end of the Golden Gate at the entrance to San Francisco Bay in California. Fort Point was built in the traditional Third System military style between 1853 and 1861 by the US Army Engineers to protect the San Francisco Bay after the California Gold Rush sparked an influx of people and commerce to the area. The fort replaced the Castillo de San Joquan, an adobe structure housing several cannons that was built by the Spanish in 1794 and abandoned soon after Mexico won its independence from Spain in 1821.

fortross.name: Fort Ross
fortross.description: Fort Ross: a Russian settlement in northern California (Sonoma County) that operated between 1812-1841. Its primary purpose was to raise food and other essentials for use by the Russian American Company, headquartered in Sitka, Alaska. Under direction from the RAC chief manager Alexander Baranov, Ivan Aleksandrovich Kuskov founded and managed Fort Ross until 1821; he brought with him a workforce of Russians, creoles, and Native Alaskans. It subsequently became a meeting place of cultures as Russians, diverse Alaska Native peoples (e.g., Aleuts, Kodiak Aluttiq), California Natives including Kashaya Pomo and Coast Miwok, and some peoples from Hawaii and Siberia interacted at the fort and within the surrounding Russian colony.

ci_fortsmith.name: Fort Smith
ci_fortsmith.description: Fort Smith: the second-largest city in Arkansas (pop. 86,200 in 2010). Fort Smith was established as a military outpost by the United States Army in 1817 and by the 1830s had become a hub for trade and communication. It was incorporated as a town in 1842.

fortsumner.name: Fort Sumner
fortsumner.description: Fort Sumner: headquarters of the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation, a million acres southeast of Santa Rosa, New Mexico. The reservation housed the Mescalero Apache and the Navajo, but both groups were eventually allowed to return to their homelands: the Mescalero Apache left the reservation in 1865, and the Navajo after the signing of the Treaty of 1868.

fyuma.name: Fort Yuma
fyuma.description: Fort Yuma: a fort located in Winterhaven, California, along the Colorado River. Built in 1849, the fort was first called Camp Calhoun and used for US military purposes. In 1884, Fort Yuma became an Indian reservation primarily housing members of the Quechan tribe. Today, it remains part of the Fort Yuma Indian Reservation, which spans parts of Imperial County, California, and Yuma County, Arizona.

co_france.name: France
co_france.description: France: a country in northwestern Europe bordered by the Alps, the Pyrenees, the Mediterranean Sea, and the Atlantic Ocean. France built a New World empire during the Age of Discovery and was a major center of the Enlightenment during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. France was an ally of the United States and Great Britain during World Wars I and II. The country remains an important part of world politics as a leading member of the European Union and one of the five permanent members of the United Nations Security Council.

re_rivera.name: French Riviera
re_rivera.description: French Riviera: a Mediterranean coastal region between France and Italy. Also known as Côte d’Azur, the Riviera is known for its mild climate, which allows tropical fruits and flowers to grow, and for its abundant sunlight, which has attracted many great modern painters to the region (e.g., Picasso, Matisse, Monet, van Gogh). The Riviera is also known as a center of the perfume industry.

thefresno.name: Fresno River
thefresno.description: Fresno River: a tributary of the San Joaquin River in California. It begins in the Sierra Nevada and travels through Madera and Merced counties before joining the San Joaquin River. It has also been known as Fresno Creek, Frezno River, and Rio Fresno.

re_gaviotapass.name: Gaviota Pass
re_gaviotapass.description: Gaviota Pass: a gap in the coastal mountain range northwest of Santa Barbara, California. It was named by the Spanish and used frequently after the establishment of Santa Ynez Mission. The path was widened for wagons as traffic increased in the 1850s, when American settlers traveled west via the route. Today, a concrete overpass spans the gap.

nideverhouse.name: George Nidever's house
nideverhouse.description: George Nidever’s house: an adobe built near the center of four lots Nidever purchased in the eastern section of Santa Barbara. The Nidever family moved into this home in 1852, and the Lone Woman was brought to it in 1853; she lived there for seven weeks. Nidever sold the land on which the adobe stood in 1880, and today the lot is covered by the 101 Freeway.

co_ger.name: Germany
co_ger.description: Germany: a country in north-central Europe. Although many Germanic-speaking groups lived in the area for millennia, Germany became a unified country in 1871, when the German Empire was formed due to the work of Prussian Prime Minister Otto von Bismarck. The German Empire was dismantled after the Central Powers’ defeat in World War I, and economic and political unrest led to the rise of Nazism. Germany was utterly defeated in World War II, and the country was split into two parts (East Germany and West Germany) until 1989-90. Germany has recovered financially and is now a leading economic power in the European Union.

ci_gil.name: Gilroy
ci_gil.description: Gilroy: a city in California’s Santa Clara Valley. Gilroy was originally part of the homeland of the Ohlone people, who were forcibly relocated to Mission Santa Cruz and Mission San Juan Bautista in the 1790s. After the Mexican-American War and during the California Gold Rush, American settlers moved to Gilroy and, in 1868, the town was incorporated. Today, Gilroy is known for hosting the Gilroy Garlic Festival.

re_gilranch.name: Gilroy's Ranch
re_gilranch.description: Gilroy's Ranch: John Gilroy's portion of Ygnacio Ortega's Rancho San Ysidro, in Santa Clara County, California, which was officially patented to him in 1867. When Ortega died in 1833, his lands were divided among his three children and their spouses. Ortega’s son-in-law, Scotsman John Gilroy, had arrived in Monterey in 1814, converted to Catholicism, and become a Mexican citizen before marrying one of Ortega's daughters. He is considered the first English-speaking resident of California, and the city of Gilroy, California, located two miles west of the ranch, was named after him.

re_thegoldengate.name: Golden Gate
re_thegoldengate.description: Golden Gate: the strait connecting the Pacific Ocean and San Francisco Bay on the United States coastline. Although it was likely seen by Sir Francis Drake in 1575, Francisco de Ortega is credited with discovering it in 1769. Today, the strait is spanned by the Golden Gate Bridge.

ci_goleta.name: Goleta Estero
ci_goleta.description: Goleta: a city just west of Santa Barbara, Goleta is part of the Chumash homeland. In the late nineteenth century, the Johnston Fruit Company pioneered commercial agriculture in California by planting 3,000 lemon trees on La Patera Ranch in the Goleta Valley. La Patera shared their disease-resistant variety of lemon with other ranches, allowing the citrus industry in Goleta to grow rapidly. In the early and mid-twentieth century, over 2,000 acres of Goleta lemons were being cultivated and shipped out of California by train annually.

ci_gr.name: Grand Rapids
ci_gr.description: Grand Rapids: a city located along the Grand River of western Michigan. Part of the Ottawa Indians homeland, the Grand Rapids area was first settled by French fur traders in 1826. After the Philadelphia Centennial Exposition (1876), Grand Rapids was known as America’s Furniture Capital.

re_greatsaltlake.name: Great Salt Lake
re_greatsaltlake.description: Great Salt Lake: a saltwater lake located in Utah. It is usually three to five times saltier than ocean water and is a remnant of Lake Bonneville, which formed in the Pleistocene Epoch (around 30,000 years ago). Although explorers and Indian tales had mentioned the lake previously, white settlers officially came upon the lake in 1824-1825.

co_greece.name: Greece
co_greece.description: Greece: the southernmost country in the Balkan Peninsula of Europe. Greece is comprised of over 2,000 islands, and approximately one-third of its population lives in and around the nation’s largest city and capital, Athens. Greece was the home of a thriving Classical culture, part of the Byzantine Empire, and under Ottoman Turkish rule for nearly four centuries. Greece gained independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1830. It was invaded and occupied by Italy and Nazi Germany in 1940-1944. Since 1974, Greece has been a democratic country. It joined the E.E.C. (now the European Union) in 1981.

re_green.name: Green River
re_green.description: Green River: a river that begins in the Wind River Mountains of Wyoming and travels 730 miles, flowing through parts of Wyoming, Colorado, and Utah. The Green River is a tributary of the Colorado River, which it joins in Canyonlands National Park. Before the arrival of Europeans, both the Ute and the Shoshone people lived along the Green River.

ci_gua.name: Gua
ci_gua.description: Gua: a Chumash village, also called "Quannegua," listed in the notes of the Cabrillo exploration of the United States' western coastline (1542-43) and identified by Alfred Kroeber (1925).

ci_guat.name: Guatemala City
ci_guat.description: Guatemala City: the capital of Guatemala and the largest city in Central America. It was founded in 1776 to replace Antigua Guatemala, which was almost completely destroyed by an earthquake in 1773. Earthquakes shook the area again in 1917-18, which resulted in the need for massive rebuilding. Today, the city serves as both the political and cultural center of the country, housing the San Carlos University of Guatemala, the Society of Geography and History, and the National Palace.

co_guat.name: Guatemala
co_guat.description: Guatemala: a Central American country that is bordered by Mexico, Belize, Honduras, and El Salvador. During the first millennium CE, the area of present-day Guatemala was the center of the Mayan empire.  The region was conquered by Spain in the sixteenth century and won its independence in 1821. In 1954, a 36-year civil war began and resulted in the death of more than 200,000 people; another 1 million became refugees before a United Nations-negotiated peace agreement was signed by the warring factions in 1996. Guatemala has enjoyed economic growth in the twenty-first century.

ci_guaymas.name: Guaymas
ci_guaymas.description: Guayamas: a city in Mexico located 240 miles south of the US border. It mostly serves as an industrial port.

re_gulf.name: Gulf of California
re_gulf.description: Gulf of California: an inlet of the Pacific Ocean located alongside Mexico’s northwestern coast between the Mexican state of Sonora and the lower California Peninsula. It is also called the Sea of Cortes and the Vermillion Sea and is approximately 62,000 square miles in size.

ci_gunn.name: Gunnison County
ci_gunn.description: Gunnison County: a county located in west-central Colorado named for explorer John Williams Gunnison. The area is part of Ute territory, and Europeans did not arrive until the early 1800s, when French fur trappers entered the region. Established in 1877, today 78% of the county’s land is owned by the federal government through the United States Forest Service, the Bureau of Land Management, and the Curecanti National Recreation Area.

gutierrez.name: Gutierrez Street
gutierrez.description: Gutierrez Street: a street in Santa Barbara, California, named after Don Octaviano Gutierrez, an artillery sergeant in the Mexican army and later an important member of Santa Barbara’s city council. In 1845, the governor of California awarded Rancho La Laguna, an enormous land grant of approximately 13,000 acres, to Gutierrez.

haham.name: Hahamogna
haham.description: Hahamogna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Pasadena, California. It also refers to a Native American group who inhabited the area around the Arroyo Seco.

haley.name: Haley Street
haley.description: Haley Street: a street in Santa Barbara, California, named after Salisbury Haley, a town surveyor of Santa Barbara. Haley completed the first survey of Santa Barbara, which was famously inaccurate and led to the creation of uneven city blocks, in 1851.

re_halfmoon.name: Half Moon Bay
re_halfmoon.description: Half Moon Bay: a bay located inside Pillar Point in San Mateo County, California. Part of the Costanoan people's homeland, the area became occupied by settlers during the Gold Rush, and in 1959, the city of the Half Moon Bay was incorporated.

harasg.name: Harasgna
harasg.description: Harasgna: the Gabrielino (Tongva) name for San Nicolas Island. The Chumash pronounced it "Ghalas-at."

hpt.name: Harris Point
hpt.description: Harris Point: a rocky promontory on the northern coast of California's San Miguel Island, which was inhabited by the Chumash and their ancestors for nearly 12,000 years. Harris Point was the site of famous San Miguel resident Herbert Lester's suicide in 1942 and is the burial location of Lester and his wife.

i_hawaii.name: Hawaii
i_hawaii.description: Hawaii: a group of volcanic islands in the Pacific Ocean and the fiftieth state of the United States of America. Polynesians began settling the islands between 300 and 600 CE. Captain James Cook arrived in Hawaii in 1778, and American missionaries arrived in 1820. The Islands became a central point of trade in the Pacific, with American and British merchant houses establishing offices in Honolulu in the 1820s and 30s. In 1848, white settlers pressured the Hawaiian monarch into signing the Great Mahele, which allowed for the division of lands and private property. A group of American and European businessmen seized control of Hawaii with the help of US Marines in 1893, resulting in Hawaii becoming a territory in 1900 and a state in 1959.

highlandinn.name: Highland Inn
highlandinn.description: Highland Inn: a hotel in Galveston, Texas. It was purchased by F. J. Wood in 1917 and was used by vacationers looking for a resort experience.

hogstye.name: Hog Stye Reefs
hogstye.description: Hog Stye Reefs: a small atoll in the Bahamas also referred to as Hogsty Reef. The first known record of the reef is on a French chart produced in 1750. The reef, because it is low-lying and difficult to see, is a ship graveyard, housing the shipwrecks of the SS Richmond P. Hobson and the Lady Eagle.

co_hol.name: Holland
co_hol.description: Holland: a former province of the Netherlands; in 1840, it was split into Noord-Holland and Zuid-Holland. In the early twelfth century Holland was established as a fief of the Holy Roman Empire. In 1572, Holland and six other northern provinces revolted against Spain. They declared their independence from the Spanish in 1579, calling themselves the United Provinces of the Netherlands. Because Holland was the most dominant province in the Dutch Republic during the seventeenth century, “Holland” is often used as a synonym for the present-day county of the Netherlands, which is bordered by Germany and Belgium.

ci_hollywood.name: Hollywood
ci_hollywood.description: Hollywood: refers to both the American film industry and a district of Los Angeles, California. It was first settled in 1853, when an adobe building was constructed there. In the early twentieth century, filmmakers selected this area as an ideal place to build studios due to its mild climate, sunny weather, varied terrain, and large labor market. Although shooting on location has become more popular in recent years, Hollywood remains an important place in both film and television production.

ci_hon.name: Honolulu
ci_hon.description: Honolulu: the capital city and primary port of the Hawaiian Islands, which were first inhabited around 400 CE. By 1820, Honolulu was the island’s economic center and a major stopping point in American and European Pacific trade; as a result, British and American merchant houses established offices in Honolulu in the 1820s and 30s. King Kamehameha III declared Honolulu the capital of Hawaii in 1850, and the city has retained that title.

metropole.name: Hotel Metropole
metropole.description: Hotel Metropole: a hotel in Avalon, on Santa Catalina Island, built by developer George Shatto in 1887. The hotel was extremely popular and thrived until 1915, when it was destroyed by a large fire that also incinerated five other hotels. Today, a new Hotel Metropole stands on the site of the original building.

houtgna.name: Houtgna
houtgna.description: Houtgna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near the San Gabriel Mission. A variant spelling is "Hout."

huasma.name: Huasna Rancho
huasma.description: Huasna Rancho: a 22,152-acre Mexican land grant in present-day San Luis Obispo County, California. The Mexican government granted the land to Isaac J. Sparks in 1843, and the land was divided among his daughters upon his death in 1867.

co_hum.name: Humboldt County
co_hum.description: Humboldt County: a county in northern California that is the home of the Wiyot people. The county and nearby bay are named after the explorer and naturalist Baron Alexander von Humbolt. It was incorporated in 1853; the Humboldt County seat is located in the city of Eureka and had a population of 134,825 in 2016.

hutuc.name: Hutucgna
hutuc.description: Hutucgna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Anaheim, Santa Ana, and the Santa Ana River in California. It is also referred to as Hutuuknga.

st_id.name: Idaho
st_id.description: Idaho: the forty-third state of the United States, which it joined on July 3, 1890. Idaho is home to the Bannock, Blackfeet, Coeur d’Alene, Kootenai, Nez Perce, Northern Paiute, Palouse, Shoshoni, and Kalispel and Spokane Salish people. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, lumber, and mining.

st_il.name: Illinois
st_il.description: Illinois: the twenty-first state of the United States, which it joined on December 3, 1818. Illinois is the homeland of the Chickasaw, Dakota Sioux, Ho-Chunk, Illinois, Miami, and Shawnee people. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, cattle, manufacturing, and mining.

ci_inc.name: Incpupu
ci_inc.description: Incpupu: a Chumash village listed in the notes of the Cabrillo exploration of the United States' western coastline (1542-43) and identified by Alfred Kroeber in 1925 as possibly being "Humkaka," near Point Conception.

independencehall.name: Independence Hall
independencehall.description: Independence Hall: a building in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, now part of the Independence National Historical Park. The Founding Fathers discussed and signed the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution in Independence Hall. In 1979, the building became a World Heritage site.

co_in.name: India
co_in.description: India: the country that occupies most of the Indian subcontinent in South Asia. From approximately 2600 to 2000 BCE, the Indus civilization dominated the northwestern portion of the subcontinent. The major religions of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism all originated on the subcontinent. Muslim traders entered from the north in the seventh century CE, and by the thirteenth century, much of India was under Muslim rule. The British arrived in the seventeenth century, and, by the mid-eighteenth century, the subcontinent largely fell under the control of the British East India Company. British occupation of India, which officially began in 1858, ended in 1947. The subcontinent split into two countries: India and Pakistan. Despite ongoing struggles with overpopulation, corruption, and poverty, India is a rapidly developing country and global power today.

klrivres.name: Indian reservation on the Klamath River
klrivres.description: Indian Reservation on the Klamath River: likely refers to a forty square mile reservation established by an executive order on November 16, 1855. This reservation was located in Humboldt County, California, and was reserved for the Klamath people.

st_ia.name: Iowa
st_ia.description: Iowa: the twenty-ninth state of the United States, which it joined on December 28, 1846. Iowa encompasses the homeland of the Dakota Sioux, Illinois, Ioway, Missouri, and Otoe peoples. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, food manufacturing, and insurance.

co_ire.name: Ireland
co_ire.description: Ireland: the westernmost island of the British Isles. It was first settled by Celtic people in the sixth century BCE. In the 1100s, England began taking Irish land, a practice that continued for many centuries. Ireland also faced a tragic potato blight in the mid-nineteenth century, causing millions of deaths. In 1922, the Roman Catholic counties of Ireland became independent, but the northern counties of Ireland remained part of the United Kingdom, causing violence between the two groups until a peace treaty was signed in 1998.

re_imc.name: Isabella Meadows Cave
re_imc.description: Isabella Meadows Cave: a cave located in Monterey County, California, in the Southern Coast Ranges. It is named after Isabella Meadows, an Ohlone who worked with anthropologist J.P. Harrington. The cave, which was used by the Esselen people, was excavated by archaeologists in 1952.

isanth.name: Isanthcagna
isanth.description: Isanthcagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Mission Vejo and Aliso Creek, California. It was the original site of the San Gabriel Mission and was cited by the Smithsonian Institution in the Handbook of American Indians North of Mexico.

i_ts.name: Isla Todos Santos
i_ts.description: Isla Todos Santos: a pair of Pacific islands (Sur and Norte) located approximately twelve miles west of Ensenada in Baja California, Mexico. The islands are home to the "Killer’s" wave break, the biggest and best-known break, and are a popular surfing destination.

i_lc.name: Islas Coronados
i_lc.description: Islas Coronados: a cluster of four sparsely populated, undeveloped islands off the western coast of Baja California, Mexico. Also referred to as Los Coronados.

re_isthmuscove.name: Isthmus Cove
re_isthmuscove.description: Isthmus Cove: a bay located on Santa Catalina Island in California. The cove, which has 242 moorings and over 100 anchorages, is the second-most popular harbor on the island.

re_ispan.name: Isthmus of Panama
re_ispan.description: Isthmus of Panama: a strip of land extending approximately 400 miles from the Panama-Costa Rica border to the Panama-Colombia border. It separates the Caribbean Sea (part of the Atlantic Ocean) from the Gulf of Panama (part of the Pacific Ocean) and connects North America and South America. The Panama Canal, which was completed in 1914, was constructed so ships could use the isthmus to cross from ocean to ocean without sailing around Cape Horn in South America.

re_isthmus1.name: Isthmus
re_isthmus1.description: Isthmus: "the Isthmus" refers to an approximately half mile-wide strip of land on Santa Catalina Island that segregates two harbors. This area is one of many on Santa Catalina Island that boasted a Gabrielino (Tongva) settlement. Juan Cabrillo of Spain landed there in 1542, and as Europeans voyaged to Santa Catalina Island over the next hundred years, they brought disease and generated ecological ruin, causing the original Gabrielino (Tongva) population of 2,000-3,000 people to decline precipitously. In the 1820s, the final group of Gabrielino (Tongva) people were removed by the Spanish to San Gabriel Mission, on the mainland. The settlement on this isthmus is now appropriately named Two Harbors.

ci_itacuara.name: Itacaruaré, Argentina
ci_itacuara.description: Itacaruaré: a small city in Argentina located in the northeast region of the country, in the Misiones Province. It was made famous in 1943 when a man named Pacifico Batista appeared in the town and was deemed “wild;” he was initially unable to speak but eventually identified himself and expressed a desire to return to the jungle.

re_jacksonhill.name: Jackson Hill
re_jacksonhill.description: Jackson Hill: the highest point on San Nicolas Island in California. The summit has a disputed height of 905 or 907 feet and is located in the central-southern portion of the island.

co_jam.name: Jamaica
co_jam.description: Jamaica: an island country in the West Indies located west of Haiti and south of Cuba. Christopher Columbus became the first European to see the island in 1494. The Spanish settled the island in the early sixteenth century and, in 1655, England seized Jamaica from the Spanish, establishing a plantation system on the island. In 1962, Jamaica became independent from the United Kingdom. Although the island was home to the Taino people, most of the current inhabitants are descendants of former African slaves.

i_jf.name: Juan Fernández
i_jf.description: Juan Fernández Islands: an archipelago located along the Chilean coastline (west of Santiago). The islands are famous for being the site of Alexander Selkirk's four years in total isolation after being marooned by his crew. Selkirk later served as Daniel Defoe's model for Robinson Crusoe.

ci_ju.name: Julian
ci_ju.description: Julian: historic gold-mining town between Volcan Mountain and the Cumayaca Mountains in California, near the Mexican border. The Kumeyaay (Diegueño), lived here in small, seasonal villages before the arrival of the Spanish mission system. The discovery of gold in 1869 led to an influx of miners, and Drury D. Batley, an ex-Confederate soldier, took it upon himself to lay out the town, naming it after his cousin, Michael S. Julian. After the Gold Rush ended, the population dropped to about 100 people; those that remained stayed to farm the exceptionally fertile land. The cultivation of apples proved especially successful and continues today. Author Scott O'Dell wrote Island of the Blue Dolphins in Julian, where he lived with his wife Dorsa O’Dell at Stoneapple Farm. At that time (the late 1950s), Julian's population was about 1,500. Today, Julian is known as a mountain resort and is categorized as a census designated place (or unincorporated community).

ci_jur.name: Jurupa
ci_jur.description: Jurupa: an area that is now mostly the City of Jurupa Valley in Riverside County, California. Part of the homeland of the Gabrielino (Tongva) and Serrano people, it became a Mexican land grant in 1838 and was known as Rancho Jurupa. Often overshadowed by the larger city of Riverside, the community voted to incorporate into its own city in 2011.

ci_kal.name: Kalamazoo
ci_kal.description: Kalamazoo: a city in western Michigan. The home of the Pottawatomie people, Kalamazoo was first settled by European-Americans in 1829. Today, one of the most distinctive features of Kalamazoo is the Kalamazoo Promise, an initiative that promises college tuition to qualified graduates of local public high schools. Both Kalamazoo College and Western Michigan University are located in the city.

ci_ka.name: Kangik
ci_ka.description: Kangik: a former (likely Killaimute) village located in North Slope County, Alaska. The village has also been spelled Kangiak, Kagnik, Kungik, and Kungitk. It was featured in the popular children’s book by Jean Craighead George, Julie of the Wolves.

st_ks.name: Kansas
st_ks.description: Kansas: the thirty-fourth state of the United States, which it joined on January 29, 1861. Kansas is the home of the Arapaho, Comanche, Kansa, Kiowa, Missouri, Osage, Otoe, and Pawnee people. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, aircraft manufacturing, and automobile manufacturing.

st_ky.name: Kentucky
st_ky.description: Kentucky: the fifteenth state to join the United States of America, which it did on June 1, 1792. In 1905, the state was home to between 2.1 and 2.3 million residents. The state's agrarian economy, however, began to experience the effects of long-term migration from rural to urban areas as well as the "Black Patch Tobacco War" (1904-11) initiated by tobacco planters against monopoly interests around the turn of the twentieth century.

re_kingsriv.name: Kings River
re_kingsriv.description: Kings River: a river in California that begins in Kings Canyon National Park and forms the boundary between Sequoia National Forest and Sierra National Forest. The river flows into the San Joaquin Valley, where much of it is used for irrigation. On January 6, 1805, Captain Gabriel Moraga named the river "El Rio de los Santos Reyes" (River of the Holy Kings) in honor of Epiphany.

ci_king.name: Kingston
ci_king.description: Kingston: the capital and chief port city of Jamaica. It was established in 1692 after a previous settlement at the mouth of the harbor, Port Royal, was destroyed by an earthquake. Today, the city and its surrounding areas are a major tourist destination and house over one-quarter of Jamaica's population.

co_klam.name: Klamath County
co_klam.description: Klamath County: a county in southern Oregon and the homeland of the Klamath, Modoc, and Yahooskin people. It was established on October 17, 1882, and is named after the Klamath Tribes, which have lived in the area for approximately 10,000 years. The county seat is located in Klamath Falls, and the county had a population of 66,810 in 2013.

re_kllake.name: Klamath Lake
re_kllake.description: Klamath Lake: refers to Upper Klamath Lake, which is located in Klamath County, Oregon, and was part of the ancient Pluvial Lake Modoc. At 61,543 acres in size, it is the largest body of fresh water west of the Rocky Mountains.

re_klriver.name: Klamath River
re_klriver.description: Klamath River: a river that begins at Upper Klamath Lake and travels through Oregon and California before emptying into the Pacific Ocean. The river is home to one of the largest salmon runs in the western United States.

knferry.name: Knight's Ferry
knferry.description: Knight's Ferry: a town along the Stanislaus River in California, established as a trading post and the site of William Knight’s ferry service in 1849. It was the county seat of Stanislaus County from 1862-72. Around 1856, a bridge was built by David Locke, which was then destroyed when a flood caused the Two Mile Bar Bridge to crash into it. A new bridge, which is still open to foot traffic today, was finished by 1863.

i_ko.name: Kodiak Island
i_ko.description: Kodiak Island: the largest island of the Kodiak Archipelago, located on the southern coast of Alaska. The native Kodiak Aluttit were able to resist Russian rule for several decades, but as the Russian fur trade expanded, Kodiak Island became a hub for the conscripted labor on which the Russian American Company relied. The island served as the capital of Russia's holdings in the Americas before administrative offices were moved to Sitka.

lasarmos.name: Las Cañada de las Armos
lasarmos.description: La Cañada de las Armos: the Spanish name for Texmaw, a former Chumash village located approximately twelve miles from the Santa Barbara Mission in California.

delcorral.name: La Cañada del Corral
delcorral.description: La Cañada del Corral: the Spanish name for Kaughii, a former Chumash village located approximately twenty two miles from the Santa Barbara Mission in California.

re_calera.name: La Calera Rancho
re_calera.description: La Calera Rancho: part of Rancho Las Positas y La Calera, a rancho comprised of two Mexican land grants located in Santa Barbara County, California. First owned by Thomas Robbins, it was originally granted to Narciso Fabregat in 1843. La Calera means "little kiln" and is named after a kiln that operated in the area while Mission Santa Barbara was built.

lgolita.name: La Golita
lgolita.description: La Golita: the Spanish name for Chuah, a former Chumash village located approximately six miles from the Santa Barbara Mission in California.

patera.name: La Patera Ranch
patera.description: La Patera Ranch: a 1,100-acre rancho located in the Goleta Valley region of California. William Whitney Stow bought the property for his son in 1871, and the ranch was well-known for its citrus cultivation. Although the Stow family sold the ranch in the 1960s, portions of it are still in operation.

lpatira.name: La Patira
lpatira.description: La Patira: the Spanish name for Alwaththalam (also spelled Alwathalama, Allvatalama, and Aswalthatans), a former Chumash village located approximately seven miles from the Santa Barbara Mission on the marsh of Goleta.

ci_pur.name: La Purisima Mission
ci_pur.description: La Purisima Mission: a mission established in the Chumash village of Algsacupi (present-day Lompoc) and founded in 1787. Also called La Purísima Concepción, La Purisima Mission had 1,520 Chumash neophytes living there at its peak in 1804. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1874. It is now a California State Historic Park and serves as a living history museum.

lasing.name: La Singaguita
lasing.description: La Sinaguita: the Spanish name for Cashwah, a former Chumash village located approximately three miles from the Santa Barbara Mission in California.

re_ladyharbor.name: Lady Harbor
re_ladyharbor.description: Lady Harbor: a bay on the northern end of Santa Cruz Island between Diablo Anchorage and Valdez Cove. It serves as an anchorage for ships and is directly west of Baby’s Cove (also known as Little Lady’s Cove).

possas.name: Las Possas
possas.description: Las Possas: the Spanish name for Otenashmoo (or Otenashmo), a former Chumash village approximately two miles from the Santa Barbara Mission.

co_latvia.name: Latvia
co_latvia.description: Latvia: country in northeastern Europe and one of the Baltic States. The Republic of Latvia was founded on November 18, 1918 but occupied and annexed by the USSR in 1940 at the start of World War II. On August 21, 1991, Latvia declared its independence and was recognized by the USSR on September 6. Russians and Latvians constitute the majority of Latvia’s ethnic make-up, and though Latvian is the official language, Russian is spoken by a third of Latvia’s approximately two million inhabitants. Riga, the country’s capital and most populous city, was the birthplace of 1961 Caldecott Award winner, Nicholas Sidjakov.

ci_li.name: Liverpool
ci_li.description: Liverpool: a city on the northwest coast of England situated along the Mersey River. The first historical mention of Liverpool is in 1207, when the borough of Liuerpul was created. Because it was close to the water, Liverpool became known for its dock system and was a hub for trade (including the transatlantic slave trade); the city also played a key role in the industrial revolution, and James Watts' steam engine was invented here.  Many ships that sailed from England to the United States left from Liverpool during the nineteenth century.

sbopera.name: Opera House
sbopera.description: Lobero Theatre: California's oldest continually operating theater, located in Santa Barbara. Founded by Jose Lobero in 1873, the theater was the largest adobe building in California when it opened. In the early part of the twentieth century, stars such as Lionel Barrymore, Bela Lugosi, and Tallulah Bankhead appeared at the theater in preview performances for Hollywood. The theater was rebuilt in 1924.

ci_london.name: London
ci_london.description: London: the capital of the United Kingdom and one of the world's largest cities. London was founded around 50 CE by Roman soldiers, who maintained control of the area until approximately 400 CE. Over the years, London became the capital of the British Empire and served as a major cultural and political center. During the nineteenth century, London continued to serve as a prosperous urban center and underwent many financial and social changes due to increasing industrialization. Such changes also led to widespread poverty and crime, as popularized in the novels of Charles Dickens.

re_lwcave.name: Lone Woman's Cave
re_lwcave.description: Lone Woman's Cave: a cave on San Nicolas Island likely inhabited by the Lone Woman and her ancestors for centuries before. An 1879 US Coast and Geodetic Survey map marked its location as "Indian Cave," but erosion and wind had buried it completely by the mid twentieth century. US Navy archaeologist Steven J. Schwartz spent two decades searching for its location, finally succeeding in 2012, when he read the field notes that had accompanied the 1879 map. As of 2016, excavation of the cave had not yet taken place.

ci_lb.name: Long Beach
ci_lb.description: Long Beach: a city in San Pedro Bay, Los Angeles County. Originally a trading camp for the Gabrielino (Tongva), the land that became Long Beach was carved out of the massive Rancho Nietos. In 1880, developer W. E. Willmore laid out plans for Willmore City and in 1882, the town was first settled. In 1888, the name was changed to Long Beach, and the city was incorporated. The population of Long Beach increased from less than 500 in 1890 to over 55,000 just thirty years later, and from 1902 to 1910, Long Beach was the fastest growing city in the entire United States. Author Scott O’Dell (born 1898) lived in Long Beach as a teenager, graduating from Long Beach Polytechnic High School.

co_la.name: Los Angeles County
co_la.description: Los Angeles County: a county located in Southern California comprised of Los Angeles and 87 surrounding cities as well as lands traditionally inhabited by the Gabrielino (Tongva) and Chumash people. It has been the most populous county in the United States since 1960. As of 2016, it has an estimated population of 10.4 million, more than that of many states, including Georgia, North Carolina, and Michigan.

mi_losangeles.name: Los Angeles Mission
mi_losangeles.description: Los Angeles Mission: a mission located in present-day Los Angeles and founded in 1797, although the first church was not completed until 1799. Also known as San Fernando Mission or San Fernando Rey de España, by 1811 the mission had 1,081 residents, most of whom were Tataviam or Gabrielino (Tongva). The mission was secularized in 1834; due to this, the Nicoleños who arrived in San Pedro in 1835 were not brought to this mission. Instead, some of the group were baptized at the Los Angeles Plaza Church. The mission was returned to the Catholic Church in 1861.

ci_la.name: Los Angeles
ci_la.description: Los Angeles: the second most populous city in the United States. Located on a broad coastal plain in California that is part of the Gabrielino (Tongva) homeland, the city is situated in Los Angeles County and surrounded by other cities such as Beverly Hills, Pasadena, and Long Beach. Los Angeles was founded by California governor Felipe de Neve, who settled the area with forty-four others in 1781. For about a century, Los Angeles remained relatively isolated with no natural harbor and lacking fuel, water, or lumber sources able to sustain a large population. San Pedro served as its port (and it was here that the Nicoleños removed from San Nicolas Island in 1835 were brought). Los Angeles' population boom started in the 1870s, when it was connected to San Francisco by the Southern Pacific Railroad and many people in search of a healthy climate moved there in the "Sick Rush." As an aqueduct was completed and the headquarters of film studios moved to Hollywood, the population soared. Today, the city is known for its semiarid climate and continued connections with the American filmmaking industry.

ci_lo.name: Los Olivos
ci_lo.description: Los Olivos: a town located in the Santa Ynez Valley of Santa Barbara County, California and part of the homeland of the Chumash. A stagecoach station was founded in the area in 1861, and the town, which was named after a nearby ranch that housed approximately 5,000 olive trees, followed in 1887.

st_la.name: Louisiana
st_la.description: Louisiana: the eighteenth state of the United States, which it joined on April 30, 1812. Louisiana is the homeland of the Atakapa, Caddo, Chitimacha, Choctaw, Houma, Natchez, and Tunica people. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, fishing, oil and natural gas extraction, and salt production.

ci_lowell.name: Lowell
ci_lowell.description: Lowell: a city at the confluence of the Merrimack and Concord rivers in Massachusetts. Originally the home of the Pennacook Indians, Lowell was first inhabited by white settlers in 1653. In the nineteenth century, the village became a major textile manufacturing center, drawing notice from Charles Dickens and Anthony Trollope. Although the local economy declined after textile mills relocated to the South, as of 206 Lowell has been undergoing revitalization efforts.

ci_macamo.name: Macamo
ci_macamo.description: Macamo: a Chumash village on Santa Cruz Island, the largest of the California Channel Islands. The Chumash people lived on the island for thousands of years and developed a highly complex society dependent on the sea, specialized craft, and mainland trade.
