More recently, the island was occupied by the Chumash, and Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo documented his encounter with Santa Rosa islanders during his exploration of the California coast in 1542. The Spanish began removing the Island Chumash to Catholic missions on the mainland in the early nineteenth century, with the last Native leaving Santa Rosa in 1817. During the nineteenth century, the island was used for sheep and cattle ranching. Today, Santa Rosa is part of the Channel Islands National Park.

ci_sr.name: Santa Rosa
ci_sr.description: Santa Rosa: a city located in Sonoma County, California, home to the Pomo, Miwok, and Wappo peoples. Santa Rosa was founded in 1833 and incorporated in 1868. The city was the home of Fountain Grove, a utopian experiment established in 1885.

re_santaynezmts.name: Santa Ynez Mountains
re_santaynezmts.description: Santa Ynez Mountains: one of the only east-west trending mountain ranges in the United States, located in Southern California’s Santa Barbara and Ventura Counties.

re_syvalley.name: Santa Ynez Valley
re_syvalley.description: Santa Ynez Valley: the valley between the Santa Ynez and San Rafael Mountains, in Santa Barbara County; it is in the Chumash homeland. Today the valley, which boasts a thriving industry in wine and tourism, is home to six towns:  Ballard, Buellton, Los Alamos, Los Olivos, Santa Ynez, and Solvang.

ci_sy.name: Santa Ynez
ci_sy.description: Santa Ynez: a town in the Santa Ynez Valley of California. Settlers began arriving in this area, which is part of Chumash territory, in 1881. The town experienced a brief population boom in the 1880s in anticipation of the railroad's arrival, but when the Southern Pacific Railroad decided not to build a connection through the town, Santa Ynez faded back into relative obscurity.

ci_sat.name: Saticoy
ci_sat.description: Saticoy: a census-designated area located in Ventura County, California, and home of the Chumash people. In 2016, the area had an estimated population of 1,101.

ci_sa.name: Sausalito
ci_sa.description: Sausalito: a city in Marin County, California. Originally the home of Coast Miwok Natives, it became a Mexican land grant, Rancho Sausalito. William Richardson, the land grantee, founded the town in 1838 and sold the land to Charles Botts after the Gold Rush in 1849. Today, Sausalito is primarily a bedroom community of San Francisco.

co_shasta.name: Shasta County
co_shasta.description: Shasta County: a county in northern California that encompasses the homeland of the Achomawi, Atsugewi, Okwanuchu, Wintu, and Yana peoples. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850. The county seat is located in the city of Redding, and the county had a population of 178,795 in 2016.

sibagna.name: Sibagna
sibagna.description: Sibagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located at the site of Mission San Gabriel in Los Angeles County, California. Alternate spellings include Tobiscagna, Toviseanga, Tobiscanga, Tobizcanga, Tuvasak, Toviscanga, and Toviseanga.

co_sierra.name: Sierra County
co_sierra.description: Sierra County: a county in northern California that includes the homeland of the Maidu and Washoe peoples. Sierra County was incorporated on April 16, 1852, and is named after the nearby Sierra Nevada mountain range. The county seat is located in the town of Downieville, and the county had a population of 3,166 in 2016.

re_sierranevada.name: Sierra Nevada
re_sierranevada.description: Sierra Nevada: a mountain range extending over 250 miles along California’s eastern edge. Part of the homeland of the Yokut, Sierra Miwok, Maidu, Owens Valley Paiute, and Washoe, the Sierra Nevada range experienced an influx of settlers during the Gold Rush of 1849. Today, the mountain range serves as an important source of water and power for much of California’s population.

sisit.name: Sisitcanogna
sisit.description: Sisitcanogna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located in the San Gabriel Valley at Pear Orchard in Los Angeles County, California. Alternate spellings include Sisitkanonga, Sisitcanonga, and Sisitkanogna.

co_siski.name: Siskiyou County
co_siski.description: Siskiyou County: a county in northern California that includes the homeland of the Karuk, Modoc, Wintu, Achomawi, and Klamath peoples. It was incorporated on March 22, 1852, and is named after the nearby Siskiyou mountain range. The county seat is located in the city of Yreka, and the county had a population of 44,563 in 2016.

sisters.name: Sisters School
sisters.description: Sisters School: a school located in Santa Barbara, California, founded in 1858 and operated by the Sisters of Charity of St. Vincent de Paul. The school was initially located near Carrillo and State Streets, where General Fremont’s headquarters were located. It later moved to 925 De La Vina Street, where it remained until the 1925 earthquake.

ci_si.name: Sitka
ci_si.description: Sitka: the capital of Russian America, known by the Russians as New Archangel. It is part of the homeland of the Tlingit people. Today, Sitka is a small city and borough located near Juneau.

re_sixbull.name: Six Bull River
re_sixbull.description: Six Bull River: a variant name for the Neosho River, a tributary of the Arkansas River. It is about 460 miles and flows through eastern Kansas and northeastern Oklahoma, meeting the Arkansas River around Muskogee. Other names for the Neosho River have included Grand River, Osage River, and Rio Blanco.

re_smugglers2.name: Smuggler's Cove
re_smugglers2.description: Smuggler’s Cove: a bay on San Clemente Island in California. Bootleggers and smugglers used the remote cove to hide their goods, but only when more convenient hiding spots were unavailable.

re_smugglerscove.name: Smuggler's Cove
re_smugglerscove.description: Smuggler’s Cove: a bay on Santa Cruz Island in California. The cove was a popular spot for bootleggers and smugglers to hide their goods because the area was isolated yet still accessible.

sonagna.name: Sonagna
sonagna.description: Sonagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located at White’s Ranch in Los Angeles County, California. Alternate spellings include Sonagno and Sonanga.

co_sonoma.name: Sonoma County
co_sonoma.description: Sonoma County: a county in California that makes up the homeland of the Pomo, Coast Miwok, and Wappo people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after a Chocuyen word meaning either "valley of the moon" or "land or tribe of the Chief Nose." The county seat is located in the city of Santa Rosa, and the county had a population of 500,487 in 2016.

re_sfrance.name: South of France
re_sfrance.description: South of France: a region of France extending from the Spanish border to the Italian border consisting of Provence, the Alps, Cote d’Azur, and Languedoc-Roussillon. Southern France (especially the French Riviera) is a popular tourist destination.

i_southernchi.name: Southern Channel Islands
i_southernchi.description: Southern Channel Islands: San Clemente, San Nicolas, Santa Barbara, and Santa Catalina lie west of the San Pedro Channel and form the Santa Catalina group. The Gabrielino (Tongva), a maritime people, lived on the larger of the three islands, San Clemente, San Nicolas, and Santa Catalina, until the Spanish brought them to the mainland, either as a direct result of the mission system or as an effect of depopulation in the wake of European arrival. The last Gabrielino (Tongva) to live on one of the Southern Channel Islands, the Lone Woman of San Nicolas, removed to the mainland in 1853.

co_spain.name: Spain
co_spain.description: Spain: a country located in the Iberian Peninsula of southwestern Europe. After Christopher Columbus reached the Americas in 1492, Spanish conquistadors laid claim to the land, often forcibly converting the Native peoples and pressing them into service. The Spanish continued this practice in what was eventually to become the US state of California, the northern reaches of their empire.  Spain lost much of its overseas possessions, including the modern day country of Mexico and US state of California, in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.

ci_sl.name: St. Louis
ci_sl.description: St. Louis: a city located on the western bank of the Mississippi River in Missouri. The region of St. Louis was inhabited by the Mound Builders of the Mississippian culture until the fourteenth century. The Missouri and Osage peoples dominated the region when the French established St. Louis as a fur trading post in 1764. Spain gained control of the territory in 1768, and though it was returned to France in 1800, St. Louis was run by Spanish governors until the Louisiana Purchase (1803), which transferred the land to the United States. St. Louis was the departure site for the Lewis and Clark expedition the following year. The town's population grew and St. Louis became an important center for commerce and trade, especially the fur industry. It was incorporated as a city in 1823. Following waves of immigration and the hosting of the 1904 Olympic Games, the population peaked at 800,000 in 1950, and then fell dramatically due to suburbanization. In 1978, Scott O’Dell attended the Catholic Library Association Convention in St. Louis to accept the Regina Medal Award.

stmarys.name: St. Mary's Church
stmarys.description: St. Mary’s Church: a Catholic cathedral located in San Francisco on the corner of California Street and Dupont (later Grant Avenue). The cornerstone for St. Mary's Church was laid in 1853 and the cathedral was dedicated at Christmas Mass in 1854. When neighborhood demographics changed in the 1880s, the church was moved. A new Cathedral of St. Mary of the Assumption was dedicated in January 1891 and the former cathedral on California Street, which became known as "Old Saint Mary's," became a Chinese Mission operated by the Paulists, a missionary society. Old Saint Mary’s was restored and rededicated in 1909, following damage in the 1906 earthquake.

re_stan.name: Stanislaus River
re_stan.description: Stanislaus River: a California river that begins in the foothills of the Sierra Nevada mountain chain and flows sixty-five miles through San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Calaveras, and Tuolumne counties to the San Joaquin River. In 1806, Gabriel Moraga named the river after Our Lady of Guadalupe, but the river was later renamed to honor Estanislao, a Native leader.

state.name: State Street
state.description: State Street: a street in Santa Barbara, named when California was celebrating its admittance to the Union as a state. Salisbury Haley conducted a survey of the city after statehood, and an attempt was made to restructure the city on a grid system. In Haley's survey, Carrillo and State Streets were the only two listed as being 80-feet-wide rather than 60-feet-wide.

ci_sto.name: Stockton
ci_sto.description: Stockton: a city located in California along the San Joaquin River. First occupied by the Yatchicumne, the area became part of a Mexican land grant in 1844. The city was founded as Tuleburg in 1849, and its population expanded greatly during the Gold Rush. The city was renamed and incorporated in 1850.

stoneapple.name: Stoneapple Farm
stoneapple.description: Stoneapple Farm: country home in Julian, California, where Scott O'Dell wrote Island of the Blue Dolphins, among other books. The remodeled apple storage and packing house was built out of stone in the late 19th century and converted into a residential home in the 1940s. After Scott O'Dell and his wife Dorsa separated, Dorsa continued to live at Stoneapple Farm until her death in 2008. The property is now owned by Dorsa O'Dell's niece and it serves as a writer's cottage.

suagna.name: Suagna
suagna.description: Suagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Cerritos or Palos Verdes in Los Angeles County, California. Alternate spellings include Shuavit, Suanga, Suangna, and Soabit.

co_sul.name: Sullivan County
co_sul.description: Sullivan County: a county in eastern Tennessee, bordering Virginia. Sullivan County’s Long Island is a sacred spot for the Cherokee. Settlers arrived in the late 1760s, and the county was established in 1780. Today, Kingsport and Bristol, two cities in Tennessee’s Tri-Cities region, are located in Sullivan County.

co_sutter.name: Sutter County
co_sutter.description: Sutter County: a county in California that encompasses the homeland of the Maidu people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after General John Augustus Sutter, a Swiss immigrant who obtained a land grant from the Mexican government and settled present-day Sacramento. The county seat is located in Yuba City, and the county had a population of 97,701 in 2016.

i_tab.name: Taboga
i_tab.description: Taboga: an island in the Bay of Panama, approximately eleven miles south of Panama City. Isla Taboga is approximately two miles long and one mile wide and is renowned for its mangoes and pineapples. The island houses Iglesia San Pedro, which is allegedly the Western Hemisphere’s second-oldest church.

co_tehama.name: Tehama County
co_tehama.description: Tehama County: a county in northern California that is the traditional homeland of the Nomlaki (also spelled Nomi Lackee) people. Tehama County was incorporated on April 9, 1856, and is named after the City of Tehama. The county seat is located in the city of Red Bluff, and the county had a population of 63,975 in 2016.

suntemple.name: Temple of the Sun
suntemple.description: Temple of the Sun: a Gabrielino (Tongva) religious complex on Santa Catalina Island, California. It consisted of a collection of outdoor altars, large stones, and ceremonial burial sites. The complex has long since vanished, possibly due to earthquakes, landslides, or heavy rains.

st_tn.name: Tennessee
st_tn.description: Tennessee: the sixteenth state of the United States, which it joined on June 1, 1796. Tennessee includes the homeland of the Cherokee, Chickasaw, Creek, Koasati, Quapaw, Shawnee, and Yuchi peoples. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, electrical power, enriched uranium production, music, automobile manufacturing, mining, tourism, and horse breeding.

i_ti.name: Terminal Island
i_ti.description: Terminal Island: a largely artificial island in San Pedro Bay, off the coast of southern California. To improve the bay’s harbor infrastructure, a long rock jetty was built between the Bay’s Deadman’s Island and nearby Rattlesnake Island to create a shipping channel between the islands and the mainland. The breakwater caused the reshaping of Rattlesnake Island and the disintegration of most of Deadman’s Island. In 1891, the Terminal Company bought Rattlesnake Island and changed its name to Terminal Island. Author Scott O’Dell was born here in 1898. What was left of Deadman’s Island was removed completely in 1928 and added on to Terminal Island. Terminal Island was a large Japanese fishing village until Executive Order 9066 in 1942 led to the internment of Japanese Americans. Today, Terminal Island is split between the city of Long Beach and the city of Los Angeles. It houses a Federal Correction Institution and is an industrial port.

st_tx.name: Texas
st_tx.description: Texas: the twenty-eighth state of the United States, which it joined on December 29, 1845. Texas is the homeland of the Apache, Bidai, Coahuiltecan, Carrizo, Caddo, Comanche, Jumano, Suma, Piro, Karankawa, Kiowa, Kitsai, Tawakoni, Tonkawa, and Wichita peoples. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, oil and natural gas extraction, steel production, banking, tourism, and insurance.

co_th.name: Thailand
co_th.description: Thailand: a country in southeastern Asia bordered by Myanmar, Cambodia, Laos, and Malaysia. Originally settled by Tai-speaking peoples in the eleventh century, Thailand became a unified kingdom in the mid-fourteenth century. Thailand, which was called Siam until 1939, is the only southeast Asian country that was never colonized by Europeans. The country was an absolute monarchy until 1932, when a bloodless revolution created a constitutional monarchy. Since 2005, Thailand has experienced a military coup and a variety of major political protests.

re_lhfork.name: Lewis & Henry's Forks
re_lhfork.description: the Lewis and Henry’s Forks: Lewis Fork is a variant name for the 1078-mile Snake River, which heads in the Grand Tetons of Wyoming and flows to Washington’s Columbia River. Henry’s Fork is a 127-mile tributary of the Snake River located in Idaho. Many Native groups, such as the , Blackfeet, and Shoshone, lived on or near the Henry’s Fork watershed.

hoperanch.name: Hope's Ranch
hoperanch.description: Thomas Hope's Land, "Las Positas": land purchased by Thomas Hope, an Irish sheep farmer, in 1861 and part of the homeland of the Cieneguitas tribe. Also known as Hope Ranch, the Southern Pacific Railroad bought the land in 1876, but residential development did not commence on the property until 1923, when Harold Chase bought the land. It is now a suburb of Santa Barbara, California.

tibih.name: Tibihagna
tibih.description: Tibihagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Bixby Knolls and Lakewood, California. Alternate spellings include Tibahanga, Tibahagna, and Tibaha.

ci_tyreus.name: Tiryns
ci_tyreus.description: Tiryns: a prehistoric city located in present-day Argolis, Greece. By the Early Bronze Age, the area was occupied by an agricultural pre-Greek civilization. The city became a center of Mycenaean culture and was destroyed by Argos around 468 BCE. By the second century CE, the area was deserted.

re_tamalesbay.name: Tomales Bay
re_tamalesbay.description: Tomales Bay: a bay in Marin County, California, that is part of the Coast Miwok homeland. It is also known as Estero Americano, Estero De Tamales, and Rio Grande de San Sebasitian.

toybip.name: Toybipet
toybip.description: Toybipet: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) settlement located in Los Angeles County, California, on a site later known as San Jose. It was also known as Sibapot and Toibi.

co_trinity.name: Trinity County
co_trinity.description: Trinity County: a county in northern California that is the part of the homeland of the Wintu people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is termed after the Trinity River, which Major Pearson B. Reading named because he mistakenly believed that it emptied into Trinidad Bay. The county seat is located in the town of Weaverville, and the county had a population of 13,583 in 2016.

re_thlare.name: Tulare County
re_thlare.description: Tulare County: a county in California that is part of the homeland of the Yokuts. Tulare County was incorporated on April 20, 1852, and is named after the Tulare Lake, which Commandante Fages deemed "Los Tules" in 1772. The county seat is located in the city of Visalia, and the county had a population of 465,013 in 2016.

co_tuol.name: Tuolumne County
co_tuol.description: Tuolumne County: a county in California that is part of the homeland of the Me-Wuk people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after a Native word meaning "many stone houses," "the land of mountain lions," or "straight up steep." The county seat is located in the city of Sonora, and the county had a population of 54,511 in 2016.

re_tuo.name: Tuolumne River
re_tuo.description: Tuolumne River: a river in California that begins in Yosemite National Park and travels through the Tuolumne Meadows and Don Pedro Reservoir before flowing into the San Joaquin River. The Tuolumne River harbors 34 endemic species, including the Yosemite toad and the Dwarf downingia.

ci_tz.name: Tzintzuntzan
ci_tz.description: Tzintzuntzan: the capital of the Tarascan Empire, which flourished in western Mexico from 1350 to 1522 CE. Tzintzuntzan was located on the northeastern part of Lake Pátzcuaro and had a population of 35,000 by 1522. It served as the commercial, governmental, and religious center of the Tarascan civilization.

ci_ueck.name: Ueckermünde
ci_ueck.description: Ueckermünde: a seaport town in northeastern Germany founded in the Middle Ages by the Ukrani, a Wendish tribe. Ueckermünde became the district town in 1818, a position it held until 1994. After World War II, Ueckermünde became part of East Germany and was recognized as an official holiday resort.

re_umpquariver.name: Umpqua River
re_umpquariver.description: Umpqua River: a river that begins nears Roseburg, Oregon, and flows approximately 111 miles through Douglas County to Winchester Bay, where it enters the Pacific Ocean. Several Native peoples, including the Umpqua, Molala, and Kalapuyan, lived on or near the river.

re_valdezcave.name: Valdez Cave
re_valdezcave.description: Valdez Harbor: refers to a location on the northwestern part of Santa Cruz Island also known as Valdez Cave and Cueva Valdez. Cueva Valdez is famous for its large cave and has been a favorite picnicking and camping destination since the early twentieth century.

vcnavy.name: Ventura County Navy Station
vcnavy.description: Ventura County Navy Station: the California naval station more commonly known as Naval Base Ventura County (NBVC). NBVC, a naval installation encompassing Point Mugu, San Nicolas Island, and Port Hueneme, was created in 2000 when the commands of Point Mugu and Port Hueneme were consolidated.

co_ventura.name: Ventura County
co_ventura.description: Ventura County: a county in Southern California encompassing Los Padres National Forest, extensive farmland, and ten cities, including Ventura, Thousand Oaks, and Simi Valley (lands part of the Chumash homeland). The county was first settled by Europeans in 1782, when Mission San Buenaventura was constructed. Ventura County was officially formed in 1873, when it split from Santa Barbara County. Its county seat is Ventura and had a population of approximately 850,000 in 2016.

ci_ven.name: Ventura
ci_ven.description: Ventura: a city in Southern California located between Malibu and Santa Barbara and within Chumash territory. Mission San Buenaventura was founded here in 1782, and the city, which became a lemon-growing center, was incorporated in 1866. It became the county seat in 1873 when the Santa Barbara and Ventura counties split. The city of Ventura grew rapidly due to an oil strike in 1914.

village1.name: Village
village1.description: Village: a Native village (or in Spanish, rancheria) located on Aliso Street, which was the road between Los Angeles and Mission San Gabriel. In the early nineteenth century, it was populated by Gabrielinos (Tongva) who were employed as farm laborers in the nearby vineyards and orchards of white settlers.

st_va.name: Virginia
st_va.description: Virginia: the tenth state of the United States, which it officially joined on June 25, 1788. Virginia is the home of the Catawba, Cherokee, Croatoan, Powhatan, Tuscarora, Tutelo, Saponi, and Yuchi peoples. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, mining, tourism, lumber, and governmental and naval activities.

wranch.name: Warner's Ranch
wranch.description: Warner's Ranch: a 44,322-acre Mexican land grant in San Diego County, California and part of the Cupeño homeland. During the Spanish mission period, the valley served as a cattle grazing area for Mission San Diego and Mission San Luis Rey. The first land grant in the valley was made in 1836 to Silvestre de la Portilla and called Rancho Valle de San José. In 1840, José Antonio Pico was granted the part of the valley called Agua Caliente (for its hot mineral springs); he named it Rancho San José del Valle. Cupeño resistance to these landholdings ultimately led both ranchers to abandon the property; as a result, Juan Jose Warner was able to acquire the entire valley in 1844. His Warner's Ranch served as a way station for those traveling to California by the Gila Trail.  In 1857, the portion of Warner's Ranch that had been Rancho Agua Caliente land was re-awarded to an earlier claimant, and in 1861, Warner lost the remaining portion of Warner’s Ranch after falling into debt.

re_washingtonterritory.name: Washington Territory
re_washingtonterritory.description: Washington Territory: an area created by an act of the US Congress in 1853. The region was named for George Washington and encompassed the homeland of the Northwest Coast and Plateau people. It comprised the present-day state of Washington and parts of Montana and Idaho.

ci_dc.name: Washington, DC
ci_dc.description: Washington, DC: the capital of the United States of America, situated between the states of Maryland and Virginia. On July 16, 1790, Congress passed a law that provided for the selection of ten square miles of land along the Potomac River to become the nation's capital. Today, it is both a political and cultural center for the country, housing many museums, memorials, and national landmarks.

re_windies.name: West Indies
re_windies.description: West Indies: a group of islands in the Atlantic Ocean stretching over 2,000 miles and separating the Caribbean Sea from the Gulf of Mexico. The West Indies is comprised of several island groups, including the Greater Antilles, the Lesser Antilles, and the Bahamas. When Christopher Columbus became the first European to explore the area in 1492, the islands were primarily occupied by Carib and Taino people. Believing that he had reached Asia, Columbus named the islands the Indies. However, when the mistake was discovered, Spain renamed them the West Indies to distinguish this group from the Spice Islands or East Indies, now called Indonesia.

st_wv.name: West Virginia
st_wv.description: West Virginia: the thirty-fifth state of the United States, which it joined on June 20, 1863, after splitting with Virginia. The land comprising the state is home to the Cherokee, Shawnee, Tutelo, and Saponi peoples. Today, West Virginia's primary industries are coal mining, agriculture, chemical and glass manufacturing, and tourism.

whit.name: Whittier Public Library
whit.description: Whittier Public Library: a library system located within the city of Whittier, California, in Los Angeles County. The library was founded in 1900, and a building was financed in 1907 by a Carnegie grant. The library’s second and only branch opened in 1968.

ci_wi.name: Wichita
ci_wi.description: Wichita: a city in Sedgwick County, Kansas, located on the Arkansas River. Originally the site of a Wichita village, the town began as a trading post founded by J. R. Mead in 1864. Due to agricultural settlement, the town grew rapidly and was incorporated in 1871.

ci_worth.name: Worthington
ci_worth.description: Worthington: a city in southwest Minnesota. It was established in 1871 as a Union Pacific Railway line stop between Sioux City and St. Paul.

st_wy.name: Wyoming
st_wy.description: Wyoming: the forty-fourth state of the United States, which it officially joined on July 10, 1890. Wyoming is the home of the Arapaho, Cheyenne, Crow, Shoshone, and Ute peoples. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, mining, tourism, and oil and natural gas extraction.

ci_xab.name: Xabaagua
ci_xab.description: Xabaagua: a Chumash village listed in the notes of the Cabrillo exploration of the United States' western coastline (1542-43) and later listed in California mission documents.

ci_xucu.name: Xucu
ci_xucu.description: Xucu: a Chumash village listed in the notes of the Cabrillo exploration of the United States' western coastline (1542-43) and later listed in California mission documents. The contemporary county of Ventura currently includes the site of this former village.

ci_xugua.name: Xugua
ci_xugua.description: Xugua: a Chumash village on Santa Cruz Island, the largest of the California Channel Islands. Also spelled Xuga, the Chumash people lived on the island for thousands of years and developed a highly complex society dependent on the sea, specialized craft, and mainland trade. Although the Chumash did not have contact with Europeans until the sixteenth century, by 1822 all Chumash people had been relocated to the mainland.

yanga.name: Yanga
yanga.description: Yanga: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near San Fernando Mission (in or near the present-day city of Los Angeles, California). It was also known as Saway-yanga.

yanonali.name: Yanonali Street
yanonali.description: Yanonali Street: a street located in Santa Barbara and named after the Chumash chief Yanonali. Chief Yanonali was the leader of one of the largest Chumash villages at the time Spaniards built the Santa Barbara Presidio. Yanonali converted to Christianity at the Santa Barbara Mission.

re_ys.name: Yellowstone River
re_ys.description: Yellowstone River: a river that begins in Wyoming and flows approximately 692 miles through Yellowstone Lake and Montana. The river joins the Missouri River near Buford, North Dakota. Yellowstone River was first explored by non-Native people during the expedition of Lewis and Clark in 1806.

i_ybi.name: Yerba Buena Island
i_ybi.description: Yerba Buena Island: a small island located in the San Francisco Bay west of Oakland and east of the Golden Gate. It was formerly called Goat Island.

co_yolo.name: Yolo County
co_yolo.description: Yolo County: Yolo County was incorporated in 1850, making it one of the first counties in the state of California. The name 'Yolo' is of Patwin origin, but the exact meaning is disputed. It may be a deviation of the tribal name "Yoloy," the chief name "Yodo," or the community name "Yodoi."

re_yosemitevalley.name: Yosemite Valley
re_yosemitevalley.description: Yosemite Valley: a glacial valley in the western Sierra Nevada mountains that is home to the Ahwaheechee people. Members of Joseph Walker's expedition were likely the first European-Americans to see Yosemite, which attracted fortune-seekers when gold was discovered in the early 1850s. Significant violence between miners and Natives ensued. The Valley had already become a popular tourist destination by the late nineteenth century, though a federal bill protected the area as early as 1864 and in 1890 Yosemite became a national park.

co_yuba.name: Yuba County
co_yuba.description: Yuba County: a county located in Northern California named for the Yuba River. While it was incorporated in 1850, the original territory was later reapportioned for the creation of the Nevada and Sierra counties that border it today. The county seat, Marysville, sits along the Yuba River and was a mining town.

re_yubariv.name: Yuba River
re_yubariv.description: Yuba River: a watershed marked by its initial point in Sierra County, California, and confluence into the Feather River of Yuba County, California. The Yuba River was significant during the Gold Rush, with some of the richest claims found along its path. Captain John Sutter gave the river its name, but the origin is disputed. It may be a variation of 'Yubu,' a Maidu village in the area, or it may be a form of the Spanish word for grapes ("'uva"), as the fruit originally grew along the river's banks.

ci_zen.name: Zenith City
ci_zen.description: Zenith City: a nickname for Santa Barbara, California. A local nineteenth-century literary society was named the Zenith City Circle in honor of this moniker.

1835crew.name: Peor es Nada Crew
1835crew.description: Peor es Nada Crew: no definitive list of the men who sailed the Peor es Nada to San Nicolas Island in 1835, took a group of Nicoleños onboard, and then deposited them in San Pedro has been found. However, possible captains include Charles Hubbard and Isaac Sparks. The crew likely included native Hawaiians, who were employed on vessels sailing in California waters at this time (including those captained by George Nidever and Isaac Sparks). It is possible that the crew also included California Indians, who were part of otter hunting crews by the 1840s. C. J. W. Russell's Hutchings' California Illustrated Magazine 1856 document group claims that California Indians were onboard. Other crew members might have included, among others, Isaac Williams, who is listed as crew in the Goliah San Francisco Daily Placer Times and Transcript 1853 group and J. J. Warner's 1894 Los Angeles Herald article about the Lone Woman; James Johnson, who is listed as crew in J. J. Warner's 1894 Los Angeles Herald article; and Job F. Dye and Lewis T. Burton, who Nidever reported as Peor es Nada crew members during an otter hunting trip the previous year.

1814huntingparty.name: 1814 Hunting Party
1814huntingparty.description: 1814 Hunting Party: a group of Russian American Company (RAC) sea otter hunters was left on San Nicolas Island by the Il’mena in 1814, and engaged in a violent confrontation with the indigenous Nicoleños.The Russian Iakov Babin was in charge, as "overseer," and the RAC held him responsible for the unauthorized violence. The names of the Alaska Natives involved in the confrontation are unknown; they were conscripted workers for the RAC. Although some Russian documents refer to them as Aleuts, they were most likely Kodiak Aluttiq people, natives of Kodiak Island.

prospecting.name: "The party who recently went to San Nicolas Island</placeName> on a prospecting tour"

showmen.name:
"two speculative showmen proposed to the captain to lease the woman"

"two speculative showmen"

"several enterprising showmen"

threesailors.name: three sailors who were with Nidever (it does not specify which sailors)

bhagroup.name: Nicoleños who left San Nicolas Island for San Pedro in 1835
bhagroup.description: Nicoleños who left San Nicolas Island for San Pedro in 1835: there are no records of who was on board the Mexican schooner Peor es Nada in 1835. Early accounts by Captain William Dane Phelps and otter hunter George Nidever describe one Nicoleño, nicknamed Black Hawk, who lived in San Pedro. More recently, six people identified in Church baptismal records for 1835 or 1836 as either Nicoleño or Island Indians are likely or plausibly among the party that sailed on the Peor es Nada. There are a number of women among them including Juana (a young woman), María Magdalena (a young woman), María Aleja (a middle-aged woman), and the (unidentified) mother of an infant baptized as María Luciana after her 1836 birth in Los Angeles.

natam_aleuts.name: Aleuts
natam_aleuts.description: Aleuts: Native maritime people of the Aleutian Islands, in southwest Alaska. Much of their land came under the control of the Russian Empire in the mid-eighteenth century. The Aleuts, or Unangax, like the Natives of Kodiak Island, were pressed into service hunting maritime animals for the Russian America Company in the nineteenth century; it is likely, however, that hunters from Kodiak Island, not the Aleutian Island chain, were involved in the 1814 massacre of the Nicoleños. During the nineteenth century, many Aleuts were forced into labor at seasonal seal-hunting camps on the remote Pribilof Islands of St. George and St. Paul. Abusive conditions continued with commercial fishing on these islands during the American era. Today, the Aleutian Pribilof Islands Association is a federally recognized tribe with government-to-government relations with the United States.

americanfurcompany.name: American Fur Company
americanfurcompany.description: American Fur Company: a fur trading company founded in 1808 by John J. Astor. The American Fur Company dominated trade in the Great Lakes region and competed with the Hudson's Bay Company in the Pacific Northwest. Astor's trading post on the Columbia River was contracted to supply Russian Alaska with food prior to the War of 1812.

mu_americanmuseum.name: American Museum
mu_americanmuseum.description: American Museum: a museum opened by P. T. Barnum in Manhattan from 1841-65. Its exhibits attracted members of various classes and featured natural history specimens, paintings, wax figures, memorabilia, and events such as lectures. The museum was destroyed in a fire on July 13, 1865, leading to the creation of a circus that ran for nearly 150 years.

natam_aztec.name: Aztec
natam_aztec.description: Aztec: the Native people of northern Mesoamerica, who ruled a large, complex empire from the mid-fourteenth to the early sixteenth centuries. The empire was destroyed when the army of Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés captured its capital city of Tenochtitlán (present-day Mexico City) in 1521. At its height, the Aztec Empire had a population of eleven million people who were supported by an elaborate agriculture system. The civilization is renowned today for its magnificent art and architecture, including its religious temples, and for its codices and calendars.

natam_Barbareno.name: Barbareños
natam_Barbareno.description: Barbareños: Chumash people who are associated with Mission Santa Barbara and who, before the mission era, lived along the Pacific coast in the region between Point Conception and Rincon Point.

bp.name: Boardman and Pope
bp.description: Boardman and Pope: a Boston-based firm engaged in the Northwest and China trade during the early decades of the nineteenth century. The shipping enterprise has been falsely connected with the massacre of Nicoleños by Russian American Company hunters. Various periodical accounts stated that the captain of a Boardman & Pope ship, the Charon, left a hunting group on San Nicolas Island in 1811, and the hunters killed the Nicoleños. Russian documents show that the massacre did not involve the Charon or its captain; moreover, the battle took place in 1814.

brystu.name: Bryant & Sturgis
brystu.description: Bryant & Sturgis: a Boston firm that traded in California throughout the Mexican period. Bryant & Sturgis purchased hides, tallow, and local produce from the ranches and missions, selling or exchanging them for manufactured goods.

gov_indianbureau.name: Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA)
gov_indianbureau.description: Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA): an agency of the US federal government established in 1824 to administer the nation’s trade and treaty relations with Native tribes within US borders (prior to this date, various configurations were used to manage government-to-government relationships between the United States and Native nations). The BIA became the entity responsible for distributing aid to the newly dislocated Native peoples when removed from their homelands and relocated to remote reservations during the nineteenth century. During the era in which assimilation dominated Native policy, the BIA administered the privatization of communal lands through an allotment program and ran boarding schools designed to educate Native youth away from tribal identities. Today, the Bureau of Indian Affairs is housed in the Department of the Interior and has a mission to support tribes’ self-determination.

mi_mainlandmissions.name: California Missions
mi_mainlandmissions.description: California Missions: the 21 Catholic missions in Alta California, built between 1769-1823, initially under the leadership of the Spanish Franciscan Father Junípero Serra. The mission system was designed to both Christianize and control the Native population and to establish Spain's political and economic dominion over the region (which would eventually become the US state of California). Missions stretched along El Camino Real, a coastal road, from San Diego in the South to San Francisco in the North; each was placed about a day's drive from each other. Following Mexican independence, the missions were secularized, in 1833, and mission properties were sold to private interests.

cayachtclub.name: California Yacht Club
cayachtclub.description: California Yacht Club: a social organization for yacht owners in the Los Angeles area. The club was founded in 1922 and located at Wilmington Harbor; in 1961, it relocated to Marina del Rey.

scatalinayachtclub.name: Santa Catalina Yacht Club
scatalinayachtclub.description: Catalina Yacht Club: one of the oldest West Coast yacht clubs that has operated continually (except during World War II) since 1924. Catalina’s owners, the Banning Brothers, founded the yacht club in 1892 but it never flourished and was disbanded in 1914. The club reopened in 1924 when James Jump signed a lease with the Santa Catalina Island Company and re-founded the club, which promotes nautical hobbies such as sailing and fishing for sport.

natam_chumash.name: Chumash
natam_chumash.description: Chumash: Native people whose homeland spans the coast from Morro Bay to Malibu and includes the northern California Channel Islands. The Chumash are likely the first Natives to have interacted with the Lone Woman when she arrived in Santa Barbara in 1853. In fact, two Santa Barbara Chumash men, Malquiares and Policarpio, were members of the hunting party, led by George Nidever, that brought the Lone Woman to the mainland. Some bands of Chumash people gained federal recognition in the late twentieth century, and today there is one Chumash reservation: Santa Ynez Indian Reservation. Many Chumash are engaged in cultural revitalization, including the teaching and study of the Chumash language and communal building and paddling of the tomol associated with their maritime culture.

halfbreeds.name: Creoles (Russian America)
halfbreeds.description: Creoles (Russian America): people of mixed ethnic ancestry, for example, of a Russian father and Alaska Native mother or Creole father and Alaska Native mother.  As outlined in the Russian American Company charter of 1821, Creoles constituted a separate estate within the Russian Empire, and they played a prominent role in the commercial activities of the RAC.  Creoles typically spoke Russian and were educated within the colony and/or in the mother country alongside Russians.  Within the fur trade, their work mirrored that of Russians more closely than that of Natives.  By the mid nineteenth century, the number of Creoles within Alaskan towns such as Sitka nearly equaled the number of Russian settlers.

daughters.name: Daughters of the American Revolution
daughters.description: Daughters of the American Revolution: an organization founded in 1890 for female descendants of soldiers who fought in the American Revolution. Members, who must prove that they are a direct descendent of a Revolutionary War soldier, promote education and historical preservation. During much of the twentieth century, the DAR was infamous for its racism and, in 1939, then First Lady Eleanor Roosevelt renounced her membership after the national organization barred the renowned African-American singer Marian Anderson from performing in their constitutional Hall in Washington, DC. In the twenty-first century, however, the organization has worked to cultivate membership diversity.

natam_DiggerIndians.name: Digger Indians
natam_DiggerIndians.description: Digger Indians: a derogatory term initially used by American miners to describe the Native people of central California, who obtained a substantial portion of their caloric needs through indigenous vegetation, including acorns and roots.

natam_Fernandenos.name: Fernandeños
natam_Fernandenos.description: Fernandeños: Gabrieliño (Tongva) peoples who lived at Mission San Fernando, south of Santa Barbara, California.

natam_GabrilenoTongva.name: Gabrielinos
natam_GabrilenoTongva.description: Gabrielinos: Native peoples of the Los Angeles Basin and the southern Channel Islands. After the arrival of the Spanish and establishment of the mission system, they acquired the name Gabrielino due to their residence at Mission San Gabriel. Today, some Gabrielino call themselves Gabrielino while others use the terms Tongva or Kizh instead of or alongside the name Gabrielino. Twenty-first century Gabrielino (Tongva) people are striving to gain federal recognition as a tribe with the right to maintain government-to-government relations with the United States.

natam_inuit.name: Inuit
natam_inuit.description: Inuit: indigenous people of the North American Arctic, including parts of the United States (Alaska), Canada, and Greenland. The name derives from the Inukitut word for "people." The name Eskimo, which is associated with the colonial past, was previously used by outsiders to describe both the Inuit and Yupik peoples of Alaska, who speak languages that belong to the same linguistic group.  The Alutiiq (Sugpiaq) people, including those of Kodiak Island, have at times been misidentified as "Inuit;" they are Pacific Yupik.

marwil.name: Marshall & Wildes
marwil.description:
Josiah Marshall (1773-1841): a businessman based in Boston, Massachusetts and co-owner, with Dixey Wildes, of the Marshall & Wildes shipping business.

Dixey Wildes: partner with Josiah Marshall in the Boston-based Marshall & Wildes shipping firm, which was engaged in trade with China.

natam_hawaiian.name: Kanakas
natam_hawaiian.description: Kanakas: a derogatory term used by Europeans and Americans in the nineteenth century to designate Hawaiian Islanders.

natam_pomo.name: Kashaya Pomo
natam_pomo.description: Kashaya Pomo: California people whose homeland is north of San Francisco Bay in coastal Sonoma County. During the nineteenth century, some Kashaya Pomo were recruited by the Russian American Company to work as seasonal laborers at Fort Ross. The Kashaya Pomo chose to form an alliance with the Russians in part to protect themselves against Spanish missionary efforts and incursions by Mexican colonists. Members of the Kashaya Pomo community were recruited to play the people of Karana’s community, as well as the Aleuts, in the 1964 film adaptation of Island of the Blue Dolphins.  Today, the Kashia Band of Pomo Indians of the Stewarts Point Rancheria is a federally recognized tribe.

natam_kodiak.name: Kodiak Alutiiq (or Sugpiaq)
natam_kodiak.description: Kodiak Alutiiq (Sugpiaq): Native maritime people of Kodiak Island.  Their territory came under the control of the Russian Empire in 1784 when a permanent Russian settlement was established on Kodiak Island. The Russians often referred to the Kodiak Alutiit as "Aleuts," but they are a linguistically and culturally distinct people from those who live on the Aleutian Islands. During the early nineteenth century, the Kodiak Alutiit, who were expert marine mammal hunters, were forced to hunt fur-bearing animals for the government-sponsored Russian American Company. Sailing ships brought Russian overseers and Kodiak islanders to hunt otters on San Nicolas and other California Channel Islands. When Alaska was purchased by the United States, the Kodiak Alutiit confronted new pressures of assimilation. The 1971 Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act brought significant benefits to Alutiiq villages; today, cultural revitalization efforts are flourishing on Kodiak Island.

mi_pur.name: La Purisima Mission
mi_pur.description: La Purisima Mission: a Spanish mission established in the Chumash village of Algsacupi (present-day Lompoc) founded in 1787. Also called La Purísima Concepción, in 1804, at the mission’s peak, 1,520 Chumash neophytes lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1874. It is now a California State Historic Park and serves as a living history museum.

mi_la.name: Los Angeles Mission
mi_la.description: Los Angeles Mission: a Catholic mission located in present-day Los Angeles, California and founded in 1797, although the mission's first church was not completed until 1799. Also known as San Fernando Mission or San Fernando Rey de España, by 1811 the mission had 1,081 residents, most of whom were Tataviam or Gabrielino (Tongva). The Nicoleños who arrived in San Pedro in 1835 were not brought to this mission due to its secularization in 1834; instead, some of the group were baptized at the Los Angeles Plaza Church. The mission was returned to the Catholic Church in 1861.

natam_Luisenos.name: Luiseños
natam_Luisenos.description: Luiseños: Native people of southern California whose homeland extends from southern Los Angeles County to northern San Diego County. They acquired the name Luiseños from their association with Mission San Luis Rey in present-day Oceanside, California. As of 2016, six bands of Luiseño people had been federally recognized as tribes with government-to-government relations with the United States, including the Pechanga Band, which in 2015 submitted a repatriation claim to the US Navy citing cultural affiliation with the Nicoleños and requesting a return of all funerary and cultural objects held by the Navy on San Nicolas Island. The claim was accepted. Linguists have determined that the Luiseño language came from the Cupan group of the Takic branch of the Uto-Aztecan language, and some believe the four recorded words spoken by the Lone Woman resemble the Luiseño language more closely than any other.

misfats.name: Mission Fathers
misfats.description: Mission Fathers: a term applied collectively, in California, to the Franciscan priests who oversaw the twenty-one Catholic missions established between 1769 and 1833. The missions sought to "gather in" the Native peoples of California, converting them to Christianity and European lifeways. No evidence documenting a priest's request to search for the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island has been found to this date.

natam_missionindians.name: Mission Indians
natam_missionindians.description: Mission Indians: unidentified California Natives who were associated with one or more of the twenty-one Catholic missions in California. In many European-American documents, they are referred to as Christian neophytes, with their tribal identities omitted. In only some instances can historical research uncover tribal or individual identities of those referenced.

mu_parismuseum.name: Musée de l’Homm
mu_parismuseum.description: Musée de l’Homm: a museum in Paris, France, established in 1938. The museum’s precursor was the Musée d’Ethnographie du Trocadéro, established in 1882 and in operation until it was joined with the Musée de Sculpture Comparée to form the Musée de l’Homme. French geologist and ethnographer Léon de Cessac collected artifacts from San Nicolas Island for this museum in the 1870s; they are still held in the museum’s collections today.
