ypsce.name: Young People's Society of Christian Endeavor

mu_natmuseumla.name: Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County
mu_natmuseumla.description: Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County: a museum featuring exhibits about both natural history and culture. Opened in 1913 as the Los Angeles County Museum of History, Science, and Art, the museum was part of a strategy to turn local fairgrounds into a cultural center. The state of California built an exposition building, the county built the museum, and the city agreed to maintain the grounds. The museum was renovated in 2007-09 to restore the original designs while also modernizing the structure.

natam_NicolenoTongva.name: Nicoleños
natam_NicolenoTongva.description: Nicoleños: the people of San Nicolas Island. Although they are Island Gabrielinos (Tongva), they became known as Nicoleños because of the Spanish name given to the island. In the early nineteenth century, the Nicoleño population dropped following contact and conflicts with hunters from Europe, North America, Alaska, and Hawaii. All the Nicoleños, except the Lone Woman, were taken from the island in 1835 on the ship Peor es Nada and brought to San Pedro, near Los Angeles, California.

natam_pepimaros.name: Pepimaros
natam_pepimaros.description: Pepimaros: a term used to describe Native peoples on the mainland who had migrated to California from the Channel Islands.

paac.name: Pierce-Arrow Motor Car Company
paac.description: Pierce-Arrow Motor Car Company: a manufacturer of luxury cars that operated from 1870-1938. Founder George N. Pierce began the business as a bicycle manufacturer, but, after producing his first single cylinder automobile in 1901, renamed the company and began producing cars full time. In 1928, the company was sold to an Indiana automobile company named Studebaker, but the Great Depression strongly affected business. The company filed for bankruptcy in 1938.

mu_lowie.name: University of California Robert H. Lowie Museum of Anthropology
mu_lowie.description: Robert H. Lowie Museum of Anthropology: the University of California’s museum of anthropology founded in 1901 and named after Berkeley professor and anthropologist Dr. Robert H. Lowie. In 1991, it was renamed the Phoebe A. Hurst Museum of Anthropology after its philanthropist founder. Ishi and Alfred Kroeber, the man who studied him, are perhaps the most famous figures associated with the museum. A Yahi man who was deemed "the last of his tribe," Ishi lived at the museum more or less as an exhibit from 1911 until his death from tuberculosis in 1916.

rogers.name: Rogers Brothers
rogers.description: Rogers Brothers: refers to Eugene Frederick, Alvah, and Herbert Augustine Rogers, the sons of Augustine B. and Hannah E. Perkins Rogers. The eldest son, Eugene, founded a seal and sea otter hunting and abalone fishing operation out of Santa Barbara in 1875. In addition to employing his younger brothers, he hired Chinese abalone fishermen and two of George Nidever's sons, George and Jake.

rfurcompany.name: Russian American Company
rfurcompany.description: Russian American Company: a joint-stock company founded in 1799 and given a monopoly on fur hunting and trade in Russian Alaska. For much of its history, the RAC was headquartered in Sitka (then New Archangel), Alaska. It served as the administrative center of the Russian Empire in North America.

mi_venturamission.name: San Buenaventura Mission
mi_venturamission.description: San Buenaventura Mission: a Catholic mission founded in 1782 and located near the Chumash village of Mitsquanaqa'n in the city of Ventura, California. At the mission's peak, in 1816, 1,328 neophytes, mostly Chumash people, lived here. In 1818, the mission was evacuated for a month due to the threat of a pirate attack. The mission was secularized in 1836 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1862.

mi_sfern.name: Mission San Fernando
mi_sfern.description: San Fernando Mission: a Catholic mission founded in 1797 and located in present-day Los Angeles, California, although the mission’s first church was not completed until 1799. Also called San Fernando Rey de de España and Los Angeles Mission, by 1811 the mission had 1,081 residents, most of whom were Tataviam or Gabrielino (Tongva). The Nicoleños who arrived in San Pedro in 1835 were not brought to this mission due to its secularization in 1834; instead, some of the group were baptized at the Los Angeles Plaza Church. The mission was returned to the Catholic Church in 1861.

in_mercantile.name: San Francisco Mercantile Library
in_mercantile.description: San Francisco Mercantile Library: a library founded by the Mercantile Library Association of San Francisco. By 1874, the institution had around 36,000 volumes. The library merged with the collection of the San Francisco Mechanics’ Institute, but the combined library was destroyed in the 1906 earthquake. A new Mechanics’-Mercantile Library opened in 1910.

mi_sg.name: Mission San Gabriel
mi_sg.description: San Gabriel Mission: a Catholic mission located nine miles east of downtown Los Angeles, California, and was founded in 1771. Also called San Gabriel Arcángel, at the mission’s peak, in 1817, 1,701 Gabrielino (Tongva) neophytes lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Church in 1859 via a proclamation signed by President James Buchanan. The Nicoleños who arrived in San Pedro, California, in 1835 were not brought to San Gabriel Mission, which was then secularized after Mexican independence. Those men and women for whom records have been found remained in the Los Angeles area.

mi_slo.name: Mission San Luis Obispo
mi_slo.description: San Luis Obispo: the fifth Catholic mission in California, located in a coastal valley the Spanish called the "Valley of the Bears." Founded in 1772, this was the first mission established on the land of the Chumash, and it had a record population of 832 people in 1804. The mission was secularized in 1835 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1859.

mi_santabarbaramission.name: Santa Barbara Mission
mi_santabarbaramission.description: Santa Barbara Mission: a Catholic mission located in Santa Barbara, California, that was founded in 1786 near the Chumash village of Xana'yan. It was the tenth mission founded by the Franciscans in California and is known as the "Queen of the Missions" because of its beauty. In 1803, at the mission's peak, 1,792 neophytes, Chumash peoples referred to as the Barbareño and the Canaleño, lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1865. In 1853, the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island was conditionally baptized and buried at the mission. She was not reunited with her people at the mission, as they had been brought to Los Angeles. Today the Santa Barbara Mission is owned and operated by the Franciscan order, serving as both a church and a museum.

scruzislandcompany.name: Santa Cruz Island Company
scruzislandcompany.description: Santa Cruz Island Company: the owners of Santa Cruz Island between 1869-87. Ten investors formed the company after purchasing the island from William Barron in the late nineteenth century; the company then operated a cattle and sheep ranch on the island. In the 1880s, one shareholder, Justinian Caire, bought out his partners. The land was divided among his family members, but most was then sold to Edwin Stanton by 1937. Stanton's son, Carey Stanton, left most of the island to the Santa Cruz Island Foundation, which operates the Nature Conservancy, in 1987.

serrashop.name: Serra Shop Old Mission
serrashop.description: Serra Shop: the gift store at Santa Barbara Mission.

tuna.name: Tuna Club
tuna.description: The Tuna Club: a private big-game fishing organization on Catalina Island, California. Charles Frederick Holder founded the club in 1898, wanting to promote stricter rules for anglers as well as marine life conservation. The club, still in operation today, has counted many celebrities and US presidents among its members. Its 1908 clubhouse is now a US National Historic Landmark.

natam_toltecs.name: Toltecs
natam_toltecs.description: Toltecs: a civilization that prospered in central Mexico from the tenth to twelfth centuries. The Toltecs crafted monumental pyramids and statues that reflected the overall militaristic nature of their empire. The Aztecs—who rose to power several centuries later—claimed descent from the Toltecs, whom they greatly admired. The Toltecs capital, Tollan, was located about fifty miles north of present-day Mexico City.

mu_vaticanmuseum.name: Vatican
mu_vaticanmuseum.description: Vatican: a sovereign city-state that was part of the city of Rome during the mid-nineteenth century. The Vatican is home to the Vatican Museum, a collection of art and artifacts collected by Catholic popes beginning in the sixteenth century and organized into separate galleries and museums in the eighteenth century. The Museum of Propaganda, one of those collections, was established to highlight the missionary achievements of evangelizers in the New World. It is currently unclear whether objects associated with the Lone Woman ever reached Rome and the Vatican Museum.

natam_Ventureno.name: Ventureños
natam_Ventureno.description: Ventureños: Chumash peoples associated with Mission San Buenaventura; prior to the mission era, they lived along the Pacific coast in the region stretching from Carpenteria to Malibu, California.

sh_active.name: Active
sh_active.description: Active: a steamer ship used by the US Coast Survey between 1852-61. It was one of the first two US Survey ships on the west coast.

sh_albatross.name: Albatross
sh_albatross.description: Albatross: an American ship, captained by New Englander Nathan Winship, that partnered with the Russian American Company. While Americans provided the ship, the RAC provided skilled Alaska Native hunters. The Albatross's route ran along the Californian coast, between Russian-owned Alaska and California, and it sailed in the Pacific in the same years as the Il’mena.

sh_alert.name: Alert
sh_alert.description: Alert: a three-masted, 398-ton ship owned by Bryant, Sturgis & Company of Boston. During the nineteenth century, the Alert traded on the California coast. In May 1841, Captain William Dane Phelps wrote that he had contact with a Nicoleño known as Black Hawk at San Pedro. Phelps also noted that another sea captain, Thomas Robbins, said that he had previously taken two Nicoleñas to San Pedro.

sh_arcticstar.name: Arctic Star
sh_arcticstar.description: Arctic Star: a ship sailed along the Californian coast by Scott O’Dell with his wife and two other men. O’Dell’s book The Cruise of the Arctic Star, published in 1973, was inspired by this voyage. The novel recounts his trip along the coast while discussing California’s history.

sh_avon.name: Avon
sh_avon.description: Avon: a ship involved in the sea otter trade along the California coast, beginning in 1817. It was renamed Trinidad in 1842.

sh_bolivar.name: Bolivar Liberator
sh_bolivar.description: Bolivar Liberator: a 224-ton brig built by Alpheus Basil Thompson, an American who became a naturalized Mexican citizen; co-owned by Thompson, Farnham and Co., and John Coffin Jones; and captained by Gorham Hayden Nye in the early 1830s. The Bolivar Liberator engaged in coastal trade. In 1832, it carried horses to the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii). It became involved in the otter trade beginning in 1834, with the ship’s crew (including George Nidever) hunting otters on San Nicolas and San Clemente Islands in 1843-44, a time overlapping with the Lone Woman's isolation. The brig was renamed Oajaca in 1843 and began sailing under the Mexican flag.

sh_charon.name: Charon
sh_charon.description: Charon: a ship owned by the P.T. Jackson, of Boston. During the War of 1812, the Charon was captured by the British ship Cherub at the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii) in 1814. Some newspaper accounts incorrectly reported that it was the Charon, captained by Whittemore, that left the hunting party responsible for the massacre of the massacre of the Nicoleños on San Nicolas Island in 1811. In reality, it was the Il'mena, a Russian American Company ship, in the year 1814.

sh_convoy.name: Convoy
sh_convoy.description: Convoy: a 135-ton brig that was owned by Josiah Marshall of Boston in 1824 and then by John Coffin Jones in the early 1830s. It engaged in the Northwest and Pacific trade for many years. Richard Henry Dana describes an 1836 visit with its crew on the California Coast in his Two Years Before the Mast. At the time, many members of the otter-hunting crew were Tlingit.

sh_nidever.name: Cora
sh_nidever.description: Cora: the 16 1/95 ton burthen schooner George Nidever brought to San Nicolas Island with hunting groups in 1852 and 1853. During the summer 1853 trip, the Lone Woman made herself visible to one member of Nidever’s hunting party, Carl Dittman, and when the crew sailed for Santa Barbara at the end of the month’s hunt, the Lone Woman joined them. Nidever had purchased the schooner together with two other men, James Alden and Thomas H. Stevens (lieutenants in the U.S. Coast Survey), in San Francisco in 1852. They used the vessel for a variety of economic pursuits.

sh_euphemia.name: Euphemia
sh_euphemia.description: Euphemia: a 133-ton brig built in Dundee, Scotland that sailed between 1834-50 under British, Hawaiian, and American flags. The Euphemia made voyages to Valparaiso (Chile), Callao (Peru), Hawaii, and Hong Kong, and it spent much of its time engaged in coastal California trade. The brig is connected to the Lone Woman’s story in two ways. In 1843, Carl Dittman joined the Euphemia’s crew in Liverpool for a commercial voyage chartered by merchants James Scott and John Wilson; although the ship was declared unseaworthy once it reached Hawaii, in 1844, Dittman joined the ship’s cargo on board the Juanita, which continued on to Alta California. Dittman then settled in California, and in 1853, he would be part of the otter hunting party that brought the Lone Woman to Santa Barbara. Meanwhile, the unseaworthy Euphemia was sold to William Heath Davis, Eliab Grimes, and Hiram Grimes. In 1846, under this ownership, the Euphemia left Hawaii and anchored near San Nicolas Island to off-load most of its cargo in a strategy designed to avoid tariffs upon docking in California. Crew member John Young was left behind on San Nicolas Island to guard the cargo; as a result, he spent two months on San Nicolas Island during the years of the Lone Woman’s isolation there. Finally, in 1849, the Euphemia was sold by Heath to the town council of San Francisco, to be used as a prison. It served this purpose and simultaneously housed the mentally ill, effectively becoming the first asylum in the region.

sh_goliah.name: Goliah
sh_goliah.description: Goliah: a steam towboat built in New York and in service between 1849-89. Also known as the Defender, its home port was San Francisco.

sh_griffon.name: Griffon
sh_griffon.description: Griffon: a brig built in 1824 in Medford, Massachusetts, and initially purchased by Bryant and Sturgis, a Boston shipping business that traded in lumber, tea, opium, and sugar, among other goods. The Griffon operated on the Northwest Coast for five years. In 1837, it was under the ownership of John Coffin Jones.

sh_hassler.name: Hassler
sh_hassler.description: Hassler: a US Coast Survey ship designed to chart the US Pacific Coast. It mapped the coast of California and its islands while in service between 1871-95.

sh_hel.name: Helvetius
sh_hel.description: Helvetius: a trading ship built in 1804 by Frenchman Stephen Girard and then converted into a whaler. The Helvetius was known for the hand-carved bust of its French philosopher namesake, which was positioned at the helm of the ship. It made its first whaling voyage to the Pacific and Virgin Islands in 1832. On its return, the Helvetius ran aground on O’ahu island just after midnight on November 10, 1834. The ship was salvaged, but there is no record of the philosopher figurehead being recovered.

sh_ilmena.name: Il'mena
sh_ilmena.description: Il'mena: a two-masted, 200-ton copper-sheathed brig part of the Russian American Company’s sailing ships that visited the California coast on company business. Originally named the Lydia, the ship had been owned by a US sea captain and sold to the Russians in 1813. The following year, in its first voyage under the Russian flag, the Il’mena brought a sea otter hunting crew to San Nicolas Island.

sh_jennie.name: Jennie Griffin
sh_jennie.description: Jennie Griffin: a schooner used for sea otter hunting along the California coast for several decades.

sh_begg.name: John Begg
sh_begg.description: John Begg: an English trading ship chartered under John Begg & Company, a firm based in Liverpool, England. William Harnell and Hugh McCulloch served as the California agents between 1822-28. After arriving in San Diego in 1822 carrying a shipment of goods from Peru, the brig struck a rock protrusion just off the coast of San Nicolas Island and was grounded in 1824. The rock was subsequently named Begg Rock.

sh_lavictoria.name: La Victoria
sh_lavictoria.description: La Victoria: a ship in Spanish explorer Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo’s fleet. Cabrillo was the first to explore the California coast between 1542-43.

sh_loriot.name: Loriot
sh_loriot.description: Loriot: an American brig that traded between Spanish California and the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii) in the 1830s. In 1833, it was seized in the San Francisco port and one of its owners, Alpheus B. Thompson, was arrested for illegally hunting otter in Spanish waters along the coast of California (other part owners included Gorham Hayden Nye and John Coffin Jones). By the late 1830s, however, the brig was again trading between the Northwest Coast, California, and Hawaii; John C. Jones was the American Consul in Hawaii at the time.

sh_louisa.name: Louisa
sh_louisa.description: Louisa: an American merchant ship (bark) owned by John Coffin Jones and involved in the Northwest fur trade along the coast of California in the 1830s.

sh_mayflower.name: Mayflower
sh_mayflower.description: Mayflower: a cargo ship that set sail in 1620 and carried 102 passengers from Plymouth, England, across the Atlantic to present-day Massachusetts.

sh_ocain.name: O'Cain
sh_ocain.description: O’Cain: the first contracted "Yankee-Russian" hunting ship. Partnering with the Russian American Company, American Joseph O’Cain provided the ship while the RAC provided skilled Alaska Native hunters. The partnership enabled O’Cain to acquire furs directly, instead of trading for them along the Spanish California coast, and allowed the RAC to hunt in waters further south. The first contract was signed in 1803, and the ship continued to sail under this system until 1816.

sh_oajaca.name: Oajaca
sh_oajaca.description: Oajaca: a brig involved in California coastal trade beginning in 1834. Previously named the Bolivar Liberator, it was owned by John Coffin Jones and Alpheus Basil Thompson and used in the sea otter trade. Outgoing cargo on an 1884 sail included beef, potatoes, goat tallow, "joints," and alcohol.

sh_orus.name: Orus
sh_orus.description: Orus: a passenger ship built in 1842. It initially operated on the Shrewsbury River in New Jersey, and was sold to the New York merchant firm Howland and Aspinwall to be used as a tug boat for ships anchored in the river. In 1849 the Orus was purchased by the Nicaragua Steamship Company and transferred to the San Juan River in Nicaragua.

sh_panama.name: Panama steamer
sh_panama.description: Panama: a steamship launched on July 29, 1848 by the Pacific Mail Steamship Company. It sailed between New York and San Francisco through the Panama Route beginning in 1849, until it was transferred to the California coast in 1858 and sold to Holladay and Flint in 1861. The Panama was given to the Mexican government in 1868 as part of a mail contract and renamed the Juarez.

sh_peoresnada.name: Peor es Nada
sh_peoresnada.description: Peor es Nada: a 20.5-ton Mexican schooner owned by Jose Joaquín Gómez of Monterey and launched in 1834. Isaac Sparks chartered the ship to hunt sea otter along the California coast in 1834 and 1835. During this time, Charles Hubbard captained the ship. In 1835, the Peor es Nada sailed to San Nicolas Island and returned to San Pedro with the remaining Nicoleños, save one woman who was left behind (she would arrive in Santa Barbara in 1853, on George Nidever's schooner). The Peor es Nada capsized at the entrance to San Francisco Bay in early 1836.

sh_pilgrim.name: Pilgrim
sh_pilgrim.description: Pilgrim: an American brig built in 1825, owned by the Boston firm Bryant & Sturgis, and engaged in the California trade. The Pilgrim was immortalized by Richard Henry Dana Jr., who recorded his experiences on the vessel in Two Years Before the Mast. The Pilgrim caught fire at sea in 1841, but a replica of the ship was built in 1945 in Dana Point, California, and still makes voyages up and down the California coast as a floating museum.

sh_quickstep.name: Quickstep
sh_quickstep.description: Quickstep: a 338-ton steamer that surveyed southern California (including mapping the Channel Islands) in 1851, as part of the U.S. Coast Survey. George Nidever served as the ship's pilot. The steamer was built in Philadelphia in 1850.

sh_rasselas.name: Rasselas
sh_rasselas.description: Rasselas: a trading ship involved in the Northwest and China trade in the 1830s. It was owned by Eliab Grims & Company.

sh_salvador.name: San Salvador
sh_salvador.description: San Salvador: the lead ship in Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo’s fleet. Cabrillo was the first European to explore the California coast, between 1542-43.

sh_srsteamer.name: Santa Rosa
sh_srsteamer.description: Santa Rosa: a passenger ship built in Pennsylvania for the Oregon Steamship Company in 1883. The Santa Rosa was sold in 1897 to the Pacific Coast Steamship Company and ran a regular route between San Francisco and San Diego with various stopping points, including Los Angeles, Redondo, Santa Barbara, Port Hartford, and Santa Cruz Island. It wrecked on July 7, 1911, near Point Arguello while carrying 200 passengers; four died as a result of the crash.

sh_california.name: California
sh_california.description: SS California: the first Pacific Mail steamer to arrive on the West Coast. First launched on May 19, 1848, the steamer’s route passed directly by San Nicolas Island. After 1877, the SS California was converted into a sailing ship and transported cargo. The steamer wrecked off the coast of Peru in 1895.

sh_fremont.name: Fremont
sh_fremont.description: SS Colonel Fremont: a ship that traveled between Panama and San Francisco, carrying both passengers and freight. During the 1850s, it was owned by the Pacific Mail Steamship Company.

sh_falcon.name: Falcon
sh_falcon.description: SS Falcon: a ship owned by the US Mail Steamship Company and in operation between 1848-52. It was then converted into a towboat. The SS Falcon was one of the first ships to run a route to California through the Panama Canal.

sh_staroffreedom.name: Star of Freedom
sh_staroffreedom.description: Star of Freedom: a wooden sloop built in 1865 in San Francisco and used by the Santa Cruz Island Company (a cattle ranching enterprise). The vessel was sold after more than 25 years of service on the California Channel Islands. The Pacific pirate Oscar Bartels subsequently stole Star of Freedom and sailed to Mexico. Its fate is unknown.

sh_undine.name: Undine
sh_undine.description: Undine: the first ship licensed in the United States, on November 5, 1890. It sank among the California Channel Islands exactly one month later.

sh_congress.name: Congress
sh_congress.description: USS Congress: a ship commissioned in 1841 and assigned to the Mediterranean squadron. During the Mexican-American War, it joined the Pacific Squadron at Monterey Bay and was deployed along the west coast of Mexico; it participated in the battle of Los Angeles. The CSS Virginia ultimately destroyed the USS Congress during the Civil War, when it was assigned to the Atlantic Blockade.

sh_edith.name: Edith
sh_edith.description: USS Edith: an American steamship originally used in the opium trade between British India and China. Sold to the American War Department in 1849, it was used during the Mexican-American War to move Army supplies along the Californian coast. On August 24, 1849, having encountered a heavy fog, it ran aground and wrecked in the Santa Barbara Channel, near San Nicolas island.

sh_por.name: Portsmouth
sh_por.description: USS Portsmouth (II): an American sloop-of-war launched in 1843. In service during the Mexican-American War and the American Civil War, it not only engaged in general surveillance and disruption of the slave trade but undertook several transoceanic voyages. The USS Portsmouth was decommissioned from the Navy and sold in 1915.

sh_vandalia.name: Vandalia
sh_vandalia.description: USS Vandalia: a Navy ship first launched in 1828. It was stationed off the coast of South America to protect merchant ships until it was decommissioned in 1831. The ship was decommissioned and recommissioned several times, assigned to the Pacific Squadron and various other duties along the coast until the outbreak of the Civil War brought it back to the South Atlantic Blockading Squadron in 1861. The USS Vandalia was decommissioned for the last time in 1863.

sh_volunteer.name: Volunteer
sh_volunteer.description: Volunteer: an American merchant ship (bark) involved in the Pacific trade in the 1830s. It was owned by John Coffin Jones and utilized for the transport of both hide & tallow and otter pelts in California.

sh_washington.name: Washington
sh_washington.description: Washington: a schooner owned by the Boston merchant firm Marshall & Wildes. It was involved in the Pacific trade during the 1820s.

sh_waverly.name: Waverly
sh_waverly.description: Waverly: a brig involved in the Northwest trade between Hawaii and California. William Goodwin Dana was associated with the vessel.

sh_generalscott.name: Winfield Scott
sh_generalscott.description: Winfield Scott: an American steamship built in 1850 by the Pacific Mail Steamship Company. Running the Panama route beginning in 1852, the steamship carried passengers and mail from New York to San Francisco. However, when traveling from San Francisco to Panama in 1853, the ship ran aground on Anacapa Island. Scavengers and treasure hunters, hoping to recover undiscovered gold from its last shipment, visited the wreckage so frequently that it was eventually listed on the National Register of Historic Places in order to protect the remaining ruins.

sh_baikal.name: Baikal
sh_baikal.description: Baikal: formerly the American brig Arab, this 215-ton vessel was purchached by the Russian American Company and was operating on the coast as late as 1839. It transported supplies, including salt, to the Russian colonies and brought Russian goods to Chile in 1829.

sh_bigloafer.name: Big Loafer
sh_bigloafer.description: Big Loafer: a sloop used for fishing in California’s coastal waters, including in and around the Channel Islands, between the 1890s-1904. It was used at least occasionally to transport Chinese abalone fishermen to the islands.

sh_crusader.name: Crusader
sh_crusader.description: Crusader: a 113-ton brig that sailed between Hawaii, California, and Alaska in 1832, chartered by John Coffin Jones. The Crusader left Honolulu in March, took aboard Kaigani Haida hunters at Norfolk Sound, and hunted otter off the California coast and around Cerros Island during the summer, before returninig to Honoulu in September.

sh_famer.name: Fama
sh_famer.description: Fama: owned by Alpheus B. Thompson and John Coffin Jones, the Fama engaged in trade between California and Hawaii. Gorham Hayden Nye frequently served as its captain. Outgoing cargo for an 1844 journey included hides, soap, horses, goatskins, deerskins, lumber, and shingles. The ship wrecked on February 26, 1846, near Santa Barbara.

sh_genova.name: Genoa
sh_genova.description: Genoa: a schooner (sometimes called the Genova or Geneva) used for fishing and pleasure trips along the coast.

sh_harrietblanchard.name: Harriet Blanchard
sh_harrietblanchard.description: Harriet Blanchard: a 62-ton schooner owned by John Coffin Jones and sailing under a US flag. It engaged in Pacific trade, traveling between California, Hawaii, and some Pacific islands. Cargo included hide & tallow, otter furs, and horses.

sh_hattie.name: Hattie
sh_hattie.description: Hattie: a sloop built in San Francisco in 1889 with home port in Wilmington, California. It measured 38.5 (length) x 12.6 (breadth) x 8.5 (depth) with a gross register tonnage of 12.96 and net register tonnage of 12.42.

sh_ida.name: Ida
sh_ida.description: Ida: a sloop built in San Francisco in 1872. It had a gross register tonnage of 15.50 and net register tonnage of 14.74.

sh_isabella.name: Isabella
sh_isabella.description: Isabella: a sloop, with home ports variously in Santa Barbara, San Diego, and San Francisco. It wrecked at San Miguel Island in 1886. The Isabella's gross register tonnage was 12.80 and net register tonnage 12.16.

sh_juan.name: Juanita
sh_juan.description: Juanita: a hermaphrodite brig owned by California shipmasters John Wilson and James Scott beginning in 1844; it engaged in coastal trade and employed a multiethnic crew that included native Hawaiians.

sh_ship2.name: Juanita
sh_ship2.description: Juanita: a hermaphrodite brig owned by California shipmasters John Wilson and James Scott beginning in 1844; it engaged in coastal trade and employed a multiethnic crew that included native Hawaiians.

sh_losangeles.name: Los Angeles
sh_losangeles.description: Los Angeles: a steamer built in San Francisco in 1874. It brought passengers to Santa Cruz Island throughout the early 1890s.

sh_mat.name: Matinee
sh_mat.description: Matinee: 37-ton schooner with home port of San Francisco.

sh_fictionalpearl.name: Pearl
sh_fictionalpearl.description: Pearl: schooner built in Benica, California with home port in San Francisco. It wrecked on Anacapa Island in 1891.

sh_phoebe.name: Phœbe
sh_phoebe.description: Phoebe: a 36-gun British Royal Navy ship engaged in the War of 1812 on the United States’ west coast. Together with the Cherub, the Phoebe captured the American ships Essex and Essex-Junior at Valparaiso, Chile in 1814. The Cherub also intercepted the American merchant ship Charon, with Isaac Whittemore as captain, near Hawaii in 1814.

sh_joinville.name: Prince de Joinville
sh_joinville.description: Prince de Joinville: a brig that was among the earliest vessels used in island ranching. Alpheus Thompson purchased the ship to to transport stock from Santa Rosa Island.

sh_rover.name: Rover
sh_rover.description: Rover: a schooner owned by Luis Antonio Arguello and engaged in otter hunting on the California coast in the early nineteenth century.

sh_santabarbara.name: Santa Barbara
sh_santabarbara.description: Santa Barbara: a schooner built in San Francisco in 1891 and owned by John Coffin Jones; it sailed between the Channel Islands and mainland, transporting abalone, shells, and other items.

sh_senator.name: Senator
sh_senator.description: Senator: a 1012-ton steamer built in New York City in 1848. It was transported sheep between Santa Cruz Island and the California mainland in the 1870s.
